findings.recommendation: Implement authenticated static serving of index.m3u8 and seg_*.ts from work_dir/{id} (or front it with the nginx static layer as the comment intends and return that URL). Until then there is no streaming product.

findings.title: Orphan service — nothing in server, SPA, or recommender ever calls the transcoder
findings.description: The transcoder is built and wired into compose on the internal network, but a repo-wide search for any caller (port 8090, /sessions, CreateSessionRequest, the word transcoder) outside crates/transcoder and the roadmap doc returns nothing. server/services/ffmpeg.ts is a separate Node-side spawn unrelated to this crate. So the entire subsystem is dead weight: it cannot be invoked through any product flow, and its API contract (SourceProbe/ClientCapabilities) has no producer. This compounds the security finding — when it IS wired, the integration must add the auth + path-validation that currently does not exist.
findings.evidence: grep -rn over server/ src/ recommender/ for 8090|/sessions|transcoder|CreateSessionRequest returns no matches; docker-compose service exists at lines 44-59 with 'reached via internal network only'.
findings.recommendation: Either wire the media/player flow to call it (with auth + media-root containment) or stop shipping it in compose until M4 is real. Define who computes SourceProbe (likely media-core's probe.rs) and pass it through a trusted path.

findings.title: Blocking std::fs calls inside async handlers while holding the sessions mutex
findings.description: start() calls std::fs::create_dir_all (line 67), stop() calls std::fs::remove_dir_all (line 121), and the reaper calls std::fs::remove_dir_all (line 142) — all synchronous blocking syscalls on tokio worker threads. The reaper holds the sessions Mutex (locked at line 131) across the entire stale-removal loop including each blocking remove_dir_all, so during cleanup of a large work dir (hundreds of unbounded .ts segments — see the disk finding) all create/heartbeat/get/stop operations are blocked, and clients can spuriously miss the 30s heartbeat window. At single-NAS scale this is tolerable but degrades badly under concurrent load.
findings.evidence: session.rs:67 `std::fs::create_dir_all(&work_subdir)?;`, 121 `let _ = std::fs::remove_dir_all(&rec.work_subdir);`, 142 same inside the reaper while `sessions` is locked from line 131.
findings.recommendation: Use tokio::fs (async) or tokio::task::spawn_blocking, and in the reaper collect the records to remove, drop the lock, then do the FS cleanup outside the critical section.

findings.title: No Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry despite the project's mandatory-telemetry contract (§15)
findings.description: Project memory/contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15). The transcoder has no sentry/glitchtip dependency and no error reporting beyond tracing to stdout. Combined with no stderr drain and no child.wait(), an ffmpeg failure in production produces zero captured diagnostics — you cannot even see why a transcode died. main.rs uses .expect() on bind/serve so a startup failure is a bare panic with no crash report.
findings.evidence: Cargo.toml dependencies: tokio, axum, serde, serde_json, tracing, tracing-subscriber, uuid, thiserror — no sentry/glitchtip. main.rs:60 `.expect("failed to bind transcoder listener")`, main.rs:65-66 `.expect("transcoder server crashed")`.
findings.recommendation: Wire the sentry/glitchtip Rust SDK (DSN distributed at boot per the §15 island model), report ffmpeg non-zero exits with the captured stderr tail, and avoid bare .expect panics for runtime failures.

findings.title: AppState.work_dir cleanup gap: child work subdirs are only removed on stop/reap, not on natural ffmpeg completion
findings.description: Work subdirs (/work/{id}) are removed only in stop() and the heartbeat reaper. There is no removal path when a session is created but the process exits and the client never explicitly DELETEs — the reaper will eventually catch it via heartbeat staleness, but only if heartbeats stop. If a buggy/abandoned client created sessions that error out instantly (ffmpeg fails — see Docker/stderr findings) yet kept the heartbeat alive, the empty/partial dir persists. There is also no startup sweep of work_dir to clear orphans left by a previous crash, so a crash-loop leaks directories across restarts.
findings.evidence: Removal occurs only at session.rs:121 (stop) and 142 (reap). main.rs creates SessionManager (line ~47) and spawns the reaper but performs no initial work_dir sweep.
findings.recommendation: On boot, clear/recreate work_dir to drop crash orphans. Tie subdir cleanup to the child.wait() completion task so any terminal state (success/failure/kill) cleans up deterministically.

findings.title: main.rs panics on any config/bind error instead of failing gracefully
findings.description: TRANSCODER_BIND parse, listener bind, and axum::serve all use .expect(), so a malformed env var or a port already in use produces a bare panic. Acceptable for fail-fast boot, but combined with no crash telemetry (§15) the operator gets nothing actionable. Also note: the same `expect` style means there is no graceful shutdown handler (no SIGTERM trap), so on container stop in-flight ffmpeg children are not cleanly killed/reaped — they are orphaned by the runtime tear-down (compounds the zombie/reaping finding).
findings.evidence: main.rs:30 `.expect("TRANSCODER_BIND must be a valid socket address")`, :60 `.expect("failed to bind transcoder listener")`, :66 `.expect("transcoder server crashed")`. No tokio signal handler / axum graceful shutdown in the file.
findings.recommendation: Add axum::serve(...).with_graceful_shutdown(...) wired to SIGTERM/SIGINT that kills + reaps all live sessions and cleans work_dir on shutdown.

findings.title: Encoder probe matches by substring and selects HW encoders that are never validated to actually work
findings.description: probe() detects HW encoders by `trimmed.contains(hw)` over `ffmpeg -encoders` output — a presence-in-build check, not a works-on-this-hardware check. ffmpeg lists h264_nvenc/h264_vaapi/h264_qsv even when no GPU/driver is present, so best_h264() can pick a HW encoder that fails at runtime (e.g. h264_vaapi with no /dev/dri, h264_nvenc with no NVIDIA runtime — the compose service mounts no GPU devices). Because no real transcode has run, this has never failed, but the first GPU-less deploy that lists nvenc would spawn ffmpeg, have it die immediately on device-open, and (per the stderr/wait findings) report nothing and hang as 'running'. The roadmap's 'HW encoder paths exist only as arg strings, never exercised' is confirmed.
findings.evidence: encoders.rs:55 `if trimmed.contains(hw)`; best_h264 returns the first listed HW name (71-76). docker-compose transcoder block (lines 44-58) mounts no devices and adds no GPU runtime, yet the image's ffmpeg may still advertise hw encoders.
findings.recommendation: Validate each candidate HW encoder with a tiny test encode (e.g. ffmpeg -f lavfi -i color -t 0.1 -c:v <enc> -f null -) during probe and only register ones that succeed, with automatic libx264 fallback at session start if the HW encode fails. Gate HW selection on the presence of the relevant device mount.

findings.title: Only the first audio track is handled and audio is force-downmixed to stereo — Plex-parity gap for multi-track / surround
findings.description: The plan models a single audio stream (SourceProbe.audio_codec is scalar) and, when re-encoding, hardcodes 2-channel AAC (APPLE_SAFE_AUDIO_CHANNELS), discarding 5.1/7.1. There is no -map selection, so ffmpeg's default mapping (one video + one audio) drops all other audio tracks and all subtitle tracks. Plex preserves/streams multiple audio languages, surround passthrough, and selectable subtitles. As built, a foreign film with English + original audio and forced subs would expose only one track and downmix surround to stereo whenever a re-encode is needed.
findings.evidence: plan.rs:138-140 `encoder: "aac", bitrate_kbps: 160, channels: 2 (APPLE_SAFE_AUDIO_CHANNELS)`; SourceProbe has a single audio_codec/audio_channels (plan.rs:29,35); args.rs emits no -map flags. plan.rs:140 always uses stereo regardless of probe.audio_channels.
findings.recommendation: Model multiple audio/subtitle streams, add -map for explicit track selection, support per-track copy vs encode, and only downmix when the client truly cannot handle the channel layout (keep AC3/EAC3 passthrough or 5.1 AAC where the client supports it). This is required for genuine Plex parity.

findings.title: Unused import warning indicates the crate is not built clean / not linted in CI
findings.description: `use std::sync::Arc;` is unused and produces a compiler warning on every build. Minor on its own, but it signals the crate is not gated by `cargo clippy -D warnings` (or even `cargo build` warning-checks) in CI — consistent with the non-compiling tests slipping through. A transcoder handling untrusted input needs a clean lint gate.
findings.evidence: `cargo build --package transcoder` emits `warning: unused import: 'Arc' --> crates/transcoder/src/routes.rs:10:5`.
findings.recommendation: Remove the import, add `cargo clippy -D warnings` and `cargo test -p transcoder` to .github/workflows/ci.yml so the dead tests and warnings cannot regress.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: This crate is the Plex "transcode-on-the-fly" engine and is currently the weakest pillar of the Plex-replacement story. (1) End-to-end playback does not exist: the advertised playlist_url 404s and segment serving is explicitly unimplemented (routes.rs:121), so there is no Plex-equivalent "press play, get a stream" path. (2) Multi-track audio, surround passthrough, and selectable/forced subtitles — table stakes for Plex — are absent: a single scalar audio stream, forced stereo AAC downmix, no -map, and dead subtitle burn-in (plan.rs:129-142, 151; args.rs:101). (3) HDR->SDR tonemapping is implemented as a filtergraph string but has never run and silently loses to the double-vf bug if subs are ever enabled (args.rs:60-64, 67-103). (4) Hardware acceleration (VideoToolbox/NVENC/VAAPI/QSV) is detected by substring only and never validated — Plex gates HW transcoding on actual device capability; here a GPU-less host can pick a HW encoder that dies at spawn (encoders.rs:54-77). (5) Adaptive bitrate / multi-rendition HLS ladders (Plex's bandwidth adaptation) are not modeled at all — single rendition, bitrate_kbps always None. (6) No seek support despite the seek=kill+respawn comment: start_seconds is hardcoded None and there is no seek route (plan.rs:79,155; args.rs:22). Net: the transcoder is a prototype skeleton with correct instincts but no proven transcode, no auth, an unbuildable image, broken tests, and a guaranteed stderr-deadlock — it is far from Plex-equivalent and is the literal long pole the roadmap calls it.

dossiers.purpose: Core feature UI of the React 19 + Vite + TS SPA: the IPTV player (hls.js/mpegts.js/native-HLS lifecycle, buffer tuning, error recovery, audio/subtitle track switching, unmount cleanup, resume seeking + position reporting), the search-as-you-type stack (SearchInput, ResultGrid, MediaCard, AiToggle, ModeToggle, FeedbackDots, TrendingRow), library browsing (LibraryAlphabet A-Z bucketing, LibraryFilters sort/status), the nine content tabs (Home/Live/Vod/Movies/Tv/Media/Downloads/Users/IptvSeries + IptvTab shell + TabPlaceholder), the detail modal, add-movie/add-series modals, media SourceToggle, queue rows, downloads panels (grab activity + AI usage), and IPTV concurrency-limit handling. Assessed for XSS, accessibility, loading/error/empty states, list performance at scale, and which tabs are real vs placeholder.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: All nine content tabs render real query-backed data (TabPlaceholder is dead/unused), the player has genuine 3-engine playback with deliberate buffer tuning and bounded recovery, search-as-you-type is debounced, and detail/add modals correctly use the native <dialog> element with Escape + focus handling; but it falls short of production-grade due to a player listener-churn loop on every render, unbounded recovery counters, unvirtualized library grids, and IPTV-specific modals that lack focus traps.
what_works: No XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML anywhere in src/ — a full repo-wide grep returned zero hits. All dynamic text (titles, overviews, EPG programme names, usernames) renders as JSX text nodes and image URLs go through src attributes, so React's default escaping covers the render path. Cast links use rel="noopener noreferrer" and posters use referrerPolicy="no-referrer".

IptvPlayer.tsx branches correctly across three engines: native HLS via canPlayType('application/vnd.apple.mpegurl') for Safari/iOS, hls.js for desktop MSE, and mpegts.js for live MPEG-TS — all behind dynamic import() so the heavy media libs are code-split out of the initial bundle (IptvPlayer.tsx:200-313).

Player buffer tuning is deliberate and well-reasoned for the proxy->cloudflared->edge transport, with extensive comments documenting real debugging: hls.js uses lowLatencyMode:false, liveSyncDurationCount:4, maxBufferLength:30, fragLoadingMaxRetry:8 (lines 221-232); mpegts.js enables worker demux, a 1MB stash buffer, liveBufferLatencyChasing, autoCleanupSourceBuffer to bound long-session memory, and fixAudioTimestampGap (lines 295-313). The latest commit 44ff715 is exactly this fix.

The engine-setup effect cleanup is correct in shape: sets cancelled=true, invokes cleanupEngine (hls.destroy() or mpegts unload+detach+destroy), nulls hlsRef, and calls resetVideo() which pauses, removes src, and reloads the element — the right teardown for MSE/worker resources on unmount and grant change (IptvPlayer.tsx:341-358).

DetailModal uses the native <dialog> element with showModal(), an onCancel handler that intercepts Escape, focus moved to the close button on open, and aria-labelledby/aria-describedby — a genuinely accessible modal, not a div-overlay hack (DetailModal.tsx:128-157). AddMovieModal and AddSeriesModal also use native <dialog> and guard onCancel against closing while a mutation is in flight (AddSeriesModal.tsx:202-206).

Search-as-you-type is debounced everywhere via the shared useDebounced hook: Movies/TV/Media at 300ms, IPTV Live/Vod/Series at 250ms, with TanStack Query keying providing request dedup/cancellation (TvTab.tsx:106, VodTab.tsx:45). My initial concern about un-debounced search is disproven by the container code.

Loading, error, and empty states are consistently handled. TrendingRow is exemplary: it has a dedicated describeError() that maps 401/402/429/5xx to actionable copy, a dominantDropReason() that explains WHY a suggestion strip is empty, and distinct skeleton/loading/degraded-source hints (TrendingRow.tsx:58-149). MoviesTab/TvTab DiscoverResults and LibraryResults branch loading/error/empty explicitly with tailored messages.

IPTV concurrency limiting (ConnectionsWidget + ConcurrencyLimitModal + concurrencyLimit.ts) is a real Plex-style 'active streams / max connections' gate: a failed grant due to the limit is caught, the modal is shown, and the original play attempt is stashed in pendingPlay and retried after the user kicks a session (LiveTab.tsx:80-97, 252-266).

Keyboard accessibility on card grids is implemented: IPTV channel/VOD/series cards are role="button" tabIndex={0} with onKeyDown handlers honoring Enter/Space and event.target===currentTarget guards so the favorite/guide sub-buttons don't double-fire (LiveTab.tsx:99-104, 119-126). FeedbackDots, AiToggle, ModeToggle, SourceToggle, and LibraryAlphabet all carry correct ARIA (role=switch/tab/tablist, aria-checked/aria-selected/aria-pressed) and focus-visible outlines.

DownloadsTab is a substantial, real component: it joins SAB queue + Sonarr/Radarr queues + library season stats to compute season/episode size math, shows an indeterminate 'indexer searching' shuttle when SAB is idle but upstream queues are pending, gates pause/resume/cancel on isAdmin and per-item isPending, and routes destructive cancels through the ConfirmProvider (DownloadsTab.tsx:108-453).

Components are unit-tested where it matters: SourceToggle.test.tsx exercises ARIA + onClick by walking the element tree (no jsdom), and IptvPlayer.test.tsx asserts the video element renders for both progressive and HLS grants.

VOD/series resume bars and completion tracking are real: resumePercent/resumePosition compute a progress bar from history, startPositionSecs is passed to the player, and onPositionUpdate marks completed when within 30s of the end (VodTab.tsx:22-31,180-183).

findings.title: Player metadata/timeupdate listener effect re-subscribes on every parent render — consumers pass fresh inline callbacks, producing a remove/add-listener churn loop during playback
findings.description: The metadata/timeupdate/ended listener effect depends on [onEnded, onPositionUpdate, startPositionSecs]. Every consumer passes onPositionUpdate as a fresh inline arrow on each render — VodTab.tsx:180 `onPositionUpdate={(positionSecs, durationSecs) => {...}}`, LiveTab.tsx:247, IptvSeriesTab.tsx:239 — so the callback identity changes every render. onTimeUpdate fires ~4x/sec and calls onPositionUpdate, which calls reportPosition (a mutation/state write in the parent), which re-renders the parent, which yields a new callback identity, which tears down and re-attaches addEventListener('timeupdate', ...). That is a churn loop running continuously during playback, on top of the steady-state listener swaps. On a low-power Apple TV / iOS WebView (the App-Store target) this is real jank and risks dropping a timeupdate mid-swap.
findings.evidence: IptvPlayer: `video.addEventListener('timeupdate', onTimeUpdate)` ... `return () => { video.removeEventListener('timeupdate', onTimeUpdate); ... }`, deps `[onEnded, onPositionUpdate, startPositionSecs]`, and onTimeUpdate body `onPositionUpdate?.(video.currentTime, durationSecs)`. Consumer VodTab.tsx:180: `onPositionUpdate={(positionSecs, durationSecs) => { const completed = ...; reportPosition(...) }}` — new function literal every render.
findings.recommendation: Stash callbacks in refs (cbRef.current = onPositionUpdate in a tiny effect) and run the listener effect with an empty dep array, OR wrap the consumer callbacks in useCallback and remove startPositionSecs from this effect's deps. Additionally throttle position reporting to ~1/sec instead of firing on every timeupdate.

findings.title: mpegts.js recovery counter is monotonic for the effect lifetime — a healthy multi-hour stream that hiccups 5 times total is permanently downgraded to terminal error
findings.description: `let recoveries = 0` is scoped to the effect and only ever increments. Live IPTV over a tunnel legitimately hits transient network errors spread across a long viewing session. After the 5th lifetime hiccup — even if each prior one recovered cleanly minutes apart — the next transient error shows 'Live stream interrupted. Close and re-open the channel to retry.' and gives up, despite the stream being fundamentally healthy. Real players reset their backoff after a sustained good period; this never does.
findings.evidence: `let recoveries = 0; player.on(mpegts.Events.ERROR, ...) { ... if ((isNetwork || isMedia) && recoveries < 5) { recoveries += 1; ... return } setError('Live stream interrupted. Close and re-open the channel to retry.') }` — recoveries is never reset after a successful resume.
findings.recommendation: Reset recoveries to 0 after N seconds of uninterrupted playback (e.g. on a timer armed on each successful timeupdate burst), so the cap means '5 failures in quick succession' not '5 ever'. Also render a Retry button on the terminal error so the user need not close and re-open.

findings.title: Library result grids (Movies/TV) render the entire filtered library unvirtualized; no virtualization dependency exists
findings.description: LibraryResults maps the full filteredLibrary array to MediaCard nodes (each with an poster) with no windowing. MoviesTab/TvTab are the Plex-replacement library surface; a mature household library is hundreds-to-thousands of titles, and there is no react-window / @tanstack/react-virtual in package.json. The A-Z alphabet and status filter narrow the set, but selecting 'All' renders every row. The IPTV tabs mitigate this with 100-item server pagination (LiveTab.tsx:55, VodTab.tsx:47), but the Radarr/Sonarr library grids do not. On an iOS WebView, rendering thousands of poster nodes triggers memory-pressure reloads.
findings.evidence: package.json dependencies contain no virtualization library. TvTab.tsx LibraryResults: `<ResultGrid>{items.map((s) => <MediaCard ... />)}</ResultGrid>` over the entire filteredLibrary; ResultGrid is a plain CSS grid div (ResultGrid.tsx).
findings.recommendation: Adopt @tanstack/react-virtual (same ecosystem they already use) for the Movies/TV library grids, or window the 'All' bucket. The IPTV pagination pattern is the right model to extend.

findings.title: IPTV player and guide modals declare role=dialog/aria-modal but implement no focus trap and no Escape-to-close
findings.description: The IPTV player modal, channel-guide modal, and series-detail modal are plain <div role="dialog" aria-modal="true">, not native <dialog>. Unlike DetailModal/AddModal (which correctly use <dialog>+showModal), these have no focus move into the dialog on open, no Tab containment, and no keydown Escape handler — only a clickable × button. Keyboard and screen-reader users can Tab out to the obscured page behind, and cannot dismiss with Escape. This is inconsistent with the (good) DetailModal pattern and is an App-Store/VoiceOver accessibility gap on the primary live-TV viewing surface. Same pattern repeats in VodTab.tsx:169 and IptvSeriesTab.tsx:182,228.
findings.evidence: LiveTab.tsx:237 `<div className="iptv-player-modal" role="dialog" aria-modal="true" aria-label={playing.title}>` with only an onClick × close button; no useEffect adding a keydown Escape listener, no focus management, no focus trap. ChannelGuide (line 304) and series detail (IptvSeriesTab.tsx:182) are identical div-overlays.
findings.recommendation: Convert these to the native <dialog> + showModal() pattern already proven in DetailModal, or add a shared modal primitive that moves focus in, traps Tab, and binds Escape. Reuse across all IPTV modals for consistency.

findings.title: startPositionSecs resume seek is best-effort, applied uniformly to live and VOD, with no post-seek verification
findings.description: Resume seeking sets video.currentTime = startPositionSecs inside onLoadedMetadata, regardless of grant.delivery. For hls.js/mpegts MSE streams, loadedmetadata can fire before a seekable range exists, so currentTime= may be silently clamped to 0. The value is applied even when the grant is live (though callers only pass startPositionSecs for VOD/series, the player itself does not distinguish). There is no 'seeked'/'canplay' re-assertion if the seek was clamped. This undermines Plex-grade resume on exactly the HLS-VOD transport where it matters.
findings.evidence: `const onLoadedMetadata = () => { updateNativeTracks(); if (startPositionSecs && Number.isFinite(startPositionSecs) && startPositionSecs > 0) { video.currentTime = startPositionSecs } }` — no grant.delivery gate, no verification the seek took.
findings.recommendation: Prefer hls.js startPosition config for MSE, re-assert currentTime on the first 'canplay' if it was clamped to ~0, and skip resume entirely for live delivery.

findings.title: Track-switch dropdowns can desync from the engine: eager updateHlsTracks before MANIFEST_PARSED and no *_TRACK_SWITCHED reconciliation
findings.description: updateHlsTracks() is called immediately after attachMedia (line 249) before MANIFEST_PARSED has populated hls.audioTracks, so the selectors can be empty on first paint and selectedAudio reflects uninitialized engine state. chooseAudioTrack sets hls.audioTrack and optimistically setSelectedAudio(trackId), but the AUDIO_TRACKS_UPDATED listener re-runs updateHlsTracks and resets setSelectedAudio(hls.audioTrack); there is no subscription to AUDIO_TRACK_SWITCHED/SUBTITLE_TRACK_SWITCHED to confirm the actually-playing track, so the UI value can diverge from what plays if hls.js clamps the selection.
findings.evidence: `hls.on(Hls.Events.AUDIO_TRACKS_UPDATED, updateHlsTracks)` resets selected state; `chooseAudioTrack` does `hls.audioTrack = trackId; setSelectedAudio(trackId)` with no SWITCHED confirmation; `updateHlsTracks()` is invoked at line 249 before MANIFEST_PARSED.
findings.recommendation: Subscribe to AUDIO_TRACK_SWITCHED / SUBTITLE_TRACK_SWITCHED and set selected state from the confirmed event; drop the eager pre-MANIFEST_PARSED updateHlsTracks() call.

findings.title: Player exposes no buffering/connecting state and no retry affordance — users see a black <video> box for seconds on a slow tunnel
findings.description: The render is the native
findings.evidence:
Render returns `
` — no waiting/buffering UI, no retry control.
findings.recommendation: Track a status state (connecting|playing|recovering|error), show a spinner/poster while connecting/recovering, and render a Retry button on terminal error that re-keys the engine effect.

findings.title: Library grid poster images lack width/height (layout shift) and broken-image fallback
findings.description: MediaCard posters have loading="lazy" decoding="async" (good) but no intrinsic width/height and no onError handler. The CSS .media-card__poster sets aspect-ratio so CLS is largely contained, but a 404/expired TMDB or Xtream poster URL renders a broken-image glyph rather than swapping to the existing letter fallback (which only shows when poster is falsy, not when it fails to load). IPTV grids (LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab ) have the same no-onError gap.
findings.evidence: MediaCard: `` — no width/height, no onError. LiveTab.tsx:139 `` likewise.
findings.recommendation: Add an onError handler that hides the broken img and reveals the letter fallback; set explicit width/height attributes to fully eliminate CLS.

findings.title: TabPlaceholder is dead code, and no tab degrades gracefully when its upstream integration is unconfigured
findings.description: TabPlaceholder still contains a 'Phase 2 wiring proof' that console.logs on confirm and is not imported by any of the nine content tabs (all render real query-backed UI). It is dead code shipping in the bundle. Separately, unconfigured integrations surface as raw error text ('Couldn't reach Sonarr. Check that the dev server has SONARR_API_KEY in .env.local.' — TvTab.tsx:509) which is developer-facing copy, not a first-run 'connect this service' state — exactly what a TabPlaceholder-style component should provide for a fresh NAS install.
findings.evidence: TabPlaceholder.tsx comment: 'Phase 2 wiring proof... Removed in Phase 3+ when real flows replace this content' and `console.log(`[probe] ${title} confirmed`)`; no consumer imports it. TvTab error copy references '.env.local', a dev-only concept.
findings.recommendation: Delete TabPlaceholder, and replace the dev-oriented integration-error copy with a user-facing 'This service isn't connected yet' empty state for fresh installs.

findings.title: LiveTab Export-M3U uses window.alert and an unguarded clipboard write for a primary action
findings.description: The Export M3U button awaits generatePlaylist(), writes to navigator.clipboard (catch-swallowed), then surfaces the URL via a blocking window.alert(). alert() is non-stylable, blocks the main thread, is unavailable in some embedded/WebView contexts, and is inconsistent with the app's Toast/ConfirmProvider system used everywhere else. A failed generatePlaylist() is unhandled (no try/catch) so a backend error throws an unhandled rejection with no user feedback.
findings.evidence: `onClick={async () => { const { url, expiresAt } = await iptvApi.generatePlaylist(); await navigator.clipboard.writeText(url).catch(() => undefined); alert(`M3U URL copied. Expires ${new Date(expiresAt).toLocaleString()}.\n\n${url}`) }}` — no try/catch around generatePlaylist; alert() for output.
findings.recommendation: Wrap in try/catch and surface success/failure via the existing Toast; replace alert() with a small modal or toast that shows the copyable URL.

findings.title: DownloadsTab/MediaTab error copy leaks dev-environment instructions to end users
findings.description: Production error states tell the user to edit .env.local: 'Couldn't reach SABnzbd. Check that the dev server has SAB_API_KEY in .env.local.' End users (the household / App-Store viewers) have no dev server and no .env.local. This is developer copy shipped to production and additionally renders String(error) which can expose internal URLs/stack detail.
findings.evidence:
DownloadsTab.tsx:85 `

Couldn't reach SABnzbd. Check that the dev server has SAB_API_KEY in .env.local.

` and `

{String(queue.error)}

`; same .env.local copy in TvTab.tsx:509 and MoviesTab.tsx:490.
findings.recommendation: Replace with user-appropriate copy ('Downloads are temporarily unavailable — the server can't reach the downloader.') and gate the raw String(error) detail behind an admin-only / dev-only flag.

findings.title: ConcurrencyLimitModal/ConnectionsWidget error-on-upstream handling could not be fully verified (read interrupted)
findings.description: The connections widget and concurrency modal are confirmed to exist and are wired into IptvTab and the play flows (the concurrency retry/stash logic in LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab is verified). However, the internal error/unknown-state handling of ConnectionsWidget (what it renders when the Xtream upstream is unreachable vs reports 0 active) could not be read in full because the harness tool-output channel degraded during the follow-up reads. Flagged as info, not a confirmed defect: a concurrency gate that silently reads upstream failure as '0 active' would mislead, so this is worth confirming.
findings.evidence: Verified wiring: LiveTab.tsx:80-97 catches concurrencyPayloadFromError and shows ConcurrencyLimitModal; IptvTab.tsx:33 renders <ConnectionsWidget/>. The widget's own error branch was not readable in this session.
findings.recommendation: Confirm ConnectionsWidget renders an explicit 'connections unknown' state on upstream failure rather than 0, so the count is never silently misleading.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Player engine coverage is genuinely strong for a Plex replacement: native HLS (iOS/Safari), hls.js (desktop MSE), and mpegts.js (live MPEG-TS) with real buffer tuning and bounded recovery — the hardest part, and it is functional, not stubbed. The IPTV surface (Live channels with EPG now/next, channel guide with catchup/archive, VOD with resume bars, series with seasons/episodes, favorites, M3U export, and a Plex-style active-streams concurrency gate) is broad and real. The Sonarr/Radarr discover+library side has Plex-grade detail (A-Z bucketing with article stripping, sort/status filters, season disclosure, cast from TMDB, ratings, 'Play in Plex' deep links). Parity gaps: (1) continue-watching/resume is fragile on the HLS-VOD transport where Plex shines (best-effort seek, no verification); (2) Movies/TV library grids are unvirtualized, so they will not browse a large library as smoothly as Plex; (3) the live-TV viewing modals lack the focus-trap/Escape accessibility that DetailModal already gets right, and the player shows no buffering/connecting/retry state (Plex always surfaces connection status); (4) no in-app playback of the Radarr/Sonarr local library — those route out to Plex via deep link, so 'Play in Plex' is a bridge, not a native player, for that content. All nine content tabs are real (TabPlaceholder is unused dead code), so feature breadth toward parity is present; the remaining work is scale-hardening, resume fidelity, player-state UX, and accessibility consistency. NOTE: all 52 in-scope component files plus the consuming tab containers, App.tsx, and package.json were read in full; three iptv/ helper files were only partially re-read because the harness tool-output channel intermittently returned empty during follow-up reads (the same transient issue seen at session start) — the one finding depending on that unread detail is marked info/unverified.

dossiers.subsystem: Server — Bootstrap, DB, Migrator & Security Plumbing
dossiers.purpose: App bootstrap and the shared security/data plumbing of the Hono backend: app.ts (route mounting, middleware order, CORS, body limits, global error handling), index.ts (Sentry init, ffmpeg/boot guards, listener, SIGTERM/SIGINT shutdown), the better-sqlite3 layer (db.ts pragmas/WAL/migration runner, serverDb.ts singleton + server_id), the migrator (migrator.ts sha256 drift guard + DESTRUCTIVE backup gate), the outbound fetch wrapper (upstream.ts), the SSRF guard, sanitize, telemetry PII scrub, ulid, the §4 internal-principal JWE minter, and the telemetry + version routes.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The plumbing boots, opens WAL DBs, runs transactional migrations, mounts routes with real CORS/CSRF/timeout/PII-scrub code, and the §4 JWE boundary has a genuine N-API->PyO3 cross-binding CI gate. But the hardened migrator (sha256 drift guard + DESTRUCTIVE backup gate in migrator.ts) is DEAD CODE — the live DBs run db.ts's separate runner that stores empty checksums and skips every guard — and the SSRF guard is string-only (DNS-rebinding/redirect bypassable). Solid bones, two material safety gaps inside an otherwise functional layer.
what_works: WAL + busy_timeout(5000) + synchronous=NORMAL + foreign_keys=ON are set on every DB at open (db.ts:19-22) — the correct pragma set for a single-process better-sqlite3 (synchronous, in-process) app on a NAS.

ulid.ts is genuinely correct: CSPRNG via node:crypto randomBytes, byte & 31 over a 32-symbol Crockford alphabet is provably unbiased (256 is an exact multiple of 32), and the alphabet matches the contract's /^[0-9A-HJKMNP-TV-Z]{26}$/ class. The header's unbias claim holds.

upstream.ts wraps fetch with an AbortController timeout, ALWAYS clears the timer in finally, and synthesizes a 504 on abort/network error so route handlers never face an unhandled rejection (upstream.ts:30-62); it also drains the body and correctly nulls it for 101/204/205/304 (lines 35-38).

telemetryPiiScrub.ts is the strongest module in scope: non-mutating recursive walk, case-insensitive substring key denylist that folds in Sentry's full default denylist as defense-in-depth, regex strip of stream-grant t=<token> and JWE eyJ... ciphertext in BOTH events and breadcrumb data.url (the Mozilla 2021 token-in-breadcrumb bug class), Authorization/Cookie header scrubbing, and a setSentryUser() wrapper that THROWS on any non-anonymous id/username to enforce the App Store linked=No label at dev time.

Telemetry is actually wired, not just declared: index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init with beforeSend=piiScrub and beforeBreadcrumb=piiBreadcrumbScrub, and loudly warns (not silently no-ops) when EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is unset.

The §4 internal-principal boundary has a REAL cross-language gate: internalPrincipal.crossBinding.test.ts mints via the prod N-API path and decrypts via the recommender's PyO3 binding, asserting claim-for-claim equality and correct deviceId-omitted->None handling, with CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1 turning a missing/drifted extension into a red gate instead of a green-by-skip. media.ts (line 47-52) and recommender.ts fail CLOSED (502) when minting fails rather than proxying anonymously.

CSRF middleware (csrf.ts) is sound: state-changing methods require an Origin in env.allowedOrigins; missing Origin in prod is rejected; an empty allow-list in prod fails closed ('csrf_misconfigured'); GET/HEAD are exempt by design and side-effecting GETs opt back in via requireTrustedOrigin. Cookies are SameSite=None only in prod (session.ts:429) and HttpOnly+Secure, making Origin the correct sole CSRF distinguisher.

Migrations are transactional (BEGIN/exec/COMMIT with ROLLBACK on error) in both runners (db.ts:119-127, migrator.ts:292-300), applied in lexically-sorted version order with an applied-set skip, and CRLF-normalized — idempotent re-runs are safe.

ssrfGuard's range table is correct: isPrivateIPv4/IPv6 covers 10/8, 127, 0/8, 169.254 metadata, 172.16/12, 192.168/16, 100.64/10 CGNAT, 224+ multicast, ::1/::, fc00::/7, fe80::/10 and ::ffff: mapped, treating octets >255 as unsafe; it IS invoked on the two IPTV proxy fetch paths (iptv.ts:680, iptv.ts:1024).

index.ts fails fast at boot on missing/old ffmpeg (validateFfmpegOrExit) and runs ensureServerId() before serving; serverDb.ts uses INSERT OR IGNORE so server_id is generated exactly once and stable across reboots. version.ts correctly re-uses the same ensureServerId (re-exported from session.ts:339 -> serverDb.ts), so /api/version, device-token serverId claims, and Keychain keying all share ONE identity source.

findings.title: Migrator drift guard + DESTRUCTIVE backup gate are DEAD CODE — server.db/iptv.db use a different runner that stores empty checksums and skips every safety check
findings.description: There are TWO migration runners. serverDb.ts:18 and iptvDb.ts both call openDb() from db.ts, whose own applyMigrations() (db.ts:93-139) is what actually runs in production. It inserts checksum='' for every migration (db.ts:122), does NO checksum verification on re-run, NO `-- DESTRUCTIVE`/DROP TABLE detection, and NO last_backup_at gate. The hardened migrator.ts — which DOES compute sha256, warn on drift, refuse unmarked DROP TABLE, and enforce a 10-minute backup window — is imported only by its own tests (verified: grep for applyMigrations callers returns db.ts, iptvDb.ts, migrator.ts, db.test.ts, iptvDb.test.ts; no production module imports migrator.ts). Every §7.1/§7.3/§7.4 protection in migrator.ts's 23-line docblock is inert for the DBs that boot. A teammate editing an already-applied migration, or shipping a DROP TABLE, triggers none of the guards on the live path; the §7.4 'DESTRUCTIVE refused without a recent backup' guarantee does not exist in running code.
findings.evidence: db.ts:122 `insert.run(version, new Date().toISOString(), '')` // checksum hardcoded empty. db.ts contains no reference to DESTRUCTIVE, sha256, or last_backup_at. serverDb.ts:18 `cached = openDb(MIGRATIONS_DIR, env.SERVER_DB_PATH, 'server')`. Caller grep: migrator.ts is referenced only by migrator.ts + db.test.ts/iptvDb.test.ts, never by serverDb.ts/iptvDb.ts/index.ts.
findings.recommendation: Route openDb() through migrator.ts's applyMigrations (passing the serverDb handle for DESTRUCTIVE enforcement), OR backport sha256/DESTRUCTIVE/backup logic into db.ts. Delete the unused runner so there is one code path. Until then, stop claiming §7.4 enforcement — it is not on the live path.

findings.title: ssrfGuard validates the URL host string only — DNS resolution is never pinned and redirects are followed unchecked (DNS-rebinding / redirect SSRF bypass)
findings.description: isPublicHttpsUpstream() inspects url.hostname as a STRING. A public name like `https://attacker.com` (has a dot, not an IP literal, not .local/.internal) passes, but nothing forces the subsequent fetch() to connect to the IP the guard reasoned about. An attacker controlling DNS for a public name can resolve it to 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 / the docker gateway (DNS rebinding or just a private A record), reaching exactly the cloud-metadata and internal services the module header says it's defending. Worse, the IPTV proxy fetches (iptv.ts:680, 1024 -> upstream/fetch) use default redirect handling (redirect:'follow'), so a guard-approved public URL that 302-redirects to http://169.254.169.254/ is fetched and streamed back without re-validation — and the http:-only rule is bypassed because the redirect target is fetched by the runtime, not re-checked by the guard.
findings.evidence: ssrfGuard.ts:65 `const host = url.hostname` then only string checks; no dns.lookup, no IP pinning, no post-resolution re-check. upstream.ts:33 `await fetch(url, { ...init, signal: controller.signal })` — no `redirect:'manual'`. Callers iptv.ts:680/1024 guard the initial URL only.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the host (dns.lookup {all:true}), reject if ANY resolved address is private, then connect by pinned IP (or use an undici Agent with a lookup hook that re-validates each address). Set redirect:'manual' on proxy fetches and re-run isPublicHttpsUpstream on every Location before following. As written, the guard is bypassable by anyone who controls a DNS name or a redirect.

findings.title: No app-level expiry/clock-skew test for the §4 JWE; Node has no verify path and key rotation (kid keymap) is comment-only
findings.description: The cross-binding test proves N-API mint -> PyO3 decrypt round-trips claims, but it sets a fixed exp and asserts ONLY equality — no test asserts that the receiver REJECTS an expired (exp in the past) or tampered JWE. The whole point of the 60s TTL (§4) is replay containment; that enforcement is unverified by any test in this repo. The Node side is mint-only (the test itself states 'N-API doesn't have internal_principal_decrypt', line 230-231), so Hono cannot verify an inbound principal if a service ever calls back. The kid is a single hardcoded constant INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_KID='internal-v1' (line 31); the rotation 'verifier keymap stays populated with both kids' is described only in the docstring — there is no code that accepts two kids, so a real key rotation would instantly invalidate every in-flight token. INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_TTL_SECS=60 (line 35) is exported but enforcement lives entirely on the Python receiver, which was not in scope to confirm.
findings.evidence: internalPrincipal.ts:31 `export const INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_KID = 'internal-v1'` (only kid). crossBinding.test.ts:161-163 uses fixed `iat: 1748169600, exp: 1748169660` and asserts equality only; no negative test for expired/tampered tokens. crossBinding.test.ts:230 comment: 'N-API doesn't have internal_principal_decrypt (Hono only mints, never receives)'.
findings.recommendation: Add negative cross-binding tests: a JWE with exp in the past must be REJECTED by PyO3 decrypt; a single-byte-flipped ciphertext must fail. Make the kid env-driven and implement an actual multi-kid verifier keymap before any rotation. Confirm (with a test) the receiver applies bounded clock skew, not unbounded acceptance.

findings.title: No global error handler and no request body-size limit on the Hono app
findings.description: app.ts registers only logger, conditional CORS, and requireSafeOrigin (verified: the only app.use calls are lines 36, 42, 66) — there is no app.onError(), no app.notFound(), and no bodyLimit anywhere in bootstrap. Any route handler that throws yields Hono's default framework-shaped 500 rather than a consistent scrubbed JSON envelope, and such throws are not funneled to Sentry from a central place (only unhandledRejection is integrated). With no body limit, every POST (feedback, invites, device pairing, recommender events, the media proxy which does c.req.arrayBuffer() at media.ts:65) accepts an arbitrarily large payload — a trivial memory-pressure DoS on a NAS and at App-Store scale. sanitize.ts caps title length at 200 only AFTER the full body is parsed, so it does not bound ingress.
findings.evidence: app.ts middleware is exactly: app.use('*', logger()) (36), conditional cors (42-59), app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin) (66). No onError/notFound/bodyLimit/secureHeaders present anywhere in the file. media.ts:65 `body = await c.req.arrayBuffer()` with no size guard.
findings.recommendation: Add app.onError() mapping known errors to a stable JSON shape, reporting unknowns to Sentry, never leaking internals; add app.notFound(); add bodyLimit (e.g. 256KB JSON) before bodies are read; add secureHeaders() on API responses.

findings.title: Shutdown closes DBs before stopping the HTTP listener and never drains in-flight requests
findings.description: shutdown() calls closeIptvDb() then closeServerDb() then process.exit() synchronously. The serve() return value is discarded (line 72), so the listener is never stopped, and there is no drain wait. On SIGTERM from docker/compose during a NAS redeploy, the DBs are closed out from under any request still executing a prepared statement — better-sqlite3 will throw 'The database connection is not open' on that in-flight request (e.g. an active device-token verify doing a SELECT on device_tokens, or a streaming media proxy). Closing DBs before stopping the listener is backwards: you must stop accepting/finish requests first, THEN close DBs. This matters for a 24/7 home server that restarts on every update.
findings.evidence: index.ts:72 `serve({ fetch: app.fetch, port: env.port }, ...)` — return value not captured. index.ts:93-99 shutdown(): `closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...)` — no server.close(), no drain.
findings.recommendation: Capture the handle from serve(); in shutdown() call server.close() first to stop accepting new connections, await a short drain window for in-flight requests, then closeIptvDb()/closeServerDb(), then exit. Prevents mid-request DB-closed errors and abrupt stream cuts on redeploy.

findings.title: version.ts hardcodes auth_modes to ['plex'] only, contradicting the shipped Apple PIN-pair + passkey/local auth surface
findings.description: The public /api/version response — which Apple apps use to decide which sign-in buttons to render (the file header says so explicitly) — always returns auth_modes: ['plex'] with a comment 'Plex is the only mode supported today.' But app.ts:107 mounts /api/auth/device (Apple PIN-pair), app.ts:111 mounts /api/auth/passkey, and migration 0003/0004 + the members allowlist establish apple:/local: subs and a passkey/SIWA flow as shipped (MEMORY: 'Auth = SIWA + Plex parallel + invites'). A correct Apple/SPA client reading this endpoint would NOT offer Sign in with Apple or passkey, defeating the very M2 pairing flow the endpoint exists to enable. (accepting_device_pairs is correctly derived from deviceTokenSecret — only auth_modes is stale.)
findings.evidence: version.ts:21-23 `// Plex is the only mode supported today...` then `auth_modes.push('plex')`, no apple/local/passkey. Contrast app.ts:107 `app.route('/api/auth/device', device)` and app.ts:111 `app.route('/api/auth/passkey', passkey)`.
findings.recommendation: Derive auth_modes from actual configured capability (plex if PLEX_CLIENT_ID; apple if Apple SIWA creds present; local/passkey if a WebAuthn RP is configured) instead of a hardcoded ['plex']. Clients gate on this contract surface — it must reflect reality.

findings.title: db.ts legacy backfill + fresh inserts store empty checksums and the two runners' schema_migrations DDL disagree, so unifying them later will mis-fire
findings.description: Even if db.ts were wired to verify checksums later, every row it writes today is uncheckable: fresh applies store '' (line 122) and both legacy backfills store '' (lines 59, 76). migrator.ts's verify skips only rows equal to '(file-not-found)' (line 258), NOT '' — so a '' row compared against a real sha256 would become a permanent false 'mismatch' WARN if the runners were ever unified. The two runners also disagree on the column DDL: db.ts declares `checksum TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''` (lines 56, 73) while migrator.ts declares `checksum TEXT NOT NULL` with no default (lines 139, 180) — a DB bootstrapped by one and extended by the other can hit a NOT NULL violation.
findings.evidence: db.ts:122/59/76 write `''`; db.ts:56/73 `checksum TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''` vs migrator.ts:139/180 `checksum TEXT NOT NULL`. migrator.ts:258 `if (stored !== checksum && stored !== '(file-not-found)')` — '' not excluded.
findings.recommendation: When consolidating to one runner (see the high finding), compute real sha256 on apply and have verify treat '' as 'unknown — skip'. Align the schema_migrations DDL exactly so a DB is never bootstrapped by one shape and extended by another.

findings.title: Hardcoded D18 boot self-destruct on 2026-06-25 will refuse to boot every server on/after that date
findings.description: index.ts throws (exit 1) unconditionally if Date.now() >= 2026-06-25, to force removal of the legacy SHA-256 session-key path (session.ts legacyKey, line 349). This is a calendar bomb: today is 2026-05-30. On 2026-06-25 ANY container that restarts — or any fresh deploy where the legacy path hasn't been removed — refuses to boot, regardless of whether legacy cookies are still in circulation. For an App-Store-bound product on an unattended NAS, a routine reboot after that date is a hard outage with no operator override. Intent (force the migration) is sound; mechanism (brick all boots) is operationally dangerous.
findings.evidence: index.ts:61 `if (Date.now() >= new Date('2026-06-25').getTime()) { throw new Error('HKDF legacy-SHA256 grace window expired; remove legacyKey path per D18') }`. session.ts:348-349 still defines the legacyKey it's guarding.
findings.recommendation: Gate the throw on whether the legacy path is actually still present (a build-time const flipped when legacyKey is deleted), or downgrade to a loud startup WARN plus a CI check that fails the build after the deadline. A running production service must never self-brick on a wall-clock date with no override.

findings.title: telemetryPiiScrub substring key denylist over-redacts benign diagnostic keys; t= and JWE regexes are conservative but have known gaps
findings.description: isRedactedKey is case-insensitive SUBSTRING match against a set including 'key','auth','token','hash','session' — so legitimate crash-context fields like 'keyboard', 'authority', 'hashRoute', 'sessionCount', 'monkey' are blanket-REDACTED, degrading telemetry usefulness (the contract wants telemetry USEFUL, not only safe). STREAM_TOKEN_RE `/\bt=([^&\s"']+)/g` also clobbers any unrelated `t=` param (e.g. ?t=1 cache-busters), and JWE_CIPHERTEXT_RE only catches compact-serialized tokens contiguous in one string — a JWE split across object fields, or a raw base64url device token not starting with the literal `eyJ` header, is not caught (those rely on the field-key denylist). Acceptable conservatism for safety, but the key false-positives in particular will hide useful debugging context.
findings.evidence: Line 81 `if (lower.includes(pattern.toLowerCase()))` over a set with 'key','auth','token','hash','session'. Line 129 STREAM_TOKEN_RE. Line 138 `JWE_CIPHERTEXT_RE = /eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]{8,}\.[^"'\s;,]*/g`.
findings.recommendation: Anchor/exact-match the generic terms (keep substring only for clearly-secret roots) or maintain a known-safe-key allowlist; scope STREAM_TOKEN_RE to URL contexts; add a tests/vectors/telemetry-pii-scrub.json case for a token-split-across-fields scenario and document the non-eyJ raw-token reliance on the key denylist.

findings.title: Default Hono logger logs full request paths including stream-grant t= tokens — an unscrubbed side channel parallel to the Sentry scrub
findings.description: app.use('*', logger()) is Hono's default logger, which prints method + full path (including query string). IPTV/stream proxy URLs carry t=<token> query params — exactly the value telemetryPiiScrub strips from Sentry. Those land in container stdout/access logs unredacted, which on a NAS may be shipped to log aggregation. The PII scrub effort is thorough for Sentry but the plain access log is an unscrubbed channel for the same token class, making the two policies inconsistent.
findings.evidence: app.ts:36 `app.use('*', logger())` (Hono default logs the full request path with query). telemetryPiiScrub.ts:129 treats `t=<token>` as sensitive.
findings.recommendation: Replace the default logger with a custom one that strips the query string (or just the t= param) from logged paths, or disable path logging on the /api/iptv proxy routes, to keep the access log consistent with the Sentry scrub policy.

findings.title: Migration runner derives version with parseInt and silently continues on non-.sql/odd names; gaps and duplicate-version files are not detected
findings.description: db.ts's runner enumerates *.sql, sorts lexically, and parses the version from file.split('_')[0]. There is no check for (a) duplicate version numbers across two files, (b) gaps in the sequence, or (c) a file whose numeric prefix collides with an already-applied version from a different filename. Lexical sort also breaks ordering if the project ever exceeds 9999 migrations or mixes widths (e.g. 0010 vs 010) — unlikely at 4 files today but a latent ordering hazard. migrator.ts's versionFromFilename at least throws on a non-numeric prefix (line 189); db.ts's parseInt would yield NaN and then applied.has(NaN) is always false, so a malformed filename would attempt to apply on every boot.
findings.evidence: db.ts:102 `fs.readdirSync(migrationsDir).filter(f => f.endsWith('.sql')).sort()`, db.ts:109 `const version = parseInt(file.split('_')[0], 10)` with no NaN guard and no duplicate/gap detection.
findings.recommendation: Validate that parsed versions are unique, strictly increasing, and non-NaN; throw on a malformed filename rather than silently retrying it every boot. (Consolidating onto migrator.ts, which already throws on non-numeric prefixes, addresses most of this.)

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: This subsystem is infrastructure, not a user-facing Plex feature, but several items bear on operating a Plex replacement safely at household + App-Store scale: (1) Plex depends on a robust, drift-checked library-DB migration story across years of upgrades; here the hardened migrator with sha256/backup gates EXISTS but is NOT on the live path (db.ts runs instead), so long-term schema evolution on a self-hoster's NAS is riskier than Plex's, and the §7.4 backup-before-destructive guarantee is unmet in running code. (2) Plex Media Server proxies remote content and is itself a hardened SSRF target; the current string-only ssrfGuard is DNS-rebinding- and redirect-bypassable, which is below the bar for a server that proxies provider-controlled HLS manifest URLs. (3) The §4 internal-principal boundary (Hono->recommender/media-core) is the analog of Plex's internal service trust; the cross-binding round-trip is genuinely tested, but expiry/tamper REJECTION is not, and key rotation is comment-only — acceptable for now, must harden before multi-service M3/M4 scale. (4) A 24/7 Plex substitute must survive NAS reboots/updates without corrupting its DB or cutting active streams; the current shutdown closes DBs before draining requests and never stops the listener, weaker than expected for an always-on server. None of these block a demo; the migrator dead-code, SSRF rebinding, and shutdown ordering should all be closed before this is trusted as an always-on Plex substitute. Telemetry/version/CSRF/PII plumbing is at or above typical self-hosted-app quality.

dossiers.purpose: A Hono backend that proxies a curated, allow-listed subset of Sonarr/Radarr/SABnzbd APIs to the SPA so household members can browse, add, and (for admins) manage downloads without ever seeing the upstream API keys. It enforces a hard disk-space gate (MIN_FREE_GB), a per-title size cap (env.maxMovieBytes / maxTvBytesPerEpisode) that intercepts Radarr/Sonarr auto-search and drives its own release selection, an append-only JSONL grab-event log for observability, a per-user Claude usage/cost log, and admin-only Discord webhook provisioning across both *arr apps.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The security-critical invariants (server-side key injection, fail-closed disk gate, global Origin/CSRF gate, path-traversal defense on DELETE) are real and correct, and telemetry/usage-logging ARE actually wired; the gaps that keep it below production-grade are no rate limiting, in-memory-only disk-reservation accounting that races across requests/replicas, JSONL stores that lose history at scale, unfiltered upstream query forwarding, and thin per-route test coverage.
what_works: API keys never reach the browser: sonarrFetch/radarrFetch inject X-Api-Key as a server-held header (services/sonarr.ts:29, radarr.ts:29) and sabCall splices apikey as a server-side query param (services/sab.ts:16); none are sourced from client input.

Disk-space gate is genuinely fail-closed: the Sonarr/Radarr add paths reject with 507 on missing path, unknown path, non-finite freeSpace, or freeSpace<minFreeBytes (sonarr.ts:525-558, radarr.ts validateRadarrRootFolderSpace:34-69), and env.ts:72-82 throws at boot on a non-numeric MIN_FREE_GB instead of letting NaN silently disable the gate. Tests confirm admins are gated too (sonarr.test.ts:210).

Path-traversal on DELETE/upgrade/monitor is closed by Number.isSafeInteger(id)&&id>0 guards (sonarr.ts:843-846, radarr.ts:592-595, 508-514) with an accurate comment explaining the encoded-slash + WHATWG URL normalization attack they defeat; the encoded-slash attack is regression-tested (sonarr.test.ts:152, radarr.test.ts:112).

CSRF defense is mounted globally — app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin) (app.ts:66) gates every POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE on Origin∈allowedOrigins, fails closed in prod (csrf.ts:35-38), and env.ts:103-108 refuses to boot in prod without ALLOWED_ORIGINS.

SAB read/mutate split is sound: reads require ?mode= and reject any ?name= param (sab.ts:32-34) to kill the legacy GET-name attack (regression-tested at sab.test.ts:115); pause/resume/delete are POST/DELETE + ensureAdmin (sab.ts:59-90); nzoId flows into value= via URLSearchParams.set so it is auto-encoded and name= is hardcoded server-side (no injection).

Non-admin policy laundering is correctly defended: materializeNonAdminSeriesBody/MovieBody discard everything outside a descriptive-metadata allowlist and re-derive rootFolderPath/qualityProfileId/monitored from server config (sonarr.ts:330-486, radarr.ts:197-332); tests assert a hostile rootFolderPath/qualityProfileId/seasons[] are scrubbed and replaced with server defaults (sonarr.test.ts:313, radarr.test.ts:234).

Upstream calls are uniformly time-bounded via fetchWithTimeout (LAN_TIMEOUT_MS=15s) which synthesizes a 504 on abort/network error (upstream.ts:24-63) so no route hangs on a dead Sonarr/Radarr/SAB; the body is fully drained into an arrayBuffer before constructing the replay Response, avoiding stream-reuse bugs.

Telemetry IS wired: index.ts:15,37 imports @sentry/node (package.json:28, ^9.47.1) and calls Sentry.init({ dsn, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, onUnhandledRejectionIntegration }) gated on env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, with env.ts:267 hard-failing prod boot if the DSN is absent — the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract is satisfied at the SDK-init level.

Usage/cost logging IS wired: suggestions.ts:2434-2436/2472/2590-2593 calls appendUsageEvent with type 'claude_call'/'claude_error' on the real Claude path, so /api/usage/me|admin|log surface live per-user spend; computeCostCents (usageLog.ts:265) is unit-tested against Haiku 4.5 pricing.

Notifications connector management is unusually careful: a serialized mutation lock (withDiscordMutationLock), fail-closed-on-list-error to avoid stacking duplicate webhooks, full reverse-order rollback of partial mutations (notifications.ts:32-44,96-119,205-287), an https://(discord.com|discordapp.com)/api/webhooks/ allowlist (notifications.ts:201), and thorough tests for create/update/dup-suppression/partial-delete (notifications.test.ts).
Radarr add rolls back the created movie when the capped grab fails or finds nothing and surfaces a 424 with phase detail (radarr.ts:439-485) rather than leaving an orphaned unmonitored entry.

grabLog/usageLog serialize appends through a single in-flight write queue to prevent interleaved JSONL lines, rotate at 5MB, tail-read backward in 64KB chunks, and summarizeUsage uses a scan-until-cutoff with a SUMMARY_HARD_CAP memory bound (usageLog.ts:118-175,216-258); chunk-spanning tail reads and rotation are tested (grabLog.test.ts:66,107).

findings.title: No rate limiting on any *arr/SAB route — an authenticated user can hammer indexer searches and disk I/O
findings.description: There is no rate-limiting middleware anywhere in the *arr/SAB surface (app.ts mounts only logger, cors, requireSafeOrigin; no token-bucket on any route). The add routes trigger real indexer release searches (sonarr.ts grabTvUnderCap does a per-season /api/v3/release search; radarr.ts grabBestUnderCap does one per add) and the read forwarders proxy straight to Sonarr/Radarr/SAB. Any authenticated household member (or a compromised/leaked session) can loop POST /api/radarr/api/v3/movie or GET /api/sonarr/api/v3/series/lookup and (a) burn the usenet provider's API/DMCA budget, (b) spin Sonarr's indexer searches, (c) flood the append-only grab log (each add writes grab_started + per-pick events). At App-Store scale with many users this is a self-inflicted DoS and a real cost vector against the indexers/provider.
findings.evidence: app.ts only mounts logger()/cors/requireSafeOrigin before the route table; grep for rateLimit/RateLimit/tokenBucket across server/ (non-test) returns only auth.ts (a passkey/login limiter), nothing on sonarr/radarr/sab routes.
findings.recommendation: Add a per-session token-bucket on the add/search/upgrade routes and the SAB mutate routes, with tighter limits on release-search-bearing POSTs than on cheap reads. (ioredis is not a current dep; a small in-process limiter or adding a shared store is needed.)

findings.title: Disk-space reservation is in-memory and single-instance; Radarr has none at all — concurrent adds bust MIN_FREE_GB
findings.description: The Sonarr path uses a module-level Map (pendingRootFolderReservations, sonarr.ts:21-40) to subtract in-flight planned bytes so two concurrent TV adds don't both pass the gate against the same stale freeSpace snapshot. The Radarr path has NO equivalent: validateRadarrRootFolderSpace reads folder.freeSpace once, and grabBestUnderCap filters releases on 'rootFolder.freeSpace - r.size >= env.minFreeBytes' against that same snapshot. Two near-simultaneous movie adds both read identical free space, both pass, and both grab up to maxMovieBytes each — the combined download drives the disk below the reserve even though each 'passed.' Even the Sonarr reservation is volatile (lost on crash/restart while the SAB download is still queued) and per-replica (no protection under multiple backend instances at App-Store scale), and neither path reconciles the snapshot against SAB's actually-queued bytes.
findings.evidence: radarr.ts:144 'rootFolder.freeSpace - r.size >= env.minFreeBytes' uses spaceGate.folder captured at request start with no pendingRadarrReservations analogue to sonarr.ts:21; sonarr.ts:21 'const pendingRootFolderReservations = new Map<string, number>()' is module-scope in-memory.
findings.recommendation: Port reservation bookkeeping to the Radarr path, back it with shared state (a DB row or shared store) keyed by root-folder path, reconcile against the SAB queue, and/or serialize adds per root folder. At minimum document that the gate is best-effort and single-instance-only.

findings.title: Read forwarders pass arbitrary client query strings straight to upstream Sonarr/Radarr
findings.description: forwardRead copies the client's entire query string (new URL(c.req.url).searchParams) into the upstream fetch via url.searchParams.set for every allow-listed read path (/series, /episode, /queue, /series/lookup, etc.). Only the PATH is allow-listed; query params are unfiltered. The X-Api-Key is a header so a ?apikey= override is harmless, but this lets any authenticated user drive arbitrary Sonarr/Radarr query parameters — /api/v3/series/lookup?term=<arbitrary> issues outbound indexer/metadata lookups, and unexpected params can change upstream behavior (paging, includeImages bloat) and amplify response size. Combined with the no-rate-limit finding, this is the cheap-amplification surface.
findings.evidence: sonarr.ts:70-71 'const search = new URL(c.req.url).searchParams; const r = await sonarrFetch(path, { method: "GET" }, search)'; services/sonarr.ts:20 'for (const [k, v] of query.entries()) url.searchParams.set(k, v)' — no param-name allowlist.
findings.recommendation: Allow-list query parameters per endpoint (e.g. seriesId/movieId numeric, term length-capped) and drop the rest, mirroring the path allow-list philosophy the file header champions.

findings.title: Grab-event /by-item is a guessable-id IDOR and grab events have no user attribution
findings.description: GET /api/grabs/by-item?app=sonarr&itemId=N is available to any authenticated user (only /recent is requireAdmin). The route comment concedes 'itemId acts as a weak capability.' Sonarr/Radarr ids are small sequential integers, so any user can enumerate itemId=1..N and read the full grab history (titles, sizes, quality, failure reasons) for everything anyone added. GrabEvent has no sub/userId field at all (grabLog.ts:31-51), so events cannot be scoped to the requester even if desired, and grab activity can never be attributed to a user for abuse investigation. Low blast radius for an invite-only household, but a genuine horizontal-authZ gap that matters at App-Store scale.
findings.evidence: grabs.ts:31 'grabs.get('/by-item', async (c) => {' with only a Number.isSafeInteger(itemId) check, no requireAdmin and no ownership filter; grabLog.ts GrabEvent type (31-51) has no sub/userId.
findings.recommendation: Record the initiating sub on GrabEvent at append time (the add routes already hold session.sub), and either filter /by-item to the caller's own events or apply a real capability check.

findings.title: JSONL grab/usage logs lose history and tail-scan O(file) per request — won't survive App-Store scale
findings.description: The store keeps only the live file plus one rotated .1 (rename overwrites any previous .1), so on a busy household the second rotation silently discards all older history — breaking the very 'why did this fail months ago' use case the module exists for, at exactly the volume that makes it useful. readEventsForItem scans a SCAN_WINDOW of max(500, limit*50) lines backward and filters in memory on every poll, and the DownloadsTab/DetailModal poll it. summarizeUsage can hold up to SUMMARY_HARD_CAP=100k events (~30MB) per call with no caching. The module's own comment says 'If we ever need richer queries, swap this module for SQLite' — at the stated scale that time is now.
findings.evidence: grabLog.ts:53 'const ROTATE_AT_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024' with single .1 rotation (84-87 renames over the previous .1); grabLog.ts:167 'const SCAN_WINDOW = Math.max(500, limit * 50)' re-scanned per /by-item call.
findings.recommendation: Move grab + usage events into better-sqlite3 (already a dependency) with indexes on (app,itemId,ts) and (sub,ts); eliminates the rotation-data-loss, the per-poll tail scan, and the per-request in-memory filter.

findings.title: Sonarr add fires grabTvUnderCap fire-and-forget, so users see success even when the cap grab silently grabs nothing
findings.description: The Radarr path was deliberately changed to AWAIT the capped grab and roll back / return 424 on failure (radarr.ts:427-485). The Sonarr path still does 'void grabTvUnderCap(...).catch(...)' and returns the upstream 2xx immediately. So a TV add where every release is over the per-episode cap (all_rejected_by_cap) or the indexer returns nothing returns success to the user; the only trace is a console.log + a grab-log event. The series sits monitored with no files and no user-visible signal — exactly the 'apparently-successful add with nothing downloaded' failure the surrounding comments repeatedly try to avoid. The asymmetry with Radarr is also a maintenance hazard.
findings.evidence: sonarr.ts:711 'void grabTvUnderCap(itemId, monitored, itemTitle, folderSnapshot).catch((e) => {' vs radarr.ts:439 'const grab = await grabBestUnderCap(...)' with 424 surfacing on no_releases/all_rejected_by_cap.
findings.recommendation: Either await the TV grab and surface a 424-equivalent when nothing was grabbed (mirroring Radarr), or return an explicit 'add accepted, grab pending' status the SPA can poll the grab log against, so the silent-no-download case is visible.

findings.title: Test coverage is thin on cap/rollback/materialize security invariants for the highest-stakes paths
findings.description: Coverage is good on disk-gate, DELETE bad-id, and non-admin materialize, but the Sonarr cap-selection logic (grabTvUnderCap: per-episode cap math, season-pack vs episode dedup, all_rejected_by_cap, all_rejected_by_profile, the planned_size_exceeds_free_space reservation path) is essentially untested — the suite explicitly opts OUT of exercising the background grab ('grabTvUnderCap runs via void ... real setTimeouts we don't want to wait on', sonarr.test.ts:662-669) and only asserts the grab is NOT spawned in the skip cases. Radarr's upgrade/grab path is well-tested by contrast, but the Sonarr cap math (the most complex code in the subsystem) has no direct unit test. sab.test.ts notes 'full coverage of SAB modes lives in integration' with no such integration test found in scope.
findings.evidence: sonarr.test.ts:662-669 comment 'the background grab uses real setTimeouts (2s + repeated 1.5s polls) we dont want to wait on ... force searchForMissingEpisodes:false to skip spawning the grab entirely'; no test calls grabTvUnderCap's selection logic directly.
findings.recommendation: Extract grabTvUnderCap's release-selection/dedup/cap math into a pure function and unit-test it (season-pack preference, overlap avoidance, per-episode cap, profile rejection, reservation overcommit), so the most complex logic in the subsystem has a regression net independent of setTimeout timing.

findings.title: SAB read forwarder relays SAB's HTML error page verbatim on a rejected apikey
findings.description: sabCall forces output=json, but the service comment notes 'a non-JSON 200 from SAB usually means the apikey was rejected and SAB returned its HTML error page.' forward() blindly relays r.status + body with the upstream Content-Type, so a misconfigured/rotated SAB_API_KEY surfaces to the SPA as a 200 with an HTML body the client fails to JSON-parse — an opaque client error rather than a clear 502. There is no JSON-shape validation of the SAB response.
findings.evidence: sab.ts:92-98 forward() relays body+status+upstream Content-Type with no JSON check; sab.ts service comment:3-5 acknowledges the HTML-on-bad-key behavior.
findings.recommendation: Detect a non-JSON 200 from SAB in forward()/sabCall and map it to 502 {error:'sab_auth_failed'} so a bad key is diagnosable.

findings.title: Movie 'upgrade' selects by qualityWeight sort with no verified delta against the current file
findings.description: The upgrade route relies entirely on Radarr setting rejected:true for equal-or-worse releases, then grabs eligible[0] by qualityWeight. If Radarr's rejection semantics differ (custom formats, propers, indexer quirks), a non-strict-upgrade can pass the filter and Radarr's import 'then decides,' so the dashboard may grab+import bandwidth for a lateral move. This mirrors the ROADMAP-noted M3 matcher concern (results.first() with no confidence filter) in the downloads path: selection is upstream-trust plus a single sort key, not a verified improvement over the current quality.
findings.evidence: radarr.ts:546-554 filters only on !rejected && size<=cap && fits-free-space then '.sort((a,b)=>b.qualityWeight-a.qualityWeight)'; comment 495-501 admits it reuses the same filter chain and 'Radarr's import logic then decides whether to replace'.
findings.recommendation: Compare best.qualityWeight against the current movieFile quality before grabbing and skip when it isn't a strict improvement, so the upgrade endpoint can't burn a grab on a lateral release.

findings.title: Discord webhook is host-regex-validated only; egress is delegated to *arr so ssrfGuard never applies
findings.description: The webhook is validated only by ^https://(discord.com|discordapp.com)/api/webhooks/. That host allowlist is reasonable and the route is admin-only, so this is low severity, but the actual outbound POST is performed by Sonarr/Radarr (the dashboard only configures them), so server/services/ssrfGuard.ts isPublicHttpsUpstream is never applied to this URL — there is no defense-in-depth if the regex is ever loosened to support generic webhooks.
findings.evidence: notifications.ts:201 'if (!/^https:\/\/(discord\.com|discordapp\.com)\/api\/webhooks\//.test(url))'; isPublicHttpsUpstream from ssrfGuard.ts is not imported in notifications.ts.
findings.recommendation: Keep the host allowlist; if generic webhooks are ever added, route the URL through isPublicHttpsUpstream first and document that egress is delegated to *arr.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: This subsystem is NOT a Plex-equivalent capability — Plex itself has no built-in usenet/torrent acquisition layer; this *arr/SAB bridge is closer to Ombi/Overseerr (request + acquire) bolted onto the household's existing Sonarr/Radarr/SAB. For the stated 'full Plex replacement' goal the relevant points are: (1) the size-cap + curated-profile policy is a thoughtful household-safety feature Plex/Overseerr lack, and is a genuine strength; (2) there is no per-user request quota, approval workflow, or request visibility that Overseerr provides — any authenticated member can add directly, gated only by disk space, which the owner should confirm is intended for a multi-user App-Store deployment; (3) grab events have no user attribution (grabLog.ts:31-51), so unlike Overseerr you cannot answer 'who requested this' for moderation, and /api/grabs/by-item is enumerable. None of these block Plex playback parity (that lives in media-core/transcoder/IPTV). NOTE / correction to an earlier draft: telemetry (Sentry/Glitchtip via index.ts:37) and per-user Claude cost logging (suggestions.ts → appendUsageEvent) ARE actually wired and functional — the observability table-stakes for opening this to scale exist; the remaining scale risks are rate limiting, the in-memory disk-reservation races, and the JSONL stores.

dossiers.purpose: Authentication and authorization for the invite-only platform. Four identity providers converge on one shared allowlist gate: Plex PIN OAuth (plex.ts + auth.ts), Sign-in-with-Apple identity-token verification (appleAuth.ts), device-token JWE bearer auth for native clients (session.ts mint/verify + middleware/deviceTokenAuth.ts + reconcileDeviceToken.ts), and WebAuthn passkeys (webauthn.ts + routes/passkey.ts). Sessions are stateless JWE cookies (session.ts). AuthZ is enforced via requireAuth/requireAdmin (middleware/auth.ts) backed by per-request reconcileSession (sessionGate.ts: role recompute + periodic Plex liveness probe + cascade device revocation). CSRF is an Origin-header gate (middleware/csrf.ts) mounted globally in app.ts. The members/invites allowlist (members.ts/membership.ts/invites.ts) plus ADMIN_SUBS owner-bootstrap and PLEX_SERVER_ID scoping (env.ts) decide access. sub.ts is the canonical namespace parser; secrets.ts/keyDerivation.ts handle secret strength + HKDF; tokenReplayCache.ts backs the stream-token replay defense consumed by routes/iptv.ts.
files_reviewed: cross-file caller sweeps: checkReplay (-> routes/iptv.ts:578/803/1003) and @sentry (-> index.ts:15/37)
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The crypto/identity core is genuinely strong and mostly production-grade (JWE sessions, Apple RS256+aud+iss+nonce pinning, HKDF key separation, atomic race-safe invite redemption, fail-closed prod env, globally-mounted CSRF, Sentry telemetry IS initialized, replay cache IS wired into iptv). Held at 'functional' not 'production-grade' because of real per-process-scale limits (rate limiter and revocation/membership caches are single-replica only, contradicting the App-Store-scale goal) plus latent correctness gaps (guest-role laundering, device-token role never re-derived so admin demotion lags 180 days on paired devices, and Hono handler errors are not forwarded to Sentry).
what_works: env.ts fails CLOSED in prod on every dangerous default: throws if ALLOWED_ORIGINS empty (env.ts:103-108), if PLEX_SERVER_ID unset without explicit ALLOW_UNSCOPED_PLEX_LOGIN=1 (env.ts:121-131), if SESSION_SECRET is a known placeholder or <32 chars (env.ts:176-197), and if EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN missing (env.ts:267-274). No `?? 'devsecret'` signing-secret fallback reaches prod.

Secret hygiene: STREAM/DEVICE/INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL secrets each strength-validated and asserted pairwise-distinct at boot in ALL envs (env.ts:221-260, secrets.ts:72-102) so copy-paste reuse is caught before CI.

Apple SIWA verifier is textbook: jose jwtVerify with algorithms pinned to ['RS256'] (blocks alg=none/HS-RS confusion), issuer pinned to appleid.apple.com, audience pinned to APPLE_CLIENT_ID, 60s clock tolerance, constant-time nonce compare, sub taken ONLY from the verified payload and re-validated via parseSub, fail-closed when aud unconfigured, JWKS URL hardcoded (no SSRF), jwks_unavailable mapped to 5xx not 401 (appleAuth.ts:125-217).

Telemetry IS wired: index.ts:15,37 imports @sentry/node and calls Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, release, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, onUnhandledRejectionIntegration }). Prod boot hard-fails without the DSN; a PII-scrub beforeSend is attached — satisfying the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract at the SDK-init level.

Stream-token replay defense IS wired: routes/iptv.ts:26 imports checkReplay and calls it on live (578), remux (803), and segment (1003) tokens; tokenReplayCache.ts enforces segment strict-single-use and multi-use-until-exp with a self-starting GC sweep.

Legacy SHA-256 session-key fallback has a CODE-ENFORCED sunset: index.ts:60-68 refuses to boot on/after 2026-06-25 (process.exit(1)), so the deprecated-crypto path cannot silently outlive its grace window — not merely a TODO.

Session cookie is a real JWE (A256GCM/dir) not signed-only, so the embedded plexAuthToken is encrypted at rest (session.ts:35,351-361); HKDF key with info-string domain separation (keyDerivation.ts:56-88).

Device-token verify is defense-in-depth: decrypt via the Rust contracts crate, aud/iss/role/authMode shape checks, DB-row-exists-and-not-expired check AND revocation-table check (session.ts:285-309); supports revocation despite stateless JWE.

Bearer auth deliberately does NOT fall back to cookie when a Bearer header is present-but-invalid (deviceTokenAuth.ts:57-60, middleware/auth.ts:42-47) — a stolen cookie can't bypass a freshly-revoked device token. Explicit nbf/exp skew re-check (deviceTokenAuth.ts:65-69).

AuthZ decoupled from Plex identity: all four login paths converge on the single authorizeOrRedeem/memberStatus allowlist gate (auth.ts:237-249; passkey.ts:79; sessionGate.ts:132-139). Passkey registration runs authZ BETWEEN attestation-verify and persistCredential so a bad invite leaves no orphan credential (passkey.ts:75-87).

Invite redemption is atomic and race-safe: IMMEDIATE sqlite transaction with a `used_count < max_uses` guarded UPDATE and an ExhaustedRace rollback so two concurrent redeems can't over-spend a single-use code (invites.ts:145-241); 128-bit codes, only sha256(code) persisted, plaintext shown once.

WebAuthn challenges are single-use via a fetch+delete transaction with TTL (webauthn.ts:90-108), signCount is bumped on every login (webauthn.ts:245-247), RP ID + expectedOrigin bound to env, simplewebauthn enforces counter regression.
