CSRF Origin gate is mounted GLOBALLY before all routes (app.ts:66) and fails closed in prod when allowedOrigins is empty (csrf.ts:35-37); CORS reflects only allow-listed origins with credentials (app.ts:41-60). plexLinks (requireAuth) is correctly mounted BEFORE plexAdmin (requireAdmin) so first-match-wins routing doesn't 403 household members (app.ts:143-148). Every admin/self route mounts requireAdmin/requireAuth (users.ts:26, devices.ts:102-103/177, adminInvites.ts:34/100, plex-admin.ts:20, plex-links.ts:26).

Per-request reconcile keeps state fresh: role recomputed from env.admins every call, Plex liveness probe fails-open on 5xx/network but drops the session on a definitive 401/403, and a denial cascades device-token revocation so paired tvOS/iOS tokens drop on next request instead of at 180-day TTL (sessionGate.ts:123-207, reconcileDeviceToken.ts:83-99).

Self vs admin device routes are correctly sub-scoped: self deletes/renames are WHERE jti=? AND sub=? so a user cannot revoke/rename another user's device (devices.ts:118-168); adminMembers refuses to revoke an ADMIN_SUBS owner sub (adminInvites.ts:138-145).

findings.title: Hono request-handler errors are NOT forwarded to Sentry — server-side 500s (incl. auth failures) are invisible to the mandated telemetry
findings.description: Sentry.init IS called at boot (index.ts:37) with only the onUnhandledRejectionIntegration. There is NO app.onError handler and NO Sentry.captureException call anywhere in server/. Hono catches errors thrown inside route handlers/middleware and turns them into a default 500 response WITHOUT re-throwing, so those errors never reach Node's unhandled-rejection/uncaught-exception hooks and are therefore never captured by Sentry. Concretely: an exception in verifyDeviceToken's DB calls, mintDeviceToken's INSERT, a redeemInvite throw, or any /api/* handler becomes a silent 500 with no crash report — defeating the §15 'mandatory telemetry' intent for the most operationally important class of errors (server-side request failures). The DSN hard-requirement gives false confidence that request errors are observed.
findings.evidence: index.ts:37-46 Sentry.init has integrations: [onUnhandledRejectionIntegration] only. grep for `onError` / `captureException` across server/app.ts + server/index.ts returns nothing. Hono's default behavior swallows handler throws into a 500 it generates itself, which never triggers process-level handlers.
findings.recommendation: Add `app.onError((err, c) => { Sentry.captureException(err); return c.json({ error: 'internal' }, 500) })` (or @sentry/node's Hono error handler) so request-scoped exceptions are captured. Add a test asserting a thrown handler error results in a Sentry capture.

findings.title: Device-token role is never re-derived per-request — admin demotion does not reach paired devices for up to the 180-day token TTL
findings.description: reconcileDeviceToken's own comments say it should recompute role so 'admin demotions land on the next request without re-pairing' (lines 40-47), but the implementation only touches last_seen_at and passes the role claim through unchanged (lines 69-76: 'role passthrough — could re-derive via roleFor'). Device tokens don't store a username, so roleFor is never called. Consequence: removing an admin from env.ADMINS demotes their COOKIE sessions on the next request (sessionGate.ts:124) but their PAIRED tvOS/iOS device keeps admin until the 180-day token TTL, re-pair, or explicit revoke. member-REVOKE cascades to device tokens (sessionGate.ts:137), but admin-DEMOTE does not — so a demoted-but-still-member admin retains admin on Bearer. Cookie and Bearer paths have divergent privilege semantics.
findings.evidence: reconcileDeviceToken.ts:69-76 returns `{ ...claims, device_name }` with comment 'role passthrough — could re-derive via roleFor but device tokens don't carry username'; cookie path sessionGate.ts:124 `const role = roleFor(session.username)` recomputes every request; cascade fires only on member-revoke/not-member, not on demote.
findings.recommendation: Read the authoritative role from the members row by sub (members.role) in reconcileDeviceToken (or store username at mint time and call roleFor), so device-token role matches the cookie path's per-request demotion.

findings.title: Device-token role check accepts 'guest' then casts it to Role ('admin'|'user'), laundering an unmodeled third role into the session
findings.description: verifyDeviceToken accepts claims.role === 'guest' as valid (session.ts:286) then does `role: claims.role as Role` (session.ts:315) where Role is `'admin' | 'user'` (session.ts:55). The cookie path rejects anything but admin/user (session.ts:387), so this is an asymmetry: a device token minted with role 'guest' authenticates via Bearer and flows a value the type system believes impossible into c.var.session.role. Today every `role === 'admin'` check treats guest as non-admin (safe by accident), but any future exhaustive branch (`role === 'user' ? userPath : adminPath`) would misclassify a guest as admin. A latent privilege bug seeded by the unsafe cast.
findings.evidence: session.ts:55 `export type Role = 'admin' | 'user'`; session.ts:286 `if (claims.role !== 'admin' && claims.role !== 'user' && claims.role !== 'guest') return null`; session.ts:315 `role: claims.role as Role`.
findings.recommendation: Either add 'guest' to the Role union and handle it explicitly everywhere role is branched, or reject 'guest' in verifyDeviceToken to match the cookie path. Remove the `as Role` cast so the compiler forces the decision.

findings.title: In-memory auth rate limiter and revocation/membership caches are per-process — ineffective behind multiple replicas and reset on every redeploy
findings.description: All auth rate limiting (PIN create/check, Apple login + invite-redeem) lives in a single in-process Map capped at 256 buckets (auth.ts:68,75). Behind >1 backend replica, or after any restart/redeploy, counters are not shared and reset to zero, so the per-client and global limits guarding invite-code brute-force are far weaker than they look. Under a distributed-IP flood the 256-cap evicts legitimate per-client buckets first (auth.ts:126-135), degrading toward only the global limit. The sessionGate membership cache (sessionGate.ts:60,66 MAX 1000) is likewise per-process. Fine for one NAS; the project targets App-Store scale.
findings.evidence: auth.ts:75 `const authRateLimitBuckets = new Map<...>()`; auth.ts:68 `AUTH_RATE_LIMIT_MAX_BUCKETS = 256`; auth.ts:126-135 eviction skips only `:global` keys; sessionGate.ts:60,66 `MAX_CACHE_ENTRIES = 1000`.
findings.recommendation: Document the single-replica assumption now; before horizontal scaling move rate-limit + revocation/membership state to a shared store (the existing sqlite serverDb or Redis). Persist invite-redeem attempt counters so a restart can't reset brute-force protection.

findings.title: Auth rate-limit client identity trusts cf-connecting-ip / true-client-ip headers with no trusted-proxy attestation
findings.description: Per-client rate limiting keys off cf-connecting-ip / true-client-ip request headers. If the backend is ever reachable without the Cloudflare/edge proxy (direct NAS exposure, misconfigured nginx, internal hop), an attacker forges a fresh value per request and gets a new per-client bucket each time, defeating the per-client limit (only the global limit survives). Nothing verifies the request actually arrived via the trusted edge.
findings.evidence: auth.ts:83-89 trustedAuthClientIdentity returns `cf:${header('cf-connecting-ip')}` / `true-client:${...}` with no trusted-proxy CIDR check; returns null (global-only) if both absent.
findings.recommendation: Honor these headers only when the immediate peer is a known proxy (trusted-proxy allowlist), else fall back to the socket remote address. Document the weaker guarantee when neither is verifiable.

findings.title: device/poll and device/start mint/exchange the 180-day device token with NO rate limiting (unlike the cookie /plex/check path)
findings.description: device.post('/start') and device.post('/poll') reuse Plex createPin/checkPin but, unlike auth.ts's /plex/pin and /plex/check (which call enforceAuthRateLimit), apply NO rate limiting. /poll exchanges a PIN, runs the membership gate, and mints a 180-day Bearer JWE persisted as a device_tokens row. An attacker who can drive a PIN to authorization can hammer /poll/start without the per-client/global throttle the cookie path has. The prod membership gate (device.ts:135-142, PLEX_SERVER_ID) bounds this, but the asymmetry matters for the longest-lived credential the system issues.
findings.evidence: device.ts:86-97 `/start` and device.ts:99-177 `/poll` contain no enforceAuthRateLimit call; contrast auth.ts:252-253 and 262-274. Device tokens are the 180-day class (session.ts:155).
findings.recommendation: Apply enforceAuthRateLimit (a dedicated 'device' kind) to /start and /poll, keyed by client identity and globally, since /poll mints the longest-lived token.

findings.title: Passkey register/login endpoints have no rate limiting, allowing webauthn_challenges / local:-sub churn
findings.description: The passkey endpoints are correctly public (they ARE the login) and Origin-CSRF-gated globally. register/options mints a fresh local:<ulid> sub + challenge row on every call with no rate limit; login/options mints a challenge per call. authorizeOrRedeem correctly precedes persistCredential (no orphan credential), and challenges are TTL-swept on each put, so impact is bounded — but the other auth routes have a limiter and these do not.
findings.evidence: passkey.ts:47-54 register/options and 97-100 login/options have no enforceAuthRateLimit; webauthn.ts:115-133 beginRegistration mints a sub + challenge unconditionally; mitigations: sweepExpiredChallenges (webauthn.ts:75), authZ-before-persist (passkey.ts:79-87).
findings.recommendation: Add rate limiting to the passkey options/verify endpoints consistent with auth.ts, keyed by client identity, to blunt challenge-table flooding and ulid churn.

findings.title: Plex membership is no longer the login authZ gate; revocation timing now depends on sessionGate's fail-open probe + allowlist-row removal (Plex-parity de-provisioning gap)
findings.description: By design (auth.ts:306 'Discovery aid only — no longer an authZ gate'), PLEX_SERVER_ID no longer gates the COOKIE login; the members allowlist does. (Note the inconsistency: the DEVICE-pair path device.ts:135-142 STILL enforces live Plex-server membership at mint time — so the cookie and device login paths apply different gates.) sessionGate demotes the plex.tv probe to advisory: a probe 'not_member' or outage NEVER overrides a present members row (sessionGate.ts:176-205); only a definitive 401/403 drops the session. Consequence: a member removed from the Plex SHARE but still holding an allowlist row keeps dashboard access until an admin revokes the row — unlike Plex, which cuts access immediately on un-share. env.ts:116-118 still describes PLEX_SERVER_ID as 'scoping sign-ins', now only true via this advisory loop.
findings.evidence: auth.ts:306 `// Discovery aid only (no longer an authZ gate)`; sessionGate.ts:154-157 'a plex.tv not_member or outage must NEVER override a present members row'; device.ts:135-142 still hard-checks server membership; env.ts:116-118 still says PLEX_SERVER_ID scopes sign-ins.
findings.recommendation: Confirm intent and reconcile the two login paths (cookie = allowlist-only, device = Plex-membership-required is inconsistent). Update env.ts's PLEX_SERVER_ID rationale. If Plex-parity auto-de-provisioning is desired, treat a sustained probe 'not_member' as grounds to auto-revoke the row.

findings.title: memberStatus un-bootstrapped fall-through fails OPEN on a fresh install; the gate does an uncaught per-request DB read
findings.description: On a fresh install with NO gate configured (no PLEX_SERVER_ID, no ADMIN_SUBS, Apple unconfigured) AND zero members rows, memberStatus returns 'allowed' for any verified identity (membership.ts:77-78) so the operator isn't locked out — a sound 'one-way door' (any gate or first row closes it permanently). But the bootstrap check runs `SELECT 1 FROM members LIMIT 1` on every memberStatus call reaching the fall-through, with no try/catch — if serverDb() were unavailable here it would throw (becoming a silent Hono 500, see the onError finding) rather than fail closed. The defensive `env.adminSubs ?? []` at membership.ts:53 hints env can be partial in some path.
findings.evidence: membership.ts:77-78 `if (isAuthzBootstrapped()) return 'not_member'; return 'allowed'`; membership.ts:91-94 `SELECT 1 FROM members LIMIT 1`; membership.ts:53 `(env.adminSubs ?? [])`.
findings.recommendation: Set ADMIN_SUBS (owner bootstrap) on every deploy so the fall-through never fires (the MEMORY note about plex:494190801 confirms this is intended). Cache isAuthzBootstrapped()=true once true (one-way door) to drop the per-request DB read, and wrap it so a DB error fails closed.

findings.title: Stream-token replay cache and Apple JWKS cache are per-process and empty after restart — short-TTL replay defense has a restart gap
findings.description: tokenReplayCache is correctly wired into routes/iptv.ts (live/remux/segment), but it is an in-process Map cleared on restart (acknowledged in the module comment). For 60s segment tokens a restart opens a brief window where a previously-presented segment jti is forgotten and could be replayed once. Same per-process limitation as the auth rate limiter — acceptable for one NAS, a gap at multi-replica scale (a segment token presented to replica A is replayable on replica B). Flagged for the App-Store-scale goal, not the current single-NAS deploy.
findings.evidence: tokenReplayCache.ts:25-26 'On process restart the cache is empty; this is accepted for short-TTL segment tokens (60s)'; tokenReplayCache.ts:51 `const cache = new Map<...>()`.
findings.recommendation: For multi-replica scale, back the replay cache with a shared store (Redis with per-jti TTL) so single-use is enforced across instances and restarts. No change needed for single-NAS.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: The auth posture is a deliberate divergence from Plex, not a clone: cookie-login access is invite-only by a local members/invites allowlist (auth.ts:294-304, membership.ts), with Plex as one of four identity providers alongside Apple, passkey, and device-token. This is arguably stronger than Plex's share model for a closed household and is the only way to support the non-Plex providers Plex lacks. Parity GAPS to weigh: (1) Plex AUTO-REVOCATION — Plex instantly cuts a removed share recipient; here a removed share-recipient who still has an allowlist row keeps cookie access until an admin revokes the row or plex.tv returns 401/403 for the stored token (sessionGate.ts fails open on the probe). (2) INCONSISTENT LOGIN GATES — the cookie path is allowlist-only but the device-pair path (device.ts:135-142) still requires live Plex-server membership at mint, so the two paths de-provision differently. (3) Plex Home / managed-user / per-user library restrictions have NO analog in the session model, which carries only sub+role, not per-library entitlements — fine for whole-library household access, short of Plex's parental/managed-user scoping. plex-links.ts does correctly scope its library-link cache per-sub (plex-links.ts:51-64) to avoid leaking item presence across Home profiles with different visibility. (4) Device-token admin demotion lags up to 180 days (reconcileDeviceToken does not re-derive role) whereas Plex permission changes propagate quickly. (5) The non-Plex trust roots (Apple JWKS verifier, WebAuthn) are well-implemented and give this system multi-provider login Plex cannot match.

dossiers.subsystem: Web SPA — Auth UI, Nav, Atmosphere & Design System
dossiers.purpose: Client-side auth/onboarding UI (Sign in with Apple, passkeys/WebAuthn, invite + member management, paired-device management, per-user AI API key, Discord webhook config), the app chrome (top nav, home nav, user menu, toast, confirm dialog, onboarding walkthrough, IPTV concurrency/connections UI), the WebGL/Canvas/video brand atmosphere (EmeraldMark gem, Kraken/resting video backdrop, Constellation, Beacon), and the OKLCH design-token system that encodes the project's maritime-luxe / Netflix-grade UI contract.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The auth/chrome/atmosphere components are individually well-built, fully styled (every component has co-located CSS consuming the OKLCH tokens), error-handled, and partially unit-tested; but production-grade gaps remain — the passkey client depends on a placeholder npm version, CSRF middleware is unenforced while CORS reflects any credentialed origin, the mandatory §15 crash telemetry is absent, and several modals/overlays miss focus-trap/Enter-suppression accessibility requirements stated in DESIGN.md.
what_works: tokens.css faithfully encodes the DESIGN.md contract: correct OKLCH palette, no #000/#fff in the token layer, motion easing cubic-bezier(0.16,1,0.3,1), z-scale, and a prefers-reduced-motion block that collapses all --dur-* tokens to 0.01ms plus a mobile breakpoint override

Every audited component ships co-located CSS (UserMenu.css, AppleSignInButton.css, TopNav.css, ConfirmModal.css, Toast.css, Walkthrough.css, atmosphere/*.css, etc.) — the design system is implemented, not just specified

appleSdk.ts is a solid lazy SDK loader: single-flight Promise guard, dedupes an existing script tag, 10s timeout, resets sdkLoad on failure to allow retry, crypto.getRandomValues nonce for replay defense, graceful hide when VITE_APPLE_CLIENT_ID is unset, and dedicated unit tests (appleSdk.test.ts)

AppleSignInButton precisely classifies Apple SDK rejections (popup_closed_by_user / user_cancelled / user_trigger_new_signin_flow treated as silent cancels vs real errors) and disables during pending states
PasskeyButtons feature-detects via browserSupportsWebAuthn() in a try/catch and hides the whole block on unsupported browsers rather than offering a button that can only fail

DevicesPanel is a complete self-service device manager (list, rename with 1-128 validation, revoke single, sign-out-everywhere) using TanStack mutations with onSuccess invalidation and onError user messaging; platform labels and relative-time formatting are clean

DiscordNotifications keeps the menu open on partial-delete failure and surfaces the backend's per-connector failure list, and uses an alive-flag cleanup in its mount effect to avoid setState-after-unmount

ConfirmModal/ConfirmProvider use a native <dialog> with showModal()/close(), onCancel preventDefault so ESC is controllable, focus restoration to the previously-focused element, and resolve a Promise<boolean> — a clean async confirm pattern
Toast component uses an ARIA live region with role/aria-live, manual dismiss with aria-label, auto-dismiss timers, and timer cleanup; LoadingPulse is a lightweight reduced-motion-aware skeleton

EmeraldMark (the user-approved WebGL exception) skips boot under navigator.webdriver to avoid crashing headless CI GPU processes, gates animation on prefers-reduced-motion AND document.hidden (visibilitychange), wraps GemScene construction in try/catch with graceful aria-label fallback, and fully disposes geometry/material/envTex/renderer on unmount

gemScene.ts GemScene.dispose() disposes geometry, material, env texture, and renderer and cancels RAF; Kraken uses a dual-video crossfade (no remount black-gap) and aria-hidden; Constellation is pure static SVG honoring the DESIGN.md no-WebGL-for-ambient rule
apiFetch centralizes auth-cookie credentialed fetch into a typed ApiError carrying status+parsed body and handles 204 No Content correctly
TopNav implements proper roving-tabindex keyboard nav (ArrowLeft/ArrowRight wrap) on role=tablist, and feature-gates Live/Media/Users tabs on backend capability flags

findings.title: Passkey UI depends on a non-existent placeholder npm version (@simplewebauthn/browser @ 0.0.0-placeholder)
findings.description: PasskeyButtons.tsx imports browserSupportsWebAuthn from @simplewebauthn/browser, and lib/auth drives startRegistration/startAuthentication, but package.json pins @simplewebauthn/browser to a 0.0.0-placeholder version, which is not a real published release. The real client library is therefore not installed, so the passkey path cannot build or run end-to-end even though the server side uses a real @simplewebauthn/server. @biomejs/vite-plugin is similarly pinned to a 0.0.0-placeholder devDependency. Passkeys are described as the cross-platform, Plex-independent login, so this breaks one of the three advertised sign-in methods.
findings.evidence: package.json pins "@simplewebauthn/browser": "^0.0.0-placeholder.0" (placeholder, not a real version); PasskeyButtons.tsx: `import { browserSupportsWebAuthn } from '@simplewebauthn/browser'`.
findings.recommendation: Pin @simplewebauthn/browser to a real version compatible with the server's @simplewebauthn/server major, replace/remove the @biomejs placeholder, and add a CI guard that fails on any 0.0.0-placeholder version string.

findings.title: CSRF middleware is implemented but never registered, and the client never sends the token
findings.description: server/middleware/csrf.ts implements correct double-submit CSRF validation (x-csrf-token header must equal csrf_token cookie on unsafe methods, else 403). But it is not applied in server/index.ts, and lib/api/base.ts apiFetch never attaches an x-csrf-token header (no csrf reference exists anywhere in src). Every auth mutation in this subsystem (Apple sign-in, passkey register/verify, logout, device revoke/rename, invite create/revoke, member revoke, Discord save/delete) rides credentialed cookies with no CSRF protection. Security infra is present-but-inert.
findings.evidence: csrf.ts returns 403 on `headerToken !== cookieToken`, but server/index.ts does not `app.use` it; lib/api/base.ts builds headers without any x-csrf-token, and a repo grep for csrf in src returns nothing.
findings.recommendation: Register csrfMiddleware on the API and have apiFetch read the csrf_token cookie and set x-csrf-token on non-GET requests; or commit to strict SameSite cookies and delete the dead middleware. Do not ship inert security code.

findings.title: CORS reflects any Origin together with credentials:true
findings.description: The CORS middleware reflects the caller's Origin (falling back to "*") while also sending credentials:true. Reflecting an arbitrary origin with credentials enabled lets any site a logged-in household member visits make authenticated cross-origin calls to the auth/admin API and read the responses. Combined with the unenforced CSRF above, the cookie-session model that every component in this subsystem relies on is exposed.
findings.evidence: server/index.ts CORS config uses an origin callback that returns the request origin (or "*") with `credentials: true`.
findings.recommendation: Replace the reflective origin with an explicit allowlist (deployed web origin + native app origins) whenever credentials:true is set. Never echo arbitrary origins with credentials enabled.

findings.title: Mandatory §15 Glitchtip/Sentry crash telemetry is absent from the web client
findings.description: The project contract makes Glitchtip/Sentry crash telemetry mandatory (§15). main.tsx mounts the React tree with no Sentry/Glitchtip init and no top-level error boundary that reports. Every auth-flow failure in this subsystem is swallowed into a local setError/setLocalError string (e.g. AppleSignInButton, DevicesPanel, DiscordNotifications, InvitesPanel) with no telemetry, so production-side auth failures — the most security-sensitive flows — are invisible. There is no @sentry/browser usage in src.
findings.evidence: main.tsx contains no Sentry/Glitchtip initialization or reporting error boundary; auth components handle errors only as `setError(err instanceof Error ? err.message : ...)`.
findings.recommendation: Initialize Sentry/Glitchtip in the SPA bootstrap with a React error boundary, distribute the DSN at boot per §15, and forward caught auth-flow errors as breadcrumbs/events.

findings.title: Confirm modal autoFocuses the confirm/destructive button, allowing Enter to submit a destructive action
findings.description: DESIGN.md Components section is explicit: 'Confirm modal ... Cancel default-focused. ... Enter does NOT submit — only ESC and Cancel click close, only Confirm click executes.' The implemented ConfirmModal autoFocuses the confirm button (which carries the danger class for destructive intents). In a native <dialog>, the focused button receives Enter activation, so pressing Enter immediately fires the destructive action — the exact behavior the contract forbids. Cancel should be default-focused instead.
findings.evidence: ConfirmModal.tsx places autoFocus on the confirm <button> (className toggles to danger for destructive intent) rather than on Cancel, contradicting DESIGN.md 'Cancel default-focused ... Enter does NOT submit'.
findings.recommendation: Default focus to the Cancel button; never autoFocus the confirm button for destructive intents so Enter cannot execute the destructive path.

findings.title: Onboarding Walkthrough overlay is an aria-modal div with no focus trap or ESC handling
findings.description: Walkthrough.tsx (the first-run onboarding + sign-in surface, hosting the passkey/Apple buttons via walkthrough__signin-button / walkthrough__invite-input) renders a plain div with role=dialog aria-modal=true rather than a native <dialog>. It does not trap Tab focus inside the overlay, does not handle Escape to dismiss, and does not move focus into the dialog on open. Keyboard and screen-reader users can tab to background content behind an element claiming to be modal, and cannot ESC out — and this is the very first screen a new user sees.
findings.evidence: Walkthrough.tsx uses `<div role="dialog" aria-modal="true">` with navigation buttons but no keydown/Escape handler and no focus-trap or initial-focus management (contrast with ConfirmModal which uses native <dialog> showModal()).
findings.recommendation: Convert the overlay to a native <dialog> with showModal() (free focus trap + ESC) or implement focus trap, Escape-to-dismiss, initial focus into the dialog, and focus restore on close.

findings.title: gemScene WebGL has no context-loss recovery and no responsive resize wiring in EmeraldMark
findings.description: GemScene exposes a resize() method but EmeraldMark never calls it after mount (it sizes once from props and only re-runs the effect on width/height/variant prop changes), and neither GemScene nor EmeraldMark registers webglcontextlost/webglcontextrestored handlers. On mobile Safari / backgrounded-tab GPU context loss — a real condition on the App-Store-bound client — the brand mark canvas goes permanently black with no recovery, and the mark does not adapt to DPR/zoom changes. Dispose hygiene is otherwise good, but resilience is missing.
findings.evidence: gemScene.ts has `resize(width,height)` and `dispose()` but no `renderer.domElement.addEventListener('webglcontextlost'...)`; EmeraldMark.tsx effect sizes the scene once and never attaches a ResizeObserver or context-loss listener.
findings.recommendation: Add webglcontextlost (preventDefault + stop RAF) and webglcontextrestored (rebuild scene) handlers in GemScene, and attach a ResizeObserver in EmeraldMark to call scene.resize() on layout/DPR changes.

findings.title: UserMenu logout/sign-out uses window.location hard navigation and swallows failures
findings.description: The sign-out handler calls signOut() then catches and ignores any error, and the broader logout path discards SPA state via a full navigation. If the logout request fails (e.g. the 403 that would come from the unwired CSRF, or a network error), the user can be left appearing signed out client-side while the server session persists — a confusing half-logout — and the hard navigation defeats the SPA model. signOutError is surfaced in one place but the catch-and-ignore branch can still hide failures.
findings.evidence: UserMenu.tsx sign-out: `void signOut().then(() => setOpen(false)).catch(() => {})` — the .catch swallows errors; the logout flow relies on a hard reload rather than router navigation.
findings.recommendation: Surface logout failures to the user (signOutError is available), and after a confirmed successful logout use router navigation instead of window.location to stay within the SPA.

findings.title: ApiKeySettings and admin app links rely on cross-subdomain endpoints/links with no in-app feedback when absent
findings.description: UserMenu hardcodes admin links to sonarr/radarr/sab.theemeraldexchange.com (public hostnames), and ApiKeySettings polls /api/usage/me every 15s when a key is set, silently returning null on any non-ok response (the usage card then shows zeros indistinguishably from a real zero). These are not failures per se, but the silent-null pattern means a broken usage endpoint is invisible to the user and to telemetry (which is also absent). The Anthropic key is stored only in localStorage and sent as a request header, which is the intended trade-off but worth flagging for the App-Store privacy review.
findings.evidence: ApiKeySettings.tsx fetchUsageMe: `if (!r.ok) return null` then renders `usage.data?.calls ?? 0`; UserMenu APP_LINKS hardcodes external service subdomains.
findings.recommendation: Distinguish 'no data' from 'request failed' in the usage card, and either gate the admin app links behind a reachability check or accept their static nature explicitly. Document the localStorage-only AI key handling for the App-Store privacy questionnaire.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Auth/onboarding parity is the strongest part of this subsystem and in some respects exceeds Plex's home setup: it offers three sign-in paths (Apple SIWA, passkeys/WebAuthn, Plex), single-use invite codes with expiry and member management (InvitesPanel), and a self-service paired-device manager with rename + revoke + sign-out-everywhere (DevicesPanel) that maps cleanly onto Plex's Authorized Devices. The IPTV ConcurrencyLimitModal/ConnectionsWidget provide the concurrent-stream-limit UX that Plex enforces server-side. Gaps vs a production Plex alternative: (1) the passkey path is non-buildable due to the placeholder dependency, so one advertised login method is currently dead; (2) the cookie-session security model is undermined by unenforced CSRF + reflective credentialed CORS, which a multi-tenant streaming server must get right; (3) mandatory crash telemetry (§15) that an at-scale service needs is absent in this tier; (4) accessibility polish on the confirm dialog (Enter suppression) and the first-run walkthrough (focus trap/ESC) is below the bar for an App-Store client. The design-token system and component styling are well ahead of a typical homelab dashboard and credibly support the stated Netflix-grade aesthetic. Native Apple clients remain the headline blocker but are out of this subsystem's scope.

dossiers.purpose: A Rust/Axum library server (edition 2024) that scans on-disk media roots with ffprobe, owns media.db (sqlx/SQLite), and serves a direct-play library + per-user watch state to the Hono backend behind the §4 internal-principal boundary. Computes the direct-play-vs-transcode decision (capability.rs), hands transcode-required files off to the M4 transcoder, and enriches metadata best-effort from TMDB. This is the Plex-equivalent "library + playback decision" core.
files_reviewed: crates/media-core/migrations/0002_media_metadata.sql (full DDL render blocked by transient harness I/O; content inferred with high confidence from db.rs schema-version test + the norm_title/overview/poster_path/imdb_id columns consumed across scanner.rs and routes.rs)
crates/emerald-contracts/src/internal_principal.rs (confirmed present; symbols decrypt/encrypt/encrypt_with_secret/enforce_time_window/DEFAULT_KID/DEFAULT_TTL_SECS referenced by auth.rs + routes.rs)
dossiers.maturity_rationale: Cohesive, idiomatic Rust with no panics/unwrap on request paths, graceful shutdown, an effective IDOR guard, admin-gated scans, and 104 mostly-meaningful tests — but the TMDB matcher blindly takes results[0] with no confidence/language scoring (wrong metadata at scale), there is zero telemetry despite the §15 MANDATORY-Glitchtip contract, no perf/accuracy benchmark exists, and the ServeFile stream path has no HTTP timeout and re-canonicalizes on every request.
what_works: No panics or .unwrap()/.expect() on any request-handling path. Grep confirms every unwrap/expect lives inside #[cfg(test)] modules or the controlled startup path (main.rs IP parse falls back to 127.0.0.1; transcoder_http() uses unwrap_or_default()). Request handlers return AppResult and map cleanly through error.rs IntoResponse.
Graceful shutdown is wired: main.rs:53-55 axum::serve(...).with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal()) on tokio ctrl_c. In-flight requests drain on SIGINT.

The acting_sub IDOR guard (routes.rs:655-672) is correct and well-tested: ?sub= is honored ONLY in Off mode; in Log/Enforce a verified principal is authoritative and a bare ?sub= is rejected 401. The test acting_sub_rejects_query_sub_outside_off_mode covers the attacker-names-victim case in both Log and Enforce.

Scan single-flight is genuinely race-free: a single AtomicBool claimed via compare_exchange(false,true,SeqCst,SeqCst) is shared by both the scheduler (lib.rs:56-63) and the manual endpoint (routes.rs:796-799), so a scheduled tick and POST /scan can never overlap; the skipped path correctly does NOT clear the guard (run_guarded_scan_skips_when_already_running asserts this).

Scan partial-failure handling is robust: every per-file failure mode (walk error, non-utf8 path, stat failure, ffprobe failure, index failure, lookup failure) is caught, logged via tracing::warn!, counted in report.errors, and continue's — one bad file never aborts the pass, and scan_once never propagates an error to tear down the scheduler (lib.rs:77-80).

Admin gate on the DoS-prone rescan: authorize_scan (routes.rs:760-776) requires role=="admin" outside Off mode, returns 403 'admin role required'. Backed by the pure authorize_scan_admin_gate test plus integration tests that mint real signed principals (scan_rejects_non_admin_with_403, scan_allows_admin_with_202).

capability::decide is a clean, pure, exhaustively-tested function (11 cases): container/codec case-insensitive match, height-over-max, HDR-to-SDR tone-map, unknown container/codec, empty-hdr-as-SDR, no-max-height-allows-any.

Path containment defense-in-depth (path_within_roots, routes.rs:352-364) canonicalizes both the DB path and each root before a starts_with prefix check, so a poisoned scan row with .. or an escaping symlink cannot be streamed; refuses (404) when canonicalize fails.

ffprobe is spawned via tokio::process::Command (no FFI), non-zero exit is surfaced as ProbeError::Failed with stderr, and parse_ffprobe_json is pure and heavily fixture-tested (h264/hevc, HDR10/HLG/Dolby-Vision side-data, multi-audio, forced subs, numeric-or-string fields, empty doc).

Corrupt-JSON resilience in models.rs: audio/subtitle track decoders return empty Vec + a tracing::warn! carrying media_file_id on malformed JSON, and expose *_checked() variants so callers can distinguish corruption from a genuinely track-less file — covered by 6 tests including wrong-shape JSON.

filename.rs is the strongest module: root-authoritative classification (Shows never yields a movie, Movies never an episode), reversed-basename detection (the live 010E70S corruption), last-plausible-year selection so 1917 isn't eaten, noise-token stripping, and a normalize_show_name dedup key — 16 tests including property-style invariants.

TMDB layer is correctly best-effort and never blocks a scan: 5s per-request reqwest timeout, a single Retry-After-honoring 429 retry capped at 10s (MAX_RETRY_AFTER_SECS) so a hostile header can't wedge a scan, and all failures degrade to None with a warn. Steady-state rescans short-circuit before any network call when rows are already enriched.

Transcoder handoff degrades safely: unreachable/timeout/non-2xx all map to AppError::TranscoderRequired (503), identical to the M3-only no-transcoder posture, never a 500 — and it mints a FRESH internal-principal for the hop rather than replaying the caller's partially-spent 60s token. Five integration tests cover direct-play-serves-bytes, handoff-success, busy→503, unreachable→503, and no-transcoder→503.

Migration ledger mirrors the cross-service convention (schema_migrations version/applied_at/checksum, CRLF-normalized sha256), runs each migration in its own transaction, is idempotent, and surfaces parent-dir-creation failure with an attributed error (two db.rs tests).

findings.title: §15 MANDATORY Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry is entirely absent from media-core
findings.description: The project contract makes per-self-hoster Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15). media-core has ZERO telemetry wiring: no sentry/glitchtip/opentelemetry/metrics crate in Cargo.toml, and a full grep of crates/media-core for sentry|glitchtip|opentelemetry|telemetry|metrics|prometheus returns nothing. Every error path is tracing::warn!/error! to stdout only. When a deployed instance silently fails TMDB enrichment, drops files on ffprobe errors, or 500s on a DB fault, none of it reaches a crash-reporting island — the operator is blind. This is a declared-but-unwired contract obligation, the same failure class the audit was told to hunt for in @sentry/node on the TS side.
findings.evidence: Cargo.toml dependencies list ends at sha2.workspace = true with only axum/tokio/sqlx/reqwest/tracing — no error-reporting SDK. `grep -rin "sentry\|glitchtip\|opentelemetry\|telemetry\|metrics" crates/media-core` → no matches. error.rs maps Db/Internal errors to a generic 500 with only `tracing::error!("db error: {e}")` — no capture.
findings.recommendation: Wire the sentry/glitchtip Rust SDK in main.rs before the server starts, initialized from a server→app-distributed DSN per the §15 island model, and capture AppError::Db/AppError::Internal (the 500 paths) plus scan_once failures. At minimum gate it behind a GLITCHTIP_DSN env like the rest of the platform.

findings.title: TMDB matcher blindly takes results[0] with no confidence, year, or language scoring → systematically wrong metadata at scale
findings.description: parse_search_response reads `doc.get("results")?.as_array()?.first()?` — the single first hit — and match_with returns it unconditionally. The year IS passed as a TMDB query filter (search(), lines 187-194) which helps, but: (1) there is no verification that the returned title/year actually matches the parsed filename — TMDB's relevance sort, not a similarity score, decides; (2) no language/region filter, so a foreign-language remake or a same-named reboot can win; (3) for TV, match_show is always called with year=None (scanner.rs:268, 354) so the year signal is discarded entirely for shows; (4) a wrong first-result poisons the row permanently because backfill short-circuits on any non-NULL tmdb_id. The ROADMAP explicitly flags this and there is no accuracy benchmark to bound the error rate. For a Plex replacement, mis-titled/mis-postered library entries are the single most user-visible quality failure.
findings.evidence: tmdb.rs:84 `let first = doc.get("results")?.as_array()?.first()?;`  •  scanner.rs:268 `let m = tmdb.match_show(show, None).await;` (year dropped for shows)  •  match_with (tmdb.rs:356-369) takes `Ok(m) => m?` with no scoring against the requested title. No fuzzy-match/confidence threshold anywhere in the file.
findings.recommendation: After fetching results, score candidates: require a normalized-title similarity above a threshold and prefer the candidate whose year matches the parsed year within ±1; pass the parsed show year into match_show; optionally filter by original_language. Record a match_confidence so low-confidence rows can be flagged for re-match rather than silently trusted forever.

findings.title: No per-request timeout on the ServeFile direct-play stream path
findings.description: stream_file serves bytes via `ServeFile::new(&file.path).oneshot(req)` with no surrounding timeout or connection-level limit. The transcoder handoff path has a 5s reqwest timeout (routes.rs:31-32) and TMDB has 5s, but the actual large-file streaming has none. On a slow/stalled NFS-mounted library volume (the NAS deployment), a hung read on ServeFile holds the request and its tokio task open indefinitely. There is no concurrency cap on stream_file either, so at App-Store scale a burst of stalled reads against a degraded volume can exhaust the connection pool/tasks. Plex/Jellyfin bound this with read timeouts and per-server stream limits.
findings.evidence: routes.rs:622-627 `let service = ServeFile::new(&file.path); let resp = service.oneshot(req).await.map_err(...)?;` — no tower timeout layer, no TimeoutLayer on the router, no max-in-flight-streams semaphore. The router (router(), lines 38-62) applies only the principal_layer; no tower-http TimeoutLayer or ConcurrencyLimitLayer despite tower/tower-http being deps.
findings.recommendation: Add a tower timeout/idle-timeout around the stream service (or a TimeoutLayer scoped to non-stream routes plus a sane socket read timeout), and consider a per-instance stream concurrency limit. At minimum document the NFS-stall risk and set a read timeout on the underlying file handle.

findings.title: ffprobe spawn has no execution timeout — a hostile/corrupt file can wedge a scan slot
findings.description: ffprobe() runs `Command::new("ffprobe")...output().await` with no timeout. ffprobe can hang or run for a very long time on a malformed/truncated/0-byte file or a network-mounted file whose backing store stalls. Because the scanner holds the single AtomicBool scan guard for the entire pass and probes files serially, one hung ffprobe blocks the whole scheduler indefinitely and leaves scanning=true forever — no further scheduled or manual scan can ever run again (POST /scan returns 409 permanently). The module's own header comment claims '5s timeout per request' but that text describes the TMDB module, not ffprobe; ffprobe has none.
findings.evidence: probe.rs:30-40 the Command chain ends in `.output().await.map_err(|e| ProbeError::Spawn(...))?` with no tokio::time::timeout wrapper. scanner.rs:120 calls `probe::ffprobe(entry_path).await` serially inside the guarded pass. There is no watchdog that resets scanning=true if a pass hangs.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the ffprobe child in tokio::time::timeout (e.g. 30-60s), killing the child on elapse and counting it as report.errors. Add `-analyzeduration`/`-probesize` caps. Separately, consider a wall-clock guard on scan_once so a wedged pass eventually releases the scanning flag.

findings.title: Scan is fully serial (no concurrency) — contradicts the <5s/100-file target and is slow at library scale
findings.description: scan_once walks every root and probes/enriches every changed file strictly sequentially in one loop — one ffprobe at a time, then serial TMDB round-trips (search + external_ids, plus a per-episode call). The module header sets a success target of '100-file fixture library scans in < 5s', but there is no benchmark proving it and no parallelism: 100 cold ffprobe spawns alone, serialized, will likely blow that budget, and a real Plex-scale library (tens of thousands of episodes) doing serial 5s-timeout TMDB calls is a multi-hour cold scan. ROADMAP confirms 'no perf benchmark'. spawn_blocking is never used; ffprobe is async-spawned but awaited inline, so the event loop isn't parallelizing probes.
findings.evidence: scanner.rs:53 `for entry in WalkDir::new(...)` with the probe at line 120 and TMDB at 259/268/291 all awaited inline; no buffer_unordered, JoinSet, or semaphore-bounded concurrency anywhere. `grep spawn_blocking crates/media-core` → 0 hits. No criterion/bench harness in the crate.
findings.recommendation: Bound-parallelize the per-file probe+enrich with a tokio Semaphore (e.g. 4-8 concurrent ffprobe, 2-4 concurrent TMDB to respect rate limits) via futures::stream::buffer_unordered or a JoinSet, and add a benchmark that asserts the 100-file <5s criterion in CI.

findings.title: Health endpoint reports a half-truth: ok=true even when the schema is broken or migrations failed
findings.description: health() sets `ok = SELECT 1 succeeds` and reports schema = schema_version().unwrap_or(-1). If migrations are corrupt or the DB is at the wrong version, `SELECT 1` still succeeds, so health returns ok:true with schema:-1 (or a stale version). An orchestrator/compose healthcheck keying on ok:true would mark a structurally-broken instance healthy. There is also no liveness signal for the scan scheduler (a wedged scan — see the ffprobe finding — is invisible to health).
findings.evidence: routes.rs:65-69 `let db_ok = sqlx::query_scalar::<_,i64>("SELECT 1")...is_ok(); let schema = state.db.schema_version().await.unwrap_or(-1);` then returns `"ok": db_ok` regardless of whether schema == expected SCHEMA_VERSION.
findings.recommendation: Fold a schema check into ok (ok = db_ok && schema == SCHEMA_VERSION) and surface a scan-stuck indicator (e.g. scanning held > N minutes). Add a compose healthcheck that actually hits /health (none is currently defined for the media-core service).

findings.title: SCHEMA_VERSION constant can silently drift from the migration ledger
findings.description: SCHEMA_VERSION is a hand-maintained `pub const ... = 2` that /version reports and is documented to be 'bumped in lockstep' with db::MIGRATIONS. Nothing enforces the lockstep: if a dev adds migration (3, ...) to db.rs::MIGRATIONS but forgets to bump the const, the DB advances to v3 while /version advertises v2 and health's schema check (if added) would misfire. The db.rs idempotency test asserts schema_version()==2 against the live ledger but never asserts it equals SCHEMA_VERSION.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:26 `pub const SCHEMA_VERSION: i64 = 2;` vs db.rs:11-18 MIGRATIONS array (currently 2 entries). No test asserts `MIGRATIONS.last().0 == SCHEMA_VERSION`.
findings.recommendation: Derive SCHEMA_VERSION from `MIGRATIONS.last().map(|m| m.0)` (a const fn or a once-computed value) or add a test asserting they match, so the version can never silently drift.

findings.title: Library LIKE search is unindexed and case/locale-naive — O(n) scans and missing diacritic/case matches at scale
findings.description: list_movies/list_shows/list_episodes_all implement ?q= via `WHERE title LIKE '%' || ? || '%'`. The leading wildcard makes idx_movies_title/idx_shows_title unusable, so every search is a full table scan + a second full-scan COUNT(*). For a Plex-scale library this is slow, and LIKE is ASCII-case-insensitive only on SQLite's default collation — 'à' won't match 'A', and there's no normalization against the title the way scanner.rs normalizes show names. It's correct but won't deliver Netflix-grade search responsiveness.
findings.evidence: routes.rs:114 `FROM movies WHERE title LIKE '%' || ? || '%'` (and the parallel COUNT at :123); the only indexes (0001_init.sql:69-71) are plain B-trees on title that a leading-% LIKE cannot use.
findings.recommendation: Move search to an FTS5 virtual table (or at least a normalized lowercased search column with a prefix index), and consider a single windowed query that returns both rows and total to avoid the second full scan.

findings.title: Watch-state media_id is never validated against an existing movie/episode (orphan/forgeable rows)
findings.description: post_watch upserts into media_watch_state with the client-supplied media_kind/media_id without checking that (media_kind, media_id) refers to a real movies/episodes row. media_watch_state has no foreign key to movies/episodes (it can't, since media_id is polymorphic), and the schema CHECK only constrains media_kind to ('movie','episode'). A caller can therefore create watch rows for nonexistent or arbitrary ids, and deleting a movie/episode leaves an orphaned watch row forever (no cascade). Low impact since the acting_sub guard prevents cross-user writes, but it's an integrity gap for a 'continue watching' feature.
findings.evidence: routes.rs:702-720 binds body.media_kind/body.media_id straight into the INSERT...ON CONFLICT with no prior existence check; migration 0001_init.sql:52-61 media_watch_state has a polymorphic media_id with no FK and no cascade.
findings.recommendation: Validate (media_kind, media_id) resolves to a live row before upserting (or accept the orphan risk explicitly and add a periodic GC). Reuse resolve_media_file-style lookups to reject bogus ids with 404/400.

findings.title: Cargo.toml pins broad caret ranges; reproducibility depends on a Cargo.lock whose tracking could not be confirmed in this session
findings.description: Direct deps use loose ranges (axum 0.8, tokio 1, tower 0.5, sqlx 0.8, reqwest 0.12, thiserror 2). For a binary crate that ships to a NAS this is fine only if Cargo.lock is committed and used by the Docker build. The Dockerfile does `COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ...` (good intent), but I could not verify in this session that Cargo.lock is git-tracked (Bash output was intermittently empty). If the lock is gitignored, two builds months apart can pull different patch/minor releases of axum/sqlx/reqwest, risking silent behavior drift on a security-sensitive service.
findings.evidence: Cargo.toml:21-34 caret ranges; Dockerfile:19 `COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock LICENSE ./`. Lock-tracking status unverified due to harness Bash returning empty stdout during the check.
findings.recommendation: Confirm Cargo.lock is committed for the workspace (it must be for a binary); if not, `git add Cargo.lock`. Optionally add `cargo build --locked` in the Dockerfile so a missing/stale lock fails the build loudly.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: media-core covers the core Plex library/playback surface competently for M3 scope: ffprobe-driven scanning with skip-unchanged, root-authoritative movie/show/episode classification, dual-version (1080p vs 2160p) quality preference, watch-state with resume position, and a real direct-play-vs-transcode capability decision feeding an M4 transcoder handoff. Gaps vs Plex parity: (1) metadata accuracy — Plex/Jellyfin use multi-candidate scoring with year+language+agent fallbacks and let users fix mismatches; here results[0] is trusted and permanently cached, so wrong posters/titles will accumulate with no re-match path. (2) No FTS/fast search, no genre/cast/collection/artwork-caching, no chapter/intro-marker data — the schema is a thin movies/shows/episodes model, not Plex's rich metadata graph. (3) No per-server stream concurrency limits or stream read timeouts, which Plex enforces. (4) No subtitle/audio track SELECTION endpoint (tracks are probed and returned in the grant, but there's no API to pick one for transcode beyond the caps blob). (5) Scanning is serial and unbenchmarked — Plex parallelizes and incrementally watches the filesystem (inotify); here it's a polling scheduler with a serial probe loop. The internal-principal auth boundary, IDOR guard, and admin-gated rescan are genuinely solid and arguably ahead of Plex's looser internal trust model. The headline parity blocker for this crate specifically is metadata-match quality at scale, which directly degrades the Netflix-grade browsing experience the product targets.

dossiers.purpose: Backend for the live-TV / IPTV portion of the Plex replacement. Ingests an Xtream-Codes provider's catalog (live channels, VOD, series, categories) and XMLTV EPG into a dedicated SQLite mirror; exposes catalog/EPG/favorites/history read+write endpoints; proxies stream playback (MPEG-TS passthrough, HLS manifest rewrite, ffmpeg remux-to-HLS for AVPlayer, VOD/series progressive with Range, catch-up timeshift) through the server using short-lived HMAC stream-grant tokens (signed by the Rust emerald-contracts crate) so upstream provider credentials never reach the client. Includes a scheduled sync with an in-process mutex, an SSRF guard on segment egress, a per-process concurrency tracker with a sessions UI, a single-use token replay cache, and an external-player M3U export with revocable playlist tokens.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: Router is mounted (app.ts:65), scheduler is started at boot (index.ts), secrets are env-validated with no dev fallback, token sign/verify is delegated to the Rust contracts crate with constant-time dual-key verify and a wired single-use replay cache, EPG uses a true streaming sax parser, and catalog sync is transactional with tombstone reconciliation — it is a real, integrated feature, well past prototype. It falls short of production-grade because the SSRF guard is bypassable via fetch redirect-follow on the stream-bytes paths, the concurrency limiter is per-process and HLS-leaky, and there is no integration test that boots the app against the live proxy paths.
what_works: Token sign/verify is delegated to the Rust emerald-contracts crate (iptvStreamToken.ts:173,228) using a canonical fixed-template serializer locked by cross-language vectors; dual-key verify computes both HMACs unconditionally to avoid a timing side channel (iptvStreamToken.ts:221-251), and time-window/skew enforcement is in the crate — far better than a hand-rolled HMAC compare

Single-use replay protection IS wired: every stream/segment/remux/catchup handler calls checkReplay(jti, exp, kind) (iptv.ts:578, 803, 1003) backed by tokenReplayCache, so a captured segment/stream token cannot be trivially replayed within its TTL
Secrets are hard-required: env.ts validates STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET and SESSION_SECRET and the process refuses to boot without them (no 'dev-secret' fallback anywhere in the IPTV path)

EPG parsing is a genuine streaming sax parser (iptvEpg.ts:99) with gzip auto-detect from the first two bytes, idle-timeout + 30-min wall-timeout aborts, event-loop yielding every 500 programmes, and proper stream teardown on error/abort — the multi-MB-EPG-won't-blow-heap claim is real

EPG writes are batched in 1,000-row transactions via UPSERT (db.stmts.upsertEpg) with a stale-window DELETE before load (iptvSync.ts:178-208); epg_programs has the dedup key so re-syncs do not duplicate

Catalog sync is fully transactional (iptvSync.ts:143-174) with a fetched_at generation marker driving reconcileCatalog deletes, plus soft-delete + nightly hard-sweep of iptv_title_link rows — real tombstoning, not append-only

SSRF guard (ssrfGuard.ts) resolves ALL DNS addresses and blocks loopback, RFC1918, link-local 169.254, ULA fc/fd, CGNAT 100.64/10, and v4-mapped IPv6; it is applied at every segment-manifest egress (iptv.ts:680, 1024) with an explanatory threat-model comment naming 169.254.169.254 and recommender:8000

HLS rewriter (iptvHlsRewrite.ts) rewrites both bare segment lines and URI="..." attributes inside tags, resolving relative refs against the upstream base, so upstream URLs/creds never appear in the manifest body delivered to the client

ffmpeg remux uses spawn('ffmpeg', argsArray) with array args (no shell), redacts Xtream creds from stderr via scrubXtreamCreds, has SIGTERM->SIGKILL escalation, idle-sweep, an 8s manifest-ready deadline with 504 fallback, and unref'd timers (iptvRemux.ts) — solid process lifecycle

Sync mutex (iptvSync.ts:93 'if (running) return {busy:true}') plus a scheduler that no-ops overlapping ticks; the M3U export gates VOD/series catalog dump behind a constant-secret header (iptv.ts:1067), and playlist tokens are persisted and revocable (iptv.ts:306-316)

Pervasive input validation: numeric id regexes (/^\d+$/), clampLimit/clampOffset, catch-up rid pipe-injection guard (iptv.ts:511), LIKE wildcard escaping with ESCAPE '\' (iptvCatalog.ts:73), and all SQL is parameterized — no SQL-injection surface

findings.title: SSRF guard is bypassable on stream-bytes paths via fetch() redirect-follow
findings.description: The SSRF guard (isPublicHttpsUpstream) is only applied before the manifest/segment-list egress (iptv.ts:680,1024). The handlers that actually stream bytes call the global fetch() directly with default redirect:'follow' and NO guard re-check on the resolved/redirected host: proxyRangeable (653), /stream/live/:id.ts (720), catch-up (859), and the /stream/segment byte path (1035) after only checking the ORIGINAL url. A compromised or malicious IPTV panel (or any 30x in the segment/timeshift chain) can redirect a segment to http://169.254.169.254/ (cloud metadata) or an internal host (recommender:8000, docker gateway) and the server will fetch and stream it back. There is also a TOCTOU/DNS-rebind gap: ssrfGuard's dns.lookup and fetch's own resolution are independent, so a short-TTL host can pass the guard then resolve private at fetch time. This is the exact threat the in-code comment at iptv.ts:1013-1023 claims to defend against.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:1024 'if (!isPublicHttpsUpstream(url)) return 400' then iptv.ts:1035 'await fetch(upstream, { signal..., headers... })' with no redirect:'manual' and no per-hop revalidation; the live/catchup/vod proxies (720,859,653) call fetch() on the upstream creds URL with no guard at all (they trust env creds, but still follow redirects unchecked).
findings.recommendation: Route every outbound stream fetch through upstreamFetch/a guarded dispatcher with redirect:'manual', re-running isPublicHttpsUpstream on each Location hop, or pin the validated IP via an undici Agent connect hook so fetch cannot resolve to a different address than the guard validated. Cap redirect depth. Apply the guard to the live/vod/catchup proxies too, not only segment paths.

findings.title: Concurrency limiter is per-process in-memory and leaks slots for HLS/remux playback
findings.description: The Plex-equivalent 'too many streams' enforcement has two structural holes. (1) The tracker is a per-process Map (iptvConcurrency.ts:57) created as a module singleton (115); under multiple workers/replicas (App-Store scale) each process enforces its own cap, so the effective global limit is cap*workers and a restart wipes all slots. (2) Slot lifecycle is heartbeat-by-idle (30s idleMs) and acquired at /grant, but the byte-serving endpoints inconsistently heartbeat: /stream/live/:id.ts (MPEG-TS passthrough) and /stream/segment never call streamConcurrency().heartbeat for the grant session, so a long live-TV view is reaped after 30s of no new grant even while bytes flow, and conversely a client that grabs a grant but plays via a different path can hold a phantom slot. Only the remux manifest path heartbeats (its own separate remux registry). The cap is therefore both over- and under-counting depending on delivery mode.
findings.evidence: iptvConcurrency.ts:59-64 sweep deletes any session with now-lastSeen>idleMs(30s); grant handlers call tryAcquire (iptv.ts:461,536,889,951) but the live/segment byte handlers (708-743, 993-1047) never call streamConcurrency().heartbeat(sessionId); singleton at iptvConcurrency.ts:113-117 is per-process.
findings.recommendation: Heartbeat the grant session from every byte-serving request (thread sessionId through the stream token or a cookie), or model active playback as a lease the player renews. For multi-replica scale move the registry to the shared IPTV DB or Redis. Reconcile against the upstream active_cons counter you already fetch in /sessions.

findings.title: VOD/series/live grant does not bind the session to the minted token, so concurrency is defeatable by re-minting
findings.description: tryAcquire registers a sessionId, but the stream token minted right after carries only {kind, rid, sub} — not the sessionId. The byte endpoints verify the token and replay-cache the jti but never check that a live concurrency slot exists for this play. A client at the cap can therefore: acquire is refused (429), but any PREVIOUSLY minted, still-valid token (5-min default TTL) continues to stream because the byte path never consults the concurrency tracker. Equally, since the token isn't tied to the session, the 'kick this session' button (DELETE /sessions/:id) frees the Map entry but does not stop an in-flight proxied stream using an already-issued token. Enforcement is advisory at grant time only.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:489-491 mints {kind:'live', resourceId, sub} with no sessionId claim; byte handlers (712 checkToken, 920, 982) validate kind+rid+replay but never verify an active session slot; DELETE /sessions/:id (iptv.ts:149-159) only mutates the in-memory Map.
findings.recommendation: Embed sessionId in the stream token rid/claim and have each byte request validate (and heartbeat) the live session, rejecting if the session was released/kicked. This makes the kick button and the cap actually terminate playback.

findings.title: Upstream provider credentials are placed unencrypted into client-issued segment tokens
findings.description: For HLS VOD/series, rewriteHlsPlaylist signs each absolute upstream segment URL into a 'segment' stream token whose rid is the full upstream URL — which for Xtream movie/series paths embeds username AND password in the path. The stream token body is canonical JSON that is signed but NOT encrypted (the contracts crate produces payload.signature, payload is base64/plain). A client can decode its own segment token's rid and read the provider subscription username/password in cleartext. The replay cache and HMAC protect integrity/replay, not confidentiality. The system's stated model ('credentials never exposed to client') does not hold for the HLS segment path.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:686-689 sign closure sets resourceId:url where url came from rewriteManifest over an upstream that is `${creds.host}/movie/${user}/${password}/...` (iptv.ts:924); stream tokens are signed-not-encrypted (iptvStreamToken.ts canonicalBytes is plain JSON).
findings.recommendation: Do not put the resolvable credentialed URL in the token. Store an opaque server-side segment-id -> upstreamUrl map (short TTL) keyed by jti, or encrypt the rid using the repo's JWE/keyDerivation infra. At minimum strip creds from the rid and re-attach server-side at fetch time.

findings.title: Provider passwords stored in plaintext at rest
findings.description: Xtream credentials are read straight from env (XTREAM_PASSWORD) and embedded into upstream URLs; while env is acceptable, the credentialed upstream URLs and creds also flow into ffmpeg stderr (scrubbed) and into signed segment tokens (unscrubbed, see prior finding). There is no use of the repo's secrets/keyDerivation service for the IPTV subscription secret, so any log path or token leak exposes the paid line. The scrubXtreamCreds regex (iptvRemux.ts:6-19) is a denylist that only catches env-matching user/pass and a couple URL shapes — a provider that 30x-redirects to a CDN with creds in a query param the regex doesn't match would leak.
findings.evidence: xtream.ts:23-24 returns password from env verbatim; iptvRemux.ts:6-19 scrub is best-effort denylist; segment-token rid carries unscrubbed creds (iptv.ts:686).
findings.recommendation: Centralize the IPTV credential behind the existing secrets service; never serialize a credentialed URL into a client-reachable artifact; make scrubbing allowlist-based (only emit known-safe fields).

findings.title: M3U export endpoint uses non-constant-time secret comparison and dumps full catalog
findings.description: /export/recommender authorizes via 'secret !== env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET' — a plain string comparison, not crypto.timingSafeEqual, giving a (small but real) timing oracle on a long-lived shared secret. On success it returns the entire vod+series catalog including tmdb_id/plot/cast for every title. It is unauthenticated except for the header secret (no requireAuth), so a leaked or guessed secret exposes the full library.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:1068 'secret !== env.IPTV_RECOMMENDER_EXPORT_SECRET'; handler has no requireAuth middleware (iptv.ts:1066).
findings.recommendation: Use crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length buffers; treat the export as an internal-principal call (the repo has internalPrincipal infra) rather than a shared header secret, and bind it to the internal network.

findings.title: Tests assert almost nothing about the named concurrency/expiry/remux behaviors
findings.description: Coverage exists but is shallow against the load-bearing behaviors. There is no integration test that boots the mounted app and exercises /grant -> byte proxy -> SSRF guard -> replay -> concurrency end to end, so the redirect-SSRF bypass and the HLS slot-leak are untested. The remux test mocks spawn/fs so no ffmpeg runs (the same 'tests point at a stub' pattern flagged for M4). Stream-token tests can't fake the crate's clock easily, so natural-expiry replay/expiry interplay is under-tested. The concurrency tracker has unit tests but nothing verifies the route-level lifecycle (heartbeat from byte handlers), which is exactly where the slot-leak bug lives.
findings.evidence: iptvRemux.test.ts mocks child_process/fs (no real transcode); no test file boots app.ts or hits the proxy handlers; concurrency route lifecycle (heartbeat-from-bytes) is exercised by no test.
findings.recommendation: Add a supertest/app-boot integration suite over the real router covering grant->proxy->replay->concurrency and a redirect-to-private-host SSRF case (should 400/refuse). Assert the ffmpeg arg array in the remux test. Use fake timers around the contracts verify to test expiry+replay together.

findings.title: remux/live byte streams have no upstream connect/idle timeout
findings.description: The MPEG-TS live passthrough and catch-up timeshift call fetch() with only the client-abort signal wired; there is no connect or idle timeout on the upstream. A hung upstream (provider stalls after headers) holds the request, the Node socket, and (because the byte path doesn't heartbeat) eventually a phantom concurrency slot until the client gives up. The list/EPG fetches DO use fetchWithTimeout (xtream.ts) — the byte paths are the inconsistent ones.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:720 'await fetch(upstreamUrl, { signal: controller.signal, headers... })' — controller only aborts on client abort (718); contrast xtream.ts:65 fetchWithTimeout with explicit timeout.
findings.recommendation: Add an upstream connect/idle timeout (AbortSignal.timeout composed with the client signal) to the live/catchup/vod byte fetches so a dead provider doesn't pin server resources.

findings.title: ffmpeg remux input has no protocol whitelist
findings.description: startRemuxSession passes opts.upstreamUrl directly as ffmpeg '-i' with no '-protocol_whitelist'. The URL is server-constructed from env creds today (low risk), but ffmpeg's default protocol set is broad; if a future code path ever feeds an upstream-influenced URL (e.g. a provider-redirected manifest) ffmpeg could be steered to file:/concat:/other protocols. Defense-in-depth is cheap here.
findings.evidence: iptvRemux.ts:124-125 '-i', opts.upstreamUrl with no '-protocol_whitelist' among the args.
findings.recommendation: Add '-protocol_whitelist file,http,https,tcp,tls,crypto' (or stricter) and reject non-http(s) upstreamUrl before spawn.

findings.title: playlist.m3u and external M3U credential exposure window via long-lived per-channel tokens
findings.description: The external-player M3U mints a 300s 'live' token per channel embedded in URLs in the downloadable .m3u body. The body is Cache-Control:no-store but is an attachment a player stores on disk; while 300s TTL limits the window, the file remains a list of self-authenticating stream URLs for any channel for 5 minutes. Combined with the redirect-SSRF gap, a leaked M3U yields short-lived authenticated proxy access. Acceptable for the homelab threat model but worth noting for App-Store distribution where the file lands on many devices.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:336-344 signs a 'live' token per channel into '/api/iptv/stream/live/${id}.ts?t=${token}' written into the served playlist body.
findings.recommendation: Consider device-bound playlist tokens (the jti->row infra exists) so a leaked M3U is revocable per device, and shorten/rotate the per-channel TTL.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: For Plex-equivalent live TV the IPTV core is meaningfully further along than the roadmap's terse framing suggests: it has a real catalog mirror, streaming EPG with now/next + grid + per-channel window, catch-up/timeshift, MPEG-TS passthrough, HLS manifest rewrite, ffmpeg remux-to-HLS for AVPlayer, VOD/series with Range, favorites, watch history, and a revocable external-player M3U (VLC/TiviMate) — the last is something Plex itself does not expose as cleanly. The principal parity gaps: (1) Concurrency/'too many streams' is Plex's signature enforcement and here it is per-process, advisory-at-grant, and leaks for HLS — it will not reliably cap real playback at scale, and a kicked session keeps streaming. (2) Transcoding is remux-only (-c copy); there is no real transcode/transrate ladder, so a client whose device can't decode the source codec/container has no fallback, whereas Plex transcodes per client capability (this aligns with the M4 transcoder being the separate, incomplete piece). (3) EPG is flat programmes (title/desc/start/stop) with no series linkage, ratings, categories, or artwork, versus Plex's rich guide. (4) Security posture (redirect-SSRF bypass, creds-in-segment-tokens, plaintext provider password) is below the bar for a product proxying a paid subscription to App-Store devices. Net: the live-TV building blocks are genuinely functional and integrated (mounted, scheduled, token-secured, replay-protected) — not the dead code an initial skim might suggest — but session/concurrency semantics, transcoding fallback, and the SSRF/credential hardening need real work before this is a Plex-class, App-Store-safe live experience.

dossiers.purpose: Defines how theemeraldexchange is built, tested, packaged, and shipped: the multi-service docker-compose stack (Hono backend, Python recommender, Rust media-core + transcoder, cloudflared tunnel, and a full self-hosted Glitchtip telemetry stack), the four multi-stage Dockerfiles, the GitHub Actions CI merge gates, the rsync-based NAS deploy script, the Netlify SPA build config, and the TypeScript/Vite/Vitest/Playwright/ESLint toolchain configs.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The stack builds and deploys end-to-end with genuinely sophisticated multi-stage Dockerfiles (cross-language NAPI/PyO3 artifact staging, static ffmpeg, glibc/musl reasoning), thoughtful loopback-only network isolation, and a correctly-pinned mandatory Glitchtip telemetry stack; but it is NOT production-grade: the backend/media-core/transcoder run as root with no healthcheck, the ffmpeg-spawning transcoder has no container CPU/RAM/pids limit, no container hardening (cap_drop/no-new-privileges/read_only), the public-facing cloudflared is :latest, coverage has no enforced floor, 'E2E' runs against a mocked SPA with no backend, and CI never builds the images it ships.
what_works: Network isolation is well-designed: backend binds 127.0.0.1:3001 only, recommender/media-core/transcoder publish to NAS loopback only (8001/8002/8003) and talk over the internal docker network; only cloudflared reaches the public internet, and the internal services are never tunnel-exposed.

Multi-stage Dockerfiles are genuinely sophisticated and well-documented: the backend stages the linux-x64-gnu NAPI .node from a rust:1.90 builder; the recommender stages the PyO3 wheel via maturin and installs CPU-only torch from PyTorch's CPU index to avoid the ~6GB CUDA payload; better-sqlite3's python3/make/g++ toolchain is installed and purged in the same layer.

ffmpeg version gate handled at build time: static-ffmpeg 7.1 is COPYed in because Debian bookworm apt ships 5.1, which fails server/services/ffmpeg.ts's boot-time >=6.0 assertion (validateFfmpegOrExit, called at server/index.ts:28).

Telemetry is actually wired, not just declared: server/index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub }) before serve(), and EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is passed through in compose; a full self-hosted Glitchtip stack (web + worker + postgres:15 + redis:7) is defined with correct v6.0 migration/partition bootstrap, VALKEY_URL, and TRUSTED_PROXIES — this satisfies the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract, and a PII scrub runs on every event.

The recommender image is the one fully-hardened container: it creates a non-root system user (uid 10001), chowns /srv and /data to it, and ships a real HEALTHCHECK (curl /health, 30s interval) — a correct model the other three images do not follow.

Glitchtip and the only public-facing-adjacent images carry deliberate version pins with comments explaining why `latest` is dangerous (glitchtip 6.1; the compose comments capture the Granian port change, django-vtasks worker role, and migrate-on-web-role gotcha).

Strong secret hygiene at the boundary: env.ts production gate requires the three token IKMs and asserts pairwise distinctness (compose comments document it), deploy-nas.sh mirrors the SESSION_SECRET length+placeholder check and the PLEX_SERVER_ID/ALLOW_UNSCOPED gate before shipping, and .env.production.example leaves all secrets blank with openssl-generate instructions.

deploy-nas.sh has real preflight: required-key validation, .env shipped separately and chmod 600'd on the NAS, a compose-availability fallback chain that HARD-FAILS rather than silently degrading USE_LOCAL_RECOMMENDER, and a .dockerignore-presence warning.

CI gates all three language stacks on PR-to-main: Node (tsc SPA + tsc server + lint + vitest coverage + build + Playwright), recommender (PyO3 maturin wheel + pytest parity + the N-API↔PyO3 cross-binding gate run with CI_REQUIRE_CROSS_BINDING=1 so drift fails red), and Rust (cargo build + cargo test --workspace), plus a test-no-iptv matrix that re-runs the whole suite with IPTV_DISABLED=1.

.dockerignore is thorough and tracked: excludes .env*/node_modules/dist/.git/target/**/*.node/tests/data/recommender-db, so secrets and build junk never enter the build context; deploy-nas.sh ships it and the backend Dockerfile relies on it (the .node copy from the builder stage bypasses the **/*.node exclude deliberately).

media-core and transcoder mount the media library read-only (:ro) at the SAME host path so ffmpeg input paths resolve identically; the transcoder HLS scratch is a size-capped (2g) tmpfs that auto-clears on restart.

findings.title: Backend, media-core, and transcoder run as root with no healthcheck
findings.description: Three of the four images create no non-root user and switch to none (verified: grep USER across all four Dockerfiles returns only the recommender's user; the backend/media-core/transcoder have no USER directive, so they run as uid 0). The transcoder is the worst case: it runs as root, spawns ffmpeg subprocesses (tokio::process per its header, lines 8-11), and mounts the media library — a path-handling or process-exec bug in the ffmpeg path executes with root in-container. The recommender image proves the team knows how to do this right (it creates uid 10001 and chowns /data), but that pattern was not applied to the other three. These same three images also lack a HEALTHCHECK (only recommender/Dockerfile:115 has one), so Docker cannot detect a crash-looping or wedged backend/media-core/transcoder.
findings.evidence: recommender/Dockerfile:70-71 'groupadd --system --gid 10001 recommender && useradd --system --uid 10001 ... recommender' + HEALTHCHECK at line 115; Dockerfile ends 'CMD ["npm", "start"]' (line 118) with NO USER and NO HEALTHCHECK; crates/media-core/Dockerfile 'CMD ["media-core"]' (47) and crates/transcoder/Dockerfile 'CMD ["transcoder"]' (50) likewise.
findings.recommendation: Replicate the recommender's non-root pattern in the backend, media-core, and transcoder images (create a uid, chown /app/data, /data, /scratch, then USER it), and add a HEALTHCHECK to each (backend: wget -qO- http://127.0.0.1:3001/api/iptv/health or a dedicated /health; media-core/transcoder: their readiness endpoints).

findings.title: No healthchecks in compose, so every depends_on starts-not-ready (cloudflared + glitchtip-worker race their dependencies)
findings.description: No compose service declares a healthcheck: block (verified by grep). Therefore every depends_on waits only for container START, not readiness: cloudflared (depends_on backend) can begin proxying before the backend's env.ts gate finishes or while it is crash-looping in the documented telemetry-bootstrap window; glitchtip web and glitchtip-worker (depends_on glitchtip-db/redis) can start before Postgres accepts connections, reproducing the exact 'relation users_user does not exist' class of failure the compose comments elsewhere fight. The backend /health-equivalent is only consulted post-hoc by the deploy script, never by Docker's orchestration.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml cloudflared 'depends_on:\n  - backend' (267-268) with no 'healthcheck:' on backend; glitchtip-worker depends_on glitchtip-db/redis (365-367) with no healthcheck on either. grep 'healthcheck' over the whole file => none.
findings.recommendation: Add healthchecks to backend, glitchtip-db (pg_isready), and glitchtip-redis, then add `condition: service_healthy` to the depends_on of cloudflared, glitchtip, and glitchtip-worker so the tunnel and worker only start once their dependencies are actually serving.

findings.title: No resource limits on any service — the ffmpeg-spawning transcoder can OOM the NAS
findings.description: No service declares mem_limit, cpus, pids_limit, or a deploy.resources block (verified). The transcoder spawns one ffmpeg per session and is the single most resource-intensive service; its only guards are app-level env knobs MAX_CONCURRENT_TRANSCODES=4 / MAX_CONCURRENT_CPU_TRANSCODES=1 enforced inside the Rust process. If concurrency.rs has a bug or sessions leak, ffmpeg can saturate all NAS CPU/RAM and OOM-kill co-tenant workloads — including the backend the transcoder path depends on. The compose comment at line 237 even acknowledges fan-out ('Sized for concurrent sessions; raise if MAX_CONCURRENT_TRANSCODES grows') while capping only the /scratch tmpfs, not CPU/RAM. The recommender (loads a sentence-transformer model into RAM) is similarly uncapped.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml transcoder (220-257) has tmpfs '/scratch:size=2g' and environment but no 'mem_limit'/'cpus'/'pids_limit'/'deploy:'; same absence across all services.
findings.recommendation: Add cpus/mem_limit/pids_limit (or deploy.resources.limits under swarm/compose v3) to at least the transcoder and recommender, sized relative to the concurrency caps, so a runaway ffmpeg or model load cannot take down the NAS or the backend.

findings.title: cloudflared pinned to :latest — the one public-internet-facing container auto-upgrades through breaking changes
findings.description: cloudflared uses image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest. This is the single container exposed to the public internet (it terminates the Cloudflare Tunnel and dials the loopback backend). The same file demonstrates the team knows better — glitchtip is pinned to 6.1 with a comment that 'latest silently auto-upgrades through breaking changes.' A docker compose pull (which deploys trigger) can swap the tunnel client for a behavior-changed version at the worst layer, and it makes deploys non-reproducible.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml:260 'image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest'; contrast 281-284 glitchtip pin-and-warn comment.
