findings.recommendation: Pin cloudflared to a specific version tag (and ideally a digest) and bump deliberately.

findings.title: No container hardening: missing cap_drop, no-new-privileges, read_only across all services
findings.description: No service declares security_opt: [no-new-privileges:true], cap_drop: [ALL], or read_only: true (verified by grep). Combined with three of four containers running as root, each retains the full default Linux capability set and can gain new privileges via setuid binaries. For a platform accepting authenticated multi-user traffic over a public tunnel and bound for the App Store, this is below baseline; the root + ffmpeg + mounted-media transcoder is the highest-value target.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml: no occurrence of 'security_opt', 'cap_drop', 'read_only', or 'no-new-privileges' in the 375-line file.
findings.recommendation: Add security_opt: ["no-new-privileges:true"] and cap_drop: ["ALL"] (re-adding only required caps) to every service; make the app containers read_only with explicit writable mounts for /app/data, /data, and /scratch.

findings.title: Coverage is collected but no threshold is enforced — the 'coverage' gate cannot fail on low coverage
findings.description: CI runs npm run test:coverage (vitest run --coverage), but the coverage block in vitest.config.ts defines only provider/reporter/include/exclude — no thresholds (lines/functions/branches/statements). Coverage is therefore merely reported, never enforced; a PR can drop coverage to near-zero and the gate still passes as long as the (possibly trivial) tests pass. The roadmap's M3/M4 'unit-green against a stub' concern is directly enabled by this.
findings.evidence: vitest.config.ts coverage block (38-49) has provider 'v8', reporter, include, exclude — no 'thresholds' key. ci.yml:45 'npm run test:coverage' with no override.
findings.recommendation: Add coverage.thresholds with a realistic floor so the coverage step fails on regression; ratchet upward over time.

findings.title: Playwright 'E2E' runs against the Vite SPA only with all APIs mocked — zero client<->server integration coverage
findings.description: webServer.command is 'npm run dev:vite' (Vite only, port 5173) and the config comment states explicitly that the full backend is intentionally not booted because every /api/* call is intercepted via page.route(). The CI step is named 'Playwright E2E' but tests only the SPA against a mock. Real regressions in CORS credentialed requests, the auth session/device-token flow, stream-token issuance, or the cross-origin VITE_API_BASE_URL wiring are not exercised by any automated test. For an App-Store-bound product there is no automated integration proof of the client/server contract.
findings.evidence: playwright.config.ts:27-34 'webServer: { command: "npm run dev:vite", url: "http://localhost:5173" ... }' and comment lines 4-7 'We start Vite alone (not the full npm run dev which also boots the Hono backend) because every /api/* call is intercepted via page.route()'.
findings.recommendation: Add a second Playwright project (or a compose-based CI job) that boots the real backend (npm start) against a seeded test DB and runs true integration specs for auth, CORS credentials, and stream-token issuance; keep the mocked suite as fast UI smoke but stop labeling it 'E2E'.

findings.title: network_mode: host on cloudflared bypasses Docker network isolation and can reach every loopback admin port
findings.description: cloudflared runs with network_mode: host (justified in the header comment so it can dial 127.0.0.1:3001). Host networking gives it the NAS host's full network namespace, so it can reach ANY loopback-published service — recommender :8001, media-core :8002, transcoder :8003, glitchtip admin :8100 — plus any other homelab service on 127.0.0.1, not just the backend it needs. With cloudflared also pinned to :latest, a compromise of that one image turns a single-port tunnel into broad host-network access. The tunnel only needs to reach the backend.
findings.evidence: docker-compose.yml:263 'network_mode: host'; the same file binds recommender/media-core/transcoder/glitchtip admin ports to 127.0.0.1 (130, 186, 230, 296) — all reachable by host-mode cloudflared.
findings.recommendation: Run cloudflared on the default bridge (or a dedicated network with backend only) and target the backend by service name (tunnel url: http://backend:3001); avoid host networking so a tunnel compromise cannot pivot to other loopback admin ports.

findings.title: deploy-nas.sh rsyncs server/, recommender/, and crates/ with --delete into appdata subdirs that sit alongside the live data volumes
findings.description: Three rsync blocks use --delete into ${APPDATA}/server, /recommender, and /crates. The compose bind-mount data volumes (/mnt/user/appdata/exchange-backend/data, /recommender-db, /media-core-db) are siblings under the same APPDATA root. The crates/ rsync (169-177) and the manifest rsync (183) and the .env scp (186) all write directly into APPDATA. The recommender rsync excludes 'data' (line 152) but the safety of the live DB volumes ultimately depends on no source tree ever producing a path that collides with data/recommender-db/media-core-db under --delete. The crates rsync's --delete with broad excludes is the riskiest: if any excluded-but-present dir ever stops matching, --delete removes destination content. This is a latent footgun where deploy artifacts and persistent SQLite/grab-log data share a root governed by --delete.
findings.evidence: scripts/deploy-nas.sh:142-145 'rsync -av --delete ... server/ ...:${APPDATA}/server/', 148-156 recommender with '--exclude data', 169-177 crates with '--delete'; compose volume '/mnt/user/appdata/exchange-backend/data:/app/data' (27) and '/recommender-db:/data' (135) are children of APPDATA.
findings.recommendation: Deploy the code tree to a dedicated subdirectory (e.g. ${APPDATA}/app) fully separate from the data volumes, or add explicit --exclude for data/recommender-db/media-core-db/media-test to every --delete rsync; consider --backup as a safety net.

findings.title: Orphaned/dead nginx config contradicts the deployed architecture
findings.description: nginx/default.conf is a complete SPA-serving config whose header says it serves the built React dist/ 'as a single-page app' from /usr/share/nginx/html. But there are exactly four tracked Dockerfiles (backend, media-core, transcoder, recommender) and none install or use nginx (verified: the only 'nginx' hit across compose/Dockerfiles is an unrelated comment about reverse proxies). The real SPA build/publish path is Netlify (netlify.toml). deploy-nas.sh:22-23 even tells operators to tear down 'the V1 nginx-static container' separately. This is dead V1 config that will mislead future operators into believing a containerized frontend exists.
findings.evidence: nginx/default.conf:1-3 comment 'Serves the built dashboard (dist/) as a single-page app'; git ls-files '*Dockerfile*' => only 4 backend/rust/python Dockerfiles; deploy-nas.sh:22-23 'Touch the V1 nginx-static container. Tear that down separately'.
findings.recommendation: Delete nginx/default.conf (and nginx/) since the SPA is Netlify-hosted, or, if a self-hosted frontend container is intended, add the nginx Dockerfile + compose service that uses it and update the docs.

findings.title: clippy and cargo fmt are advisory (continue-on-error) — Rust lint never gates the security-critical contracts crate
findings.description: The rust job's fmt and clippy steps both set continue-on-error: true, so they always pass regardless of findings. The clippy invocation even includes '-- -D warnings' (intent to deny) but the continue-on-error neutralizes it. For the workspace that holds the canonical cross-language contracts (emerald-contracts: token/JWE/HMAC/HKDF), clippy catches real correctness/soundness issues, not just style. This lets lint-flagged or unsound patterns merge silently into the most security-critical code in the repo.
findings.evidence: ci.yml:238-240 'run: cargo clippy --all-targets --workspace -- -D warnings' with 'continue-on-error: true' (240); fmt likewise at 232-236.
findings.recommendation: Drop continue-on-error from the clippy step so 'cargo clippy ... -- -D warnings' actually gates; keep fmt advisory if desired but enforce clippy at least on the contracts/token crates.

findings.title: Base images pinned to mutable tags, not digests — non-reproducible builds
findings.description: All base/runtime images use floating tags (rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim, debian:bookworm-slim, python:3.12-slim, mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1, postgres:15, redis:7-alpine). These tags receive ongoing patch pushes, so two builds of the same commit can differ, breaking reproducibility and supply-chain provenance; a poisoned upstream tag is pulled silently. glitchtip:6.1 is the one digest-less-but-pinned good example with explanatory comment.
findings.evidence: Dockerfile:23 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm', 68 'FROM node:24-slim', 77 'mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1'; docker-compose.yml:322 'postgres:15', 333 'redis:7-alpine' — none use @sha256 digests.
findings.recommendation: Pin runtime base images by digest (FROM node:24-slim@sha256:...) or at minimum immutable patch tags, and adopt a Renovate/Dependabot bump policy.

findings.title: .env.example ships a literal weak SESSION_SECRET default
findings.description: .env.example leaves SESSION_SECRET blank with a generate-with-node comment — actually correct on closer read. The real low-severity issue is XTREAM_HOST=https://example.mybunny.tv being a concrete-looking default and the dev example embedding live-looking NAS LAN IPs in commented backing-service URLs (line 48-50) and .env.production.example shipping real LAN IPs (10.0.0.52) and the real owner username (ADMINS=ChrisPachulski, line 82). For a repo the owner flags as potentially public (CLAUDE.md), committing the real admin username and internal LAN topology in .env.production.example is information disclosure, even if not a secret.
findings.evidence: .env.production.example:82 'ADMINS=ChrisPachulski'; 108 'SONARR_URL=http://10.0.0.52:8989/tv', 110/112 same host — real internal IP + admin identity in a tracked example file. CLAUDE.md: 'Repo may be public — never hardcode ... IP addresses, or personal info'.
findings.recommendation: Replace the real admin username with a placeholder (e.g. your-plex-username) and the 10.0.0.52 LAN IP with a documented placeholder (e.g. <nas-lan-ip>) in .env.production.example to honor the repo's own no-PII/no-IP rule.

findings.title: ESLint applies browser globals to server code and uses no type-aware linting
findings.description: eslint.config.js applies one flat config to all **/*.{ts,tsx} with languageOptions: { globals: globals.browser }. server/ is Node/Hono code needing globals.node (process, Buffer); with only browser globals, server-side undefined-global usage is either noisy or defanged. There is no parserOptions.project, so the highest-value TS lint rules for an async streaming backend — no-floating-promises and no-misused-promises — are not active, meaning unawaited async in the backend (e.g., the void registerIptvSchedule pattern) is never lint-caught.
findings.evidence: eslint.config.js:18-20 'languageOptions: { globals: globals.browser }' — single block for all ts/tsx, no globals.node override for server/, no parserOptions.project.
findings.recommendation: Split into a browser block for src/ and a node block for server/ (globals.node) and enable type-aware linting (parserOptions.project) so no-floating-promises/no-misused-promises gate the backend.

findings.title: CI never builds or smoke-tests the Docker images that actually ship to production
findings.description: All CI jobs run on the host runner via npm/cargo/pytest directly. Nothing runs docker build on any of the four Dockerfiles or docker compose config/build. The Dockerfiles contain non-trivial, fragile logic (cross-stage NAPI .node staging with the documented npx --package @napi-rs/cli quirk, static-ffmpeg COPY, better-sqlite3 source compile, maturin PyO3 wheel, CPU-torch). A change that breaks the image build passes CI green and only fails at docker compose up -d --build on the NAS during deploy — there is no pre-deploy validation of the deployable artifact.
findings.evidence: ci.yml jobs test/test-no-iptv/recommender/rust (14-256) contain no 'docker build', 'docker compose build', or 'docker compose config'; the only image build is scripts/deploy-nas.sh:204-219 on the NAS itself.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI job running 'docker compose build' (or per-Dockerfile builds) plus 'docker compose config -q' so image-build regressions are caught before deploy.

findings.title: Telemetry init has no error-capture integration and no flush-on-shutdown — crashes during graceful shutdown may not be reported
findings.description: Sentry.init only wires onUnhandledRejectionIntegration; there is no Hono onError -> Sentry.captureException bridge in app.ts (verified: no onError/captureException), so handled errors thrown inside route handlers are returned to the client but never reach Glitchtip unless they bubble to an unhandled rejection. Additionally, the shutdown() handler (93-99) closes DBs and calls process.exit without awaiting Sentry.flush(), so any event queued just before SIGTERM (the normal container-stop signal) can be dropped before transmission. Given §15 makes telemetry mandatory, silently losing handled-error and shutdown-window events undercuts the contract's intent.
findings.evidence: server/index.ts:43-45 integrations only '[Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration(...)]'; shutdown() (93-99) 'closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...)' with no 'await Sentry.flush()'; no app.onError/captureException in app.ts.
findings.recommendation: Add a Hono app.onError that calls Sentry.captureException before returning the error response, and make shutdown() await Sentry.flush(2000) before process.exit so queued events transmit on SIGTERM.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Infra is architected for a multi-service Plex-class system (library scanner = media-core, transcode-on-demand = transcoder, recommendations = recommender), and the read-only :media mount shared identically between media-core and transcoder mirrors how Plex separates scanning from transcoding. The gaps that matter for Plex parity are operational, not architectural: (1) Plex transcoding is resource-governed; here the transcoder has NO container CPU/RAM/pids limit (only app-level concurrency knobs), so under many concurrent streams it can OOM the NAS and take down the backend it depends on — 'many concurrent transcodes' is Plex's defining workload and this stack has no container-level backpressure for it. (2) No healthchecks on backend/media-core/transcoder means a crash-looping transcode service is invisible to the orchestrator while cloudflared keeps routing. (3) The transcoder running as root spawning ffmpeg over a mounted library is a privilege posture Plex does not take. (4) CI proves unit-green but never proves a real transcode or even builds the shipping images (consistent with the roadmap's M4 'no real transcode has ever run' note), so there is no infra-level evidence the transcode path works end-to-end. For an App-Store-bound Plex replacement the infra is a credible skeleton, but it is not yet hardened or resource-governed to survive Plex-equivalent concurrent-streaming load. Note: the recommender image is correctly hardened (non-root + healthcheck), so the team has a working template to bring the other three services up to that bar quickly.

dossiers.purpose: Server-side media discovery and recommendation surface for the SPA: a thin authenticated streaming/proxy to media-core (routes/media.ts), a read-only accessor over media-core's media.db for local-availability tagging (mediaLibraryDb.ts + mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts + localAvailability.ts), the Claude-Haiku-backed personalized suggestions engine with a TMDB candidate-pool/discover/trending fallback chain and an alternate local-recommender-sidecar fast path (routes/suggestions.ts), the recommender bridge with internal-principal HMAC auth and bounded timeouts (recommender.ts, recommenderCaller.ts), per-user feedback + household rejection stores (userFeedback.ts, rejections.ts, routes/feedback.ts), a click-mirror endpoint (recommenderEvents.ts), a TMDB credits/trending proxy that keeps the key server-side (routes/tmdb.ts), the section-9 grant-time source-precedence resolver (sourcePrecedence.ts), an ffprobe boot-validation helper (ffmpeg.ts), and the N-API glue that loads the Rust contracts addon under ESM (contractsBinding.ts).
files_reviewed: NOTE: server/services/media.ts, server/routes/tagLocalAvailability.ts, server/services/sourcePrecedence.test.ts and a recommender_events table/migration DO NOT EXIST on this branch — confirmed by existence checks and full-tree greps; the audit brief's assumed routes/media.ts->services/media.ts->media-core chain and recommenderEvents logging-to-DB are not the actual implementation.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The suggestions engine and feedback/rejections stores are genuinely production-minded (bounded timeouts, single-flight coalescing, atomic temp+rename writes, fail-closed corruption handling, cross-store rollback with split-brain logging, retry/fill chains that never serve an empty strip) and the media proxy correctly preserves byte-range streaming and fails closed on mint failure; but the read-only media.db local-tagging path is not consumed by the web client's search/detail, the server->recommender internal-principal is minted-but-not-enforced-by-default on the wire, and the local-media grant/source-selection/playback chain is acknowledged-in-code as deferred to M3+ — so it is solidly functional, not yet production-grade at App-Store scale.
what_works: routes/media.ts is a correctly-built streaming proxy: it forwards range/if-range/if-none-match/if-modified-since and copies back content-range/accept-ranges/etag/last-modified, preserving 206 Partial Content and 304 so HTTP seeking works end-to-end (media.ts:14-31, 80-93). It fails CLOSED on internal-principal mint failure with 502 rather than silently proxying an unauthenticated write (media.ts:47-52) — the right posture. The /api/media mount is correctly gated behind USE_MEDIA_CORE (app.ts:170).

mediaLibraryDb.ts opens media-core's media.db with {readonly:true, fileMustExist:true} and deliberately runs NO migrations and NO WAL pragma, with a comment explaining a second writer would race media-core's sqlx; the singleton degrades gracefully (returns null, latches openFailed, never throws) when the file is missing (mediaLibraryDb.ts:39-45, mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts:22-36). Correct ownership model, proven by tests (read-only rejected writes, fileMustExist).

Input validation and per-user isolation are sound (no IDOR): feedback/recommenderEvents derive sub from c.get('session').sub (set by requireAuth from a verified token), never from the client; tmdbId is gated by Number.isSafeInteger && >0 on POST and DELETE (feedback.ts:102-104, 233-235; recommenderEvents.ts:49-51); kind is enum-checked; SQL into media.db is parameterized (localAvailability.ts:74-77, suggestions.ts:1783-1788).

userFeedback.ts and rejections.ts are well-engineered persistence: atomic temp-file + rename(2) writes (userFeedback.ts:198-213), fail-closed on JSON parse corruption rather than wiping household data (userFeedback.ts:185-194, rejections.ts:107-118), a serialized write queue where the CALLER awaits the real op (route returns 500 on ENOSPC instead of lying {ok:true}) with a separate recovery branch keeping the chain alive — and the tests actually exercise ENOSPC and concurrent-failure ordering (userFeedback.test.ts:308-385).

feedback.ts handles the two-store (personal feedback + household rejection) cross-file race with a per-(kind,tmdbId) mutex (itemLocks) and explicit rollback-with-split-brain-logging on partial failure in both the dislike and red-to-green like branches (feedback.ts:48-73, 115-192). Genuinely careful concurrency work, not demo code.

The recommender bridge fails safe and is bounded: scoreOnce has a 5s AbortController timeout (recommender.ts:61,139-161), mirror posts a 3s timeout (recommender.ts:68,100-125), all mirror calls are fire-and-forget (void) so a hung sidecar never blocks the user, and recommenderCaller latches ensureServerId failure to avoid hammering disk on every request (recommenderCaller.ts:25-48).

The suggestions engine is genuinely sophisticated and defensive: single-flight coalescing on every TMDB GET (titleByIdInFlight/lookupInFlight), settled-result + null caches with key caps, 429 Retry-After honoring bounded by the abort budget, BACKFILL_MAX_PER_CALL so legacy bare-id rows don't fire hundreds of simultaneous TMDB requests, parallel paginated trending with per-page timeouts + allSettled, a Claude 20s deadline + transient-529/503 retry, tool-use-enforced output with a max_tokens-truncation guard, and a never-empty retry->discover->trending fill chain (suggestions.ts:654-820, 1248-1305, 1562-1598, 2305-2427, 2607-2681). It also sanitizes titles on read AND write as prompt-injection defense (userFeedback.ts:85, rejections.ts:65).

ffmpeg.ts boot validation is hardened: execFileSync with a hardcoded ['-version'] arg list (no injection surface), 5s timeout, ENOENT vs non-zero-exit handling, and a pass-unconditionally policy for git-dev/master version strings so it doesn't false-negative on nightly builds (ffmpeg.ts:106-171).

contractsBinding.ts correctly solves a real ESM/N-API footgun: it uses createRequire to load @emerald/contracts-napi because the addon's dynamic module.exports=loadBinding() resolves to undefined under a bare ESM star-import — the comment documents exactly why a naive import would silently yield undefined functions that throw only at call time (contractsBinding.ts:1-16). internalPrincipal.ts gates minting on INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET and throws a clear error if missing.

tmdb.ts keeps the TMDB key server-side and surfaces only /credits and /trending/:type, validating type against the movie|tv enum and returning 503 when unconfigured (tmdb.ts:42-44, 93-96). The suggestions route adds an explicit requireTrustedOrigin CSRF gate on its GET because it has write side effects via the sidecar (suggestions.ts:72-79) — a subtle correct call most teams miss.

findings.title: Server->recommender internal-principal Bearer is minted but the receiver DEFAULTS to 'off' (declared-but-unenforced auth on the bridge)
findings.description: The server mints and attaches the internal-principal Bearer to recommender calls, but the recommender receiver's enforcement mode (RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE) DEFAULTS to 'off', meaning the principal header is ignored entirely unless an operator explicitly flips it to 'log' then 'enforce'. recommender.ts:73-76 confirms this is intentional/pending. So today the only real auth on the server->sidecar channel is the shared x-recommender-secret HMAC header, and the sub is carried as a plain BODY field (recommender.ts:164-174 postFeedback({sub,...})), not bound to a verified principal. Any process that can reach recommender:8000 inside the docker network with the static secret can post feedback/impressions/rejections/shown for ANY user. This is the section-4 Hybrid-D internal-auth decision (a recorded one-way-door in MEMORY.md) being wired on the mint side but a no-op on the verify side for the default deployment.
findings.evidence: config.py:94 `raw = os.environ.get('RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE', 'off')`; config.py:42-44 comment 'Mode is \"off\" by default so a deployment that hasn't yet provisioned the secret keeps working'. recommender.ts:73-76 'The recommender service does not enforce the principal yet ... one-line recommender-side enforcement flip.' Bodies carry sub directly (recommender.ts:164-174).
findings.recommendation: Land PyO3 verification in the recommender image, set RECOMMENDER_INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_MODE=enforce in the production compose env, and take sub from the verified principal rather than the request body. Until then, document that cross-user event spoofing is possible by any holder of x-recommender-secret on the internal network, and confirm the secret is actually required (it is validated when present in config.py but the channel is only as strong as 'enforce' being on).

findings.title: Local-availability tagging works but is never reachable from search/detail — the 'in your library' signal only appears on the Discover strip
findings.description: tagLocalAvailability is real and correct (tmdb_id JOIN against media.db movies/shows plus a strict normalized-title + EXACT-year fallback) and IS applied inside the suggestions engine (suggestions.ts:2147). But the web media client (src/lib/api/media.ts) exposes only posterFor + media DTO types, and grepping src for grant/stream_token/watch against /api/media returns only IPTV grant usage (iptvApi.grant*). There is no search/detail JSON endpoint that runs TMDB results through tagLocalAvailability. So Plex's pervasive 'you already own this' badge only shows on the personalized Discover strip, not on search or browse — the highest-traffic discovery surfaces don't consume the capability that was specifically built for them.
findings.evidence: src/lib/api/media.ts has no searchMedia/getMediaDetail returning localAvailable; grep across src shows grant/StreamGrant used only via iptvApi (LiveTab/VodTab/IptvSeriesTab). tagLocalAvailability is imported only by the suggestions path.
findings.recommendation: Add a typed media search/detail endpoint (or extend an existing one) that runs results through tagLocalAvailability, and render an 'in your library' badge everywhere the SPA lists titles, matching Plex's availability indicator.

findings.title: Grant-time source precedence cannot select local/media-core — the section-9 local-first resolver only ever returns IPTV
findings.description: sourcePrecedence.ts is named/documented as the section-9 Resolution-A local-first resolver, but buildCandidates() only ever returns an IPTV candidate: the media-core (local) slot is a comment ('media-core (M3+): not wired, always absent in M1.5') and Plex is probed only to populate available_alternatives, never as an auto-fallback because 'we cannot construct a Plex play URL for an IPTV stream_id in M1.5'. So despite local-first being the contract, a title that exists locally in media.db can NEVER be selected by this resolver — it resolves to IPTV or null. There is no media_title_link/getRatingKey(tmdb_id) lookup, and routes/media.ts has no grant step in front of it, so there is no working local-media playback-authorization path — the defining Plex capability.
findings.evidence: sourcePrecedence.ts:111-125 buildCandidates: 'media-core (M3+): not wired, always absent in M1.5.' then `const iptv = await probeIptv(); if (iptv) return [{source:'iptv',...}]; return []`. Comment at :108-109 'M3+ will replace this function with one that queries media_title_link...'.
findings.recommendation: Implement the media_title_link/getRatingKey lookup so local (media-core) and Plex become real ResolvedSource candidates ranked above IPTV, and put a grant step in front of routes/media.ts streaming so playback is authorized per the precedence result.

findings.title: Suggestion validation fans out many TMDB lookups per request — latency and rate-limit degradation the team has already observed
findings.description: Non-pool Claude picks are each resolved against TMDB /search via tmdbLookup, and legacy bare-id rows are backfilled via resolveTitles. There is single-flight coalescing, short result caches, and a pool fast-path that avoids the lookup for pool hits — all good — but the per-request caches are in-process and do NOT help concurrent DISTINCT users, and the code itself documents the failure mode: at the TMDB free-tier ~40 req/10s limit, 'call 2 (cache cold for lookups) gets 429-throttled and returns 1-4 items instead of 20.' At App-Store scale with many households the same popular title is re-looked-up per user and TMDB rate limits will routinely produce short strips. There is no persistent cross-user TMDB metadata cache.
findings.evidence: suggestions.ts:2364-2366 `Promise.all(survivors.map(({pick}) => tmdbLookup(type, pick.title, pick.year)...))`; suggestions.ts:654 comment 'TMDB rate limit is ~40 req / 10s ... call 2 ... gets 429-throttled and returns 1-4 items instead of 20.'
findings.recommendation: Add a persistent (SQLite or shared LRU with TTL) TMDB detail cache keyed by (kind,tmdbId)/(kind,title,year) shared across users, plus a server-side TMDB request budgeter. The local-recommender fast path largely avoids this, but the BYO-Claude path is exposed.

findings.title: TMDB credits proxy does not validate tmdbId/tvdbId before spending an upstream call
findings.description: tmdb.get('/credits') reads tvdbId/tmdbId from the query string and interpolates them into the upstream path via encodeURIComponent (which prevents path traversal) but with NO numeric validation, unlike the strict Number.isSafeInteger guards used in feedback.ts and recommenderEvents.ts and the enum guard on /trending/:type. A client can send arbitrary/garbage ids that each turn into an outbound TMDB call, an amplification toward api.themoviedb.org that burns the server's shared TMDB rate budget on nonsense and adds an SSRF-adjacent unauthenticated-fan-out surface.
findings.evidence: tmdb.ts:47-48 reads raw query; :53 `tmdbFetch(/find/${encodeURIComponent(tvdbId)}...)` and :71 `/movie/${encodeURIComponent(tmdbId)}/credits` with no Number.isSafeInteger/positive-int check.
findings.recommendation: Validate tmdbId/tvdbId as positive safe integers and return 400 otherwise, mirroring the validation already standard elsewhere in this subsystem.

findings.title: Internal-principal HMAC comparison in the Rust verifier should use a constant-time compare
findings.description: The Python receiver uses constant-time hmac.compare_digest. The Rust verifier (used by media-core and the napi verify path that backs the server->media-core proxy auth minted in routes/media.ts) should likewise use the hmac crate's constant-time verify_slice rather than a PartialEq byte comparison of the provided vs expected signature. routes/media.ts mints one of these tokens for every media-core request, so the verifying side is on the hot internal-auth path. Severity is medium because exploitation requires network position and the secret is shared internally; note I verified the verifier returns Ok(claims) on success and Err on mismatch (via grep) but could not re-read the exact comparison line at final pass, so the team should confirm whether verify_slice is already used before actioning.
findings.evidence: Earlier grep of internal_principal.rs showed a signature-mismatch return (`return Err(\"signature mismatch\")`) on a direct slice comparison rather than mac.verify_slice; contrast recommender/app/internal_principal.py which uses hmac.compare_digest. Final re-read was blocked by an unresponsive shell, so confirm the exact operator.
findings.recommendation: Ensure verification uses mac.verify_slice(&provided_sig) (constant-time). Additionally validate the `service` claim against an expected allowlist per endpoint so a token minted for one service cannot be replayed against another.

findings.title: recommenderEvents click-mirror and feedback mirror drop on failure with no metric — silent loss of the recommender's training signal
findings.description: The /event route fires postFeedback as void and always returns {ok:true} even when the sidecar is down or the 3s mirror timeout trips; the file header states this click mirror is the ONLY path for click attribution. mirrorPost only console.warns on non-ok (recommender.ts:117-124) — there is no counter/metric for drop rate. For a system whose recommender is supposed to learn from these outcomes, a silently-down sidecar means click/feedback signal is lost with zero alerting. Given section-15 makes Glitchtip telemetry mandatory, console.warn-only is a gap.
findings.evidence: recommenderEvents.ts:66-72 `void postFeedback(...)` then unconditional `return c.json({ ok: true })`; recommender.ts:117-124 mirrorPost only `console.warn` on non-ok, no metric emission.
findings.recommendation: Emit a Glitchtip counter/breadcrumb on mirror failure so signal loss is observable, wiring these drops into the mandatory telemetry pipeline rather than console only.

findings.title: tagLocalAvailability title-fallback full-scans the local movies/shows table on every uncached suggestions refresh
findings.description: When any item is unmatched by tmdb_id (expected today, since media.db rows currently have tmdb_id=NULL per the in-code comment), the fallback runs `SELECT title, year FROM ${table} WHERE year IS NOT NULL` — a full table scan of all movies (or all shows) — then rebuilds a normalized key set in memory, on every suggestions request reaching this path, with no caching of that key set across requests. For a large library this is repeated O(N) work per refresh per user.
findings.evidence: localAvailability.ts:89-91 `db.raw.prepare(`SELECT title, year FROM ${table} WHERE year IS NOT NULL`).all()` runs whenever unmatched.length>0; suggestions.ts has the same fallback inline at :1804-1806. No memoization of localKeys.
findings.recommendation: Cache the normalized (title|year) key set per table with a short TTL invalidated on media.db mtime change, or rely on the tmdb_id JOIN once media-core TMDB enrichment lands and drop the full-scan fallback.

findings.title: Two copies of tagLocalAvailability exist (route module + extracted service) and can drift
findings.description: suggestions.ts contains a private inline tagLocalAvailability (1762-1845) that is byte-for-byte the same logic as the extracted, unit-tested server/services/localAvailability.ts. The route uses its OWN inline copy (suggestions.ts:2147), not the tested service. So the tests in tagLocalAvailability.test.ts validate the service module, but production traffic runs the duplicate inline copy — the two can silently diverge, and a fix to one won't reach the other.
findings.evidence: suggestions.ts:1762 `function tagLocalAvailability(items, kind)` (full duplicate) and its call at :2147; identical logic lives in localAvailability.ts:55-130 which is what the test imports.
findings.recommendation: Delete the inline copy and import the tested service so the unit tests actually cover the production path.

findings.title: media-library/local-availability relies on media.db existing but the file is created only by media-core, which the rest of this branch does not run
findings.description: The singleton correctly degrades to null when media.db is absent, which is the SAFE behavior — but it also means that in any deployment where media-core hasn't scanned (the common case today, since the Apple/native and media-core integration is incomplete), local-availability tagging is silently a no-op for the entire process lifetime (openFailed latches true; only closeMediaLibraryDb resets it). Operators get one console.warn at first miss and then nothing. Combined with the search/detail gap above, the practical effect is that 'local availability' is off in most real deployments without any surfaced indication.
findings.evidence: mediaLibraryDbSingleton.ts:24 `if (openFailed) return null` (latched), :29-34 single console.warn. env.MEDIA_DB_PATH is read once; no re-probe until closeMediaLibraryDb() (which nothing calls in steady state).
findings.recommendation: Surface media.db availability in a health/diag endpoint so operators can see whether local tagging is active, and consider a periodic re-probe (or mtime watch) so tagging lights up automatically once media-core first writes the file without requiring a process restart.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: This subsystem is discovery-and-proxy, and the local-media half of Plex parity is not yet operational. (1) PLAYBACK: routes/media.ts is a competent byte-range-preserving streaming proxy to media-core, but there is no grant/authorization step in front of it, and sourcePrecedence.ts cannot select a local/media-core source at all (buildCandidates only ever returns IPTV; the media-core and Plex slots are explicit comments saying 'not wired'). So even though media.db can be read and the proxy can stream bytes, there is no end-to-end 'pick the local copy and play it' flow — the defining Plex capability. The only working grant path in the SPA is IPTV (iptvApi.grant*). (2) LOCAL-AVAILABILITY: the read-only media.db tagger is real and well-built, but only the Discover suggestion strip consumes it; search/browse never show the 'in your library' badge Plex shows everywhere, and in most current deployments media.db doesn't exist so tagging is a silent no-op. (3) RECOMMENDATIONS / continue-watching analog: the Claude suggestions engine plus the local-recommender sidecar is genuinely sophisticated and arguably exceeds Plex's discovery polish, BUT the server->sidecar internal-principal auth defaults to 'off' (unenforced) and click/feedback mirroring drops silently with no telemetry, so the learning loop is fragile and spoofable on the internal network. Strengths toward parity: a correct media.db ownership boundary (read-only, no migration/WAL races), a real seeking-capable streaming proxy, careful per-user feedback isolation with no IDOR, and a never-empty fallback chain. The gap to a true Plex replacement here is the entire local-playback authorization-and-source-selection path, which the code itself defers to M3+.

dossiers.purpose: Audit every dependency manifest and lockfile across the polyglot stack (Node/TS SPA + Hono server, Rust workspace of 5 crates, Python FastAPI recommender) for unpinned/loose ranges on security-sensitive deps, hallucinated or impossibly-future major versions, unused declared dependencies, version-pin consistency between CI and Dockerfiles, telemetry wiring, and the GPL/x264 ffmpeg + IPTV licensing exposure against the proprietary LICENSE and the stated App-Store/paid-distribution goal.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: The polyglot supply-chain hygiene is split. The Rust side is genuinely production-grade: workspace-pinned crypto crates, Cargo.lock committed (66KB), rustls instead of OpenSSL, strip+lto release profile, and CI/Dockerfiles consistently pinned to rust 1.90. The cross-cutting infra (Sentry/Glitchtip telemetry IS wired at server/index.ts:37, CI node 24 / rust 1.90 / python 3.12 matches all four Dockerfiles, ffmpeg pinned to mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) is solid. But two issues keep this at prototype, not production: (1) the Node toolchain pins a cluster of majors that did not exist as GA stable as of the Jan 2026 cutoff (typescript ~6, vite 8, eslint 10, vitest 4) — these are either prerelease or hallucinated, and `npm ci` reproducibility on a clean machine cannot be assumed; depending on non-GA build/test/lint majors for an App-Store-bound product is reckless. (2) The transcoder ships GPL ffmpeg + libx264 (confirmed: args.rs:46 maps the CPU encoder to "libx264") under a strictly-proprietary LICENSE that explicitly names Apple App Store distribution — an unresolved copyleft-vs-proprietary conflict that is distribution-blocking for the paid/App-Store endpoint. The Python recommender's unbounded >= floors with no committed lockfile compound the non-reproducibility. Works on the author's pinned local lockfiles; not yet reproducible-from-clean nor license-clean for distribution.
what_works: Rust workspace dependencies are centralized via [workspace.dependencies] in the root Cargo.toml and inherited with .workspace=true across all 5 crates, giving a single source of truth for crypto primitives (aes-gcm 0.10.3, hkdf 0.12, hmac 0.12, sha2 0.10, subtle 2, rand 0.8) — current, appropriate versions for the contracts crate.
Cargo.lock IS committed (66589 bytes tracked) — the Rust dependency graph is hash-locked and reproducible, the strongest supply-chain artifact in the repo.
media-core's reqwest uses default-features=false + rustls-tls, deliberately avoiding system OpenSSL/native-tls — correct for reproducible static cross-compilation and to avoid OpenSSL CVE exposure.
Rust release profile (opt-level=3, lto=thin, codegen-units=1, strip=true) is production-appropriate; the maturin/pyo3 build also strips.

Version pins are CONSISTENT across CI and all Dockerfiles: CI uses node 24, rust 1.90 (dtolnay/rust-toolchain toolchain: '1.90'), python 3.12; the root, media-core, transcoder, and recommender Dockerfiles all use rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim/node:24, and python:3.12-slim respectively — no CI/Docker base-image drift.

edition=2024 + rust-version=1.85 are internally consistent (2024 edition requires >=1.85); pyo3 abi3-py312 matches the recommender's requires-python>=3.12 and the python:3.12 runtime, so the Python ABI target is coherent end-to-end.

Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry (§15 MANDATORY) is genuinely wired, not just declared: @sentry/node is imported and Sentry.init is called at server/index.ts:37 with the boot-time DSN (EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN), a PII-scrub beforeSend (server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.ts), and the DSN is surfaced to clients via /api/telemetry/config — matching the per-self-hoster crash-island design.

ffmpeg is pinned to a specific binaries-only image tag (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) and copied as static glibc-free binaries in all three media Dockerfiles, with a boot-time version gate (server/services/ffmpeg.ts rejects <6.0) — a disciplined, reproducible way to ship a known ffmpeg version.

The transcoder already implements a hardware-encoder abstraction (args.rs HwEncoder enum with VideoToolbox, nvenc/qsv/vaapi paths, libx264 only as the CPU fallback) — the architectural seam needed to drop GPL x264 on Apple platforms already exists.

The LICENSE is a clean, deliberate proprietary placeholder (not a copy-pasted MIT) that explicitly enumerates the Apple App Store/TestFlight path and household-only scope — the right instinct for a private, App-Store-bound product.

Build orchestration is thoughtful: the napi .node and PyO3 wheel are built in dedicated Rust toolchain stages and copied into runtime images, so the runtime containers don't carry a Rust toolchain; better-sqlite3's node-gyp toolchain is installed and purged in a single layer to keep the image slim.
package-lock.json is committed (288940 bytes) so `npm ci` is reproducible on the one machine/registry state where it was generated.

findings.title: GPL ffmpeg/libx264 in the transcoder is incompatible with the proprietary LICENSE and the paid App-Store distribution goal
findings.description: The transcoder bundles and drives a GPL-enabled ffmpeg with libx264 — confirmed by code, not just inference: crates/transcoder/src/args.rs:46 maps the CPU encoder to "libx264", concurrency.rs:5 calls out the "(libx264) transcode", and all three media Dockerfiles COPY from mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1 (a GPL build, since it includes libx264). libx264 is GPLv2+ and the ffmpeg build is GPL-licensed copyleft. The repository LICENSE is strictly proprietary, no-redistribution (lines 25-31: 'you may NOT copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, or sell copies of the Software or any portion thereof'), and explicitly names Apple App Store/TestFlight distribution as an intended channel (clause (b), lines 17-19). You cannot distribute a proprietary binary incorporating GPL x264 through Apple's App Store — Apple's standard EULA imposes usage restrictions that conflict with GPLv2/v3's no-additional-restrictions and anti-DRM clauses (the well-known VLC App-Store precedent). The LICENSE makes ZERO mention of any third-party/open-source component obligations, and no NOTICE/THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES file is tracked. For household self-hosted server use this is tolerable; for the stated paid/App-Store endpoint it is a hard blocker.
findings.evidence: LICENSE lines 25-31: 'No other rights are granted. Specifically, you may NOT: - copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, or sell copies of the Software or any portion thereof;'. crates/transcoder/src/args.rs:46: 'HwEncoder::Cpu => "libx264",'. crates/transcoder/Dockerfile:30: 'COPY --from=mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1 /ffmpeg /ffprobe /usr/local/bin/'. No THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES/NOTICE file in git ls-files.
findings.recommendation: Resolve before any binary leaves the household. The encoder seam already exists (args.rs HwEncoder::VideoToolbox): on Apple platforms use VideoToolbox H.264/HEVC and bundle ZERO GPL code in the client; keep GPL ffmpeg only in the self-hosted server (which the household operator never redistributes). For the server image, consider an LGPL-only ffmpeg build (no --enable-gpl, no libx264) if any redistribution to self-hosting operators (LICENSE clause (c)) is contemplated. Add a THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES/NOTICE file enumerating every bundled OSS component (ffmpeg, x264, sqlite, hls.js, mpegts.js, three, etc.) and its license — also an App-Store review requirement.

findings.title: Node toolchain majors pinned to versions that were not GA-stable as of the knowledge cutoff (typescript ~6, vite 8, eslint 10, vitest 4) — reproducibility and prerelease-toolchain risk
findings.description: Several devDependency majors are pinned ahead of any GA stable as of the Jan 2026 cutoff: typescript ~6.0.2 (5.x was the stable line; 6.0 not GA), vite ^8.0.10 (6.x stable line), eslint ^10.2.1 and @eslint/js ^10.0.1 (9.x stable line), vitest ^4.1.5 and @vitest/coverage-v8 ^4.1.5 (3.x stable line), @vitejs/plugin-react ^6.0.1, globals ^17.5.0. CI runs `npm ci` (which respects the committed lockfile) so CI may be green TODAY off the author's lockfile, but these are the entire build/typecheck/lint/test toolchain: if any of these majors is in fact a prerelease/canary or does not exist on the registry, a clean `npm install` or any lockfile regeneration on another machine will not resolve reproducibly, and the 'green typecheck/vitest' claims rest on one frozen lockfile rather than a stable toolchain. Even where a prerelease exists, shipping an App-Store-bound product on non-GA TypeScript/ESLint/Vite/Vitest majors means depending on churning, pre-stable APIs.
findings.evidence: package.json devDependencies: 'typescript': '~6.0.2', 'vite': '^8.0.10', 'eslint': '^10.2.1', '@eslint/js': '^10.0.1', 'vitest': '^4.1.5', '@vitest/coverage-v8': '^4.1.5', '@vitejs/plugin-react': '^6.0.1', 'globals': '^17.5.0'. Build script: 'tsc -b && vite build && tsc -p server/tsconfig.json'; CI step 'Type-check (SPA): npx tsc --noEmit' and 'Test + coverage: npm run test:coverage' depend on these majors.
findings.recommendation: Run `npm view typescript@6 version`, `npm view vite versions`, `npm view eslint versions`, `npm view vitest versions` to confirm what actually published. Pin to the latest GA stable majors (realistically TS 5.x, Vite 6.x, ESLint 9.x, Vitest 3.x as of early 2026) unless there is a documented reason to ride a prerelease. Regenerate package-lock.json from a clean install on a clean machine and verify `npm ci && npm run build && npm test && npm run lint` from scratch. Do not ship on prerelease toolchain majors.

findings.title: Caret ranges on every security-sensitive Node runtime dependency (jose, simplewebauthn, better-sqlite3, sentry)
findings.description: All runtime deps, including auth/crypto-critical ones, use caret ranges: jose ^6.2.3 (JWT/JWE — device_token and stream_token primitives ride on this), @simplewebauthn/server ^13.3.1 and @simplewebauthn/browser ^13.3.0 (the entire passkey auth surface), better-sqlite3 ^11.10.0 (native module, every DB read/write), @sentry/node ^9.47.1 (the §15-mandatory crash lifeline). Caret means any in-major minor/patch satisfies the range. On any host that runs `npm install` rather than `npm ci`, or after a lockfile regeneration, these can silently float to a build that was never reviewed — a supply-chain exposure for the exact libraries that gate authentication. The Dockerfile and CI correctly use `npm ci`, which mitigates the FLOAT at deploy time, but deploy-nas.sh and any local dev `npm install` are unguarded, and lockfile regen is unconstrained.
findings.evidence: package.json dependencies: 'jose': '^6.2.3', '@simplewebauthn/browser': '^13.3.0', '@simplewebauthn/server': '^13.3.1', 'better-sqlite3': '^11.10.0', '@sentry/node': '^9.47.1' — every one a caret range.
findings.recommendation: Pin auth/crypto deps (jose, both @simplewebauthn packages, @sentry/node) to exact versions; enforce `npm ci` in deploy-nas.sh as well as CI/Docker; add `npm audit --audit-level=high` and `npm audit signatures` as a CI gate; add Renovate/Dependabot with a manual-review group for the crypto/auth deps so version bumps are deliberate, not silent.

findings.title: Python recommender deps are unbounded (>= floors only) with no committed lockfile — non-reproducible builds
findings.description: Every recommender dependency uses an open-ended lower bound with no upper cap: fastapi>=0.115, uvicorn[standard]>=0.32, pydantic>=2.9, sqlite-vec>=0.1.9, numpy>=1.26, httpx>=0.28, apscheduler>=3.10, anyio>=4.6, anthropic>=0.42, tenacity>=9.0, python-dateutil>=2.9, sentence-transformers>=3.3. No poetry.lock / requirements.txt with hashes is tracked, and the build-backend is hatchling (no lock). The Dockerfile does `pip install .` (line 103), so a build today vs next month resolves a different transitive closure — pydantic v2->v3, numpy 1.x->2.x ABI breaks, and the sentence-transformers/torch stack churn that routinely breaks at runtime. sqlite-vec>=0.1.9 is a pre-1.0 native extension floor with no ceiling, so a breaking 0.2 is silently accepted. The Dockerfile already had to special-case torch (CPU-only index, line 102) to avoid a 6GB CUDA pull — evidence that the unbounded ML stack is fragile. This undermines the cross-language byte-identical-contracts guarantee: the PyO3 ABI (abi3-py312) is fixed but the surrounding Python stack is not.
findings.evidence: recommender/pyproject.toml lines 6-19: all entries are '<name>>=<floor>' with no '<' upper bound. recommender/Dockerfile:102-103: 'RUN pip install --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu torch' then 'RUN pip install .' — no --require-hashes, no lockfile. No poetry.lock/requirements*.txt under recommender/ in git ls-files.
findings.recommendation: Add upper bounds on the breaking-major-risk libs (pydantic>=2.9,<3; numpy>=1.26,<3; sqlite-vec; pin torch + sentence-transformers explicitly) and commit a lockfile (uv.lock or pip-compile requirements.txt --generate-hashes). Install with --require-hashes in the Dockerfile. Evaluate whether anthropic>=0.42 is still needed (see unused-SDK finding) given the recommender's own description is 'Replaces per-request Claude calls'.

findings.title: @runwayml/sdk is a declared but completely unused dependency
findings.description: @runwayml/sdk ^3.21.0 (a commercial video-generation SDK) is declared in devDependencies but is imported by ZERO source files — a repo-wide search for 'runwayml' returns matches ONLY in package.json and package-lock.json, never in src/**, server/**, or any .ts/.tsx/.mjs. It is dead weight in the dependency tree: every declared dep is install surface, audit noise, and a potential supply-chain vector, and an LLM/generative-media SDK that can make outbound network calls is exactly the kind of dependency you do not want sitting unused in a product bound for App-Store review.
findings.evidence: git grep for 'runwayml' across the repo returns only: 'package-lock.json' and 'package.json'. No importer exists in any source tree.
findings.recommendation: Remove @runwayml/sdk from package.json and regenerate the lockfile. If it was added for an experiment, keep that on a feature branch, not on the integration branch bound for release.

findings.title: @anthropic-ai/sdk retained in the server despite the recommender being built to replace per-request Claude calls
findings.description: @anthropic-ai/sdk ^0.95.2 IS used (server/routes/suggestions.ts, server/services/usageLog.ts, plus SPA UsageDashboard/ApiKeySettings/TrendingRow read usage data), so it is not dead code. But the recommender service's own pyproject description states it 'Replaces per-request Claude calls with a SQLite + sqlite-vec scoring pipeline', and project memory records the recommender data-model decision that local-first scoring supersedes per-source Claude calls. This means the TS layer still carries a live outbound-LLM dependency that the architecture intended to retire — an outbound network/cost/PII surface and a pre-1.0 SDK (0.95.x) on the critical suggestions path. This is a coherence/parity gap, not a correctness bug.
findings.evidence: package.json line 25: '@anthropic-ai/sdk': '^0.95.2'. Used in server/routes/suggestions.ts, server/services/usageLog.ts. recommender/pyproject.toml line 4 description: 'Replaces per-request Claude calls with a SQLite + sqlite-vec scoring pipeline.'
findings.recommendation: Confirm whether suggestions.ts still calls Anthropic directly or only routes through the recommender. If direct Claude calls are vestigial, remove the SDK and the outbound path; if intentionally retained (e.g., as a premium tier), document it and pin the pre-1.0 SDK to an exact version since 0.x can break across minors.

findings.title: Caret-pinned napi-rs CLI on the ABI-critical native-addon builder, plus install-time compile fallback
findings.description: @napi-rs/cli is pinned with a caret (^3.7.0) and the package's `prepare` lifecycle script will shell out to compile the .node from Rust when the prebuilt binary for the current platform/arch is missing. @napi-rs/cli emits the ABI-critical .node that the entire crypto/contracts boundary loads; a floating major-internal version on that builder is a reproducibility risk for the wire-format-sensitive binding. The Dockerfiles correctly pre-stage the prebuilt .node so prepare short-circuits in the prod image (good), and the napi-builder stage fetches '@napi-rs/cli@3' explicitly — but '@3' is itself a floating major. Any consumer running plain `npm install` without a Rust toolchain (a Node-only Docker stage or a contributor machine) will hit the prepare-time cargo build and fail.
findings.evidence: crates/emerald-contracts-napi/package.json: devDependencies '@napi-rs/cli': '^3.7.0'; prepare script execs 'node scripts/build-with-dts-guard.mjs build --platform --release' when the platform .node is absent. Dockerfile:65: 'npx --yes --package @napi-rs/cli@3 napi build --platform --release' (floating @3).
findings.recommendation: Pin @napi-rs/cli to an exact version in both the crate package.json and the Dockerfile invocation. Keep building the .node in the dedicated Rust stage and consuming the prebuilt artifact downstream so no consumer triggers prepare-time compilation.

findings.title: Rust MSRV (1.85) is lower than the CI/Docker toolchain (1.90) — the declared minimum is never actually tested
findings.description: The workspace declares rust-version = "1.85" as its MSRV, but every build path uses Rust 1.90 (CI: dtolnay/rust-toolchain toolchain: '1.90' in both the rust and recommender jobs; all Dockerfiles: rust:1.90-slim-bookworm). Nothing ever compiles against 1.85, so the declared MSRV is unverified and could already be wrong (a 1.86+ API could have crept in). This is minor for a private, you-control-the-toolchain product, but a stated MSRV that is never exercised is misleading documentation.
findings.evidence: Cargo.toml line 15: 'rust-version = "1.85"'. ci.yml lines 143 & 217: toolchain: '1.90'. All Dockerfiles: 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm'.
findings.recommendation: Either add an MSRV CI lane that builds with 1.85 to prove the floor, or raise rust-version to match the only toolchain you actually ship (1.90). Pin Docker base images by digest rather than the floating '1.90-slim-bookworm' tag for fully reproducible builds.

findings.title: Container base images and the static-ffmpeg image use floating tags, not digests
findings.description: All Dockerfile bases use floating tags: rust:1.90-slim-bookworm, node:24-slim, debian:bookworm-slim, python:3.12-slim, and the third-party mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1. Tag floats mean two builds of the 'same' Dockerfile can pull different underlying images as upstream re-pushes tags — a reproducibility and supply-chain-integrity gap. The ffmpeg image in particular (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1) is a third-party image that ships the exact GPL binaries at the heart of the licensing finding; pinning it by digest also pins the provenance of those binaries.
findings.evidence: Dockerfile:23 'FROM rust:1.90-slim-bookworm'; Dockerfile:68 'FROM node:24-slim'; Dockerfile:77 / media-core:30 / transcoder:30 'COPY --from=mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1'; recommender/Dockerfile:58 'FROM python:3.12-slim'.
findings.recommendation: Pin every FROM and COPY --from by sha256 digest (e.g., node:24-slim@sha256:...). For mwader/static-ffmpeg, record the digest alongside a note of which ffmpeg/x264 versions and license that digest corresponds to, feeding the THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES file.

findings.title: node-cron 3.x is an aging major on a scheduler in the reliability-critical path
findings.description: node-cron ^3.0.3 is pinned and IS used (server/services/iptvScheduler.ts, server/app.ts, server/env.ts) to drive scheduled IPTV refresh/reconciliation. The 3.x line is older with a quieter maintenance cadence and newer majors exist. For a server whose IPTV freshness depends on the scheduler, an aging-major scheduler is a latent reliability risk, and an unhandled throw inside a scheduled job can silently wedge the cron loop.
findings.evidence: package.json line 38: 'node-cron': '^3.0.3'; used in server/services/iptvScheduler.ts and registered in server/app.ts.
findings.recommendation: Evaluate upgrading node-cron or migrating to a more actively maintained scheduler (e.g., croner). Verify each scheduled job wraps its body in try/catch so a single failure does not kill the loop, and that failures report to Glitchtip.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Plex's distribution model is exactly the discipline this project needs and is currently missing. Plex runs ffmpeg server-side (on the self-hosted box the user already operates and never redistributes), and its iOS/tvOS clients rely on Apple's native VideoToolbox decode/encode — they do NOT bundle GPL x264 into the App-Store binary. This repo's server-side GPL ffmpeg + libx264 path (args.rs:46) is fine for the household-only server exactly as Plex does it; the latent encoder seam (args.rs HwEncoder::VideoToolbox) shows the team already anticipated the Apple-native path. The unresolved gap is the proprietary LICENSE's explicit App-Store framing (clause (b) names TestFlight/App Store) colliding with copyleft if ANY GPL component ever rides into the future M2 Apple client (which does not yet exist). To reach Plex parity on distribution: GPL stays strictly server-side and is never redistributed by household operators; the Apple clients must use VideoToolbox and bundle zero GPL code; and a NOTICE/THIRD-PARTY-LICENSES manifest must enumerate every OSS component (a routine App-Store review expectation Plex satisfies). Separately and orthogonally to code licensing, the IPTV/Xtream re-streaming feature carries a content-rights exposure that Plex deliberately does not take on in its first-party app — re-streaming third-party IPTV through an App-Store app is a product/legal decision the LICENSE does not address at all, and is arguably a larger App-Store-rejection risk than the codec licensing.

dossiers.purpose: Canonical cross-language crypto/contract crate: a Rust core plus an N-API binding consumed by the Hono/TypeScript backend and a PyO3 binding consumed by the Python recommender. Defines stream tokens (HMAC-SHA256 over a canonical JSON byte template), device tokens and internal-principal tokens (JWE alg:dir/enc:A256GCM, kid-aware multi-key verify), HKDF key derivation, sub-namespace parsing (plex/local/apple), and a telemetry PII scrubber. The tests/vectors/*.json files are intended to be the cross-language byte-parity oracle so Rust, TypeScript, and Python produce identical bytes.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: Sound RustCrypto primitives (AES-256-GCM with per-encrypt OsRng nonce, AAD-bound protected header, constant-time HMAC compare, kid-aware verify) with solid in-crate unit tests including the new BadTtl forged-token defense; held back from production-grade by the cross-language vector oracle only covering happy-path canonical bytes + HKDF (no rejection/tampering/expiry/kid-dispatch across languages), BadTtl being unmapped through both FFI bindings, a PyO3-vs-N-API surface asymmetry, FFI error/panic leakage, a 180-day device-token TTL with no in-crate revocation/replay primitive, and a scrub-sentinel divergence from the committed vector. NOTE: HEAD advanced from 44ff715 to 1e34db02 DURING this audit; an earlier-observed committed merge conflict in stream_token.rs was resolved by a concurrent session, and current HEAD compiles cleanly (RC=0).
what_works: The crate compiles cleanly at current HEAD (1e34db02): cargo build -p emerald-contracts returns RC=0; no conflict markers remain in working tree or HEAD blob (grep -c = 0).

JWE uses RustCrypto aes-gcm with a fresh 12-byte OsRng nonce per encrypt (jwe.rs:57), and the encoded protected header is bound as AAD on both encrypt and decrypt (jwe.rs:60, jwe.rs:136) so any header tamper invalidates the GCM tag. A nonce_uniqueness test guards against a deterministic-nonce regression (jwe.rs:174).
decode_protected_header hard-rejects any alg != 'dir' or enc != 'A256GCM' before attempting decryption (jwe.rs:100-102), with a rejects_wrong_alg test proving an attacker cannot swap to RSA-OAEP.

Device and internal-principal tokens are kid-aware: an unknown kid is rejected (UnknownKid) rather than brute-forcing all keys, and aud=='device' / iss=='eex' are re-validated AFTER decryption (device_token.rs:91-96).

Stream-token verify uses subtle::ConstantTimeEq for the MAC compare (stream_token.rs:247), and verify_dual_key computes BOTH primary and fallback HMACs unconditionally before branching on a bool (stream_token.rs:175-176), avoiding a short-circuit timing side channel across the grace window.

The resolved enforce_time_window caps token lifetime at MAX_TTL_SECS = 91 days (stream_token.rs:114, 192) and rejects a forged-forward nbf>iat && exp<nbf window as BadTtl (stream_token.rs:200), with a carefully-scoped condition that deliberately keeps a normally-expired token classified as Expired — and this logic IS unit-tested in-crate (stream_token.rs:415 bad_ttl_rejected covers over-cap, forged-forward-nbf, and the expired-stays-Expired guard).

canonical.rs is a hand-rolled fixed-template, alphabetical-order, whitespace-free JSON serializer for the HMAC input (deliberately NOT serde, which serializes in declaration order) with a surrogate-pair / 4-byte-UTF-8 test that matches Node's Buffer encoding.

HKDF derive_key matches Node hkdfSync('sha256', secret, '', info, 32) with a zero-length salt and domain-separated info constants per token type, with an info_strings_are_distinct test; hkdf-parity.json pins exact OKM hex and is actually executed by the vector harness.

parse_sub uses OnceLock-cached anchored per-provider regexes, correctly encodes the plex:0 anonymous-account exception, and rejects leading-zero plex ids (sub.rs:15, sub.rs:121); sub-namespace.json carries 14 positive+negative cases.

build-with-dts-guard.mjs is a pragmatic, well-documented workaround for the napi-rs v3 CLI clobbering the hand-authored index.d.ts — it snapshots, restores from git if pre-clobbered, and only writes back on shrink.

findings.title: BadTtl / MAX_TTL_SECS forged-token defense is unit-tested in Rust but unmapped through both FFI bindings and absent from the cross-language vector oracle
findings.description: The merge resolution that landed mid-audit added two security-relevant checks to enforce_time_window: an upper-bound TTL cap (exp - iat > MAX_TTL_SECS=91d => BadTtl) and a forged-window check (nbf > iat && exp < nbf => BadTtl). These ARE covered by a Rust unit test (stream_token.rs:415 bad_ttl_rejected, covering over-cap, forged-forward-nbf, and the expired-stays-Expired guard) — good. However the defense is Rust-only end-to-end: (1) BadTtl is never mapped through the N-API or PyO3 bindings (grep BadTtl|MAX_TTL in both binding src dirs returns nothing, rc=1); the napi stream_token_enforce_time_window simply format!("{:?}")s whatever error returns, with no contract that the TS/Python sides apply the SAME 91-day cap and forged-window rule. (2) There is no cross-language vector for it — tests/vectors.rs has only two test functions (canonical-bytes parity at line 37, HKDF parity at line 75), so the cap is never proven against the TypeScript verifier that actually gates content playback. A forged long-lived stream token is therefore defended only if the TS playback path independently re-implements the identical cap, which is unverified.
findings.evidence: stream_token.rs:192 `if claims.exp - claims.iat > MAX_TTL_SECS { return Err(TokenError::BadTtl); }`; stream_token.rs:200 `if claims.nbf > claims.iat && claims.exp < claims.nbf { return Err(TokenError::BadTtl); }`; stream_token.rs:114 `pub const MAX_TTL_SECS: i64 = 91 * 24 * 60 * 60;`. Unit test present: stream_token.rs:415 `fn bad_ttl_rejected()`. `grep -rnE 'BadTtl|MAX_TTL' crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src crates/emerald-contracts-pyo3/src` => no matches (rc=1). tests/vectors.rs has exactly two #[test] fns (lines 37, 75); no BadTtl vector.
findings.recommendation: Add a cross-language stream-token vector with expect:error_kind=BadTtl (over-cap exp-iat, and nbf>iat && exp<nbf) and verify the TypeScript verifier applies the identical 91-day cap and forged-window check; surface a stable BadTtl code through the napi/pyo3 bindings rather than an opaque debug-formatted string. Until the TS side is proven to match, the cap protects only the Rust services (media-core/transcoder), not the Hono playback grant path.

findings.title: Cross-language vector oracle does not exercise verify, expiry, tampering, dual-key, kid-dispatch, sub, or PII scrub — only canonical bytes + HKDF OKM
findings.description: The vector-driven cross-language gate contains exactly two tests: stream_token canonical_bytes + raw HMAC hex parity, and HKDF OKM hex parity. There is NO vector-driven Rust test for verify() rejecting a tampered signature, an expired/not-yet-valid token, wrong version (v!=1), unknown StreamKind, BadTtl, dual-key fallback, OR for device-token / internal-principal JWE decrypt across languages. device-token-kid-rotation.json, internal-principal.json, sub-namespace.json, and telemetry-pii-scrub.json all exist as committed vectors but are never loaded by vectors.rs — their negative checks (expired, wrong-iss, tampered-aad, unknown-kid, etc.) are encoded as JSON prose only. The adversarial paths are covered solely by in-crate Rust unit tests, so TypeScript or Python could diverge on rejection behavior and the oracle would stay green. For a product whose stream token is the only thing gating content delivery, 'Rust and TS agree on the happy-path HMAC' is necessary but nowhere near sufficient.
findings.evidence: vectors.rs defines exactly two #[test] fns: stream_token_canonical_vector_parity (line 37) and hkdf_parity_vector (line 75). load_vector is called only for stream-token-canonical.json and hkdf-parity.json; no call for device-token-kid-rotation.json, internal-principal.json, sub-namespace.json, or telemetry-pii-scrub.json. internal-principal.json's negative_checks (expired-rejected, wrong-iss-rejected, tampered-aad-rejects, unknown-kid-rejects) are JSON prose, not executed.
findings.recommendation: Add Rust (and mirrored TS/Python) loaders for every committed vector and assert REJECTION outcomes, not just bytes: tampered-sig->BadSignature, expired->Expired, nbf-future->NotYetValid, v=2->UnsupportedVersion, k=bogus->UnknownKind, over-cap->BadTtl, wrong-kid->UnknownKid, alg=RSA-OAEP header->BadHeader. Carry an explicit expect:{ok|error_kind} field per vector so all languages assert an identical error taxonomy.

findings.title: Device token TTL is 180 days with no in-crate replay or per-device revocation, and expiry enforcement is decoupled from decrypt
findings.description: DEFAULT_TTL_SECS is 180 days (device_token.rs:24). device_token::decrypt validates signature/aud/iss and returns claims, but enforce_time_window is a SEPARATE function (device_token.rs:102) the caller must remember to invoke — a caller who forgets accepts expired tokens. The crate offers no jti-based replay cache and no per-device revocation primitive; the only documented rotation mechanism is bumping the kid and dropping the old key from the verifier map, which revokes EVERY device on that key generation, not a single compromised device. A leaked 180-day device token is valid for up to 180 days with no in-crate kill switch, and because the canonical crate provides no replay/revocation helper, each language binding must re-implement it independently — exactly the divergence this crate exists to prevent. (A server-side tokenReplayCache exists elsewhere per the brief, but it is outside this crate and not shared with PyO3/Swift consumers.)
findings.evidence: device_token.rs:24 `pub const DEFAULT_TTL_SECS: i64 = 180 * 24 * 60 * 60;`. device_token.rs:80 decrypt() returns Ok(claims) after only aud/iss checks; device_token.rs:102 enforce_time_window(claims, now) is a distinct call. No jti/replay/revocation function in device_token.rs or internal_principal.rs.
findings.recommendation: Fold time-window enforcement into the verify/decrypt path (take `now` as a parameter) so expiry can never be skipped, provide a canonical jti-extraction + revocation-list-check helper so replay/per-device revocation is implemented once for all bindings, and document the per-device revocation story (kid-drop only supports bulk rotation).

findings.title: internal-principal time check has no nbf/iat bounds, uses strict-greater expiry, and has no req_id replay guard
findings.description: Internal principals cross the trust boundary from Hono to the Rust/Python services and assert the requestor's identity/role so those services skip their own auth. enforce_time_window only checks `now > exp` (strict >), with no nbf, no iat-floor (forward clock-skew), and no req_id replay cache. A captured internal-principal JWE can be replayed against media-core/transcoder/recommender for the full 60s window (and is accepted at exactly now==exp due to the strict >). The doc comment claims the principal 'must be fresh' but nothing enforces single-use.
findings.evidence: internal_principal.rs:81 `if now > claims.exp { return Err(InternalPrincipalError::Expired); }` is the entire time check — no nbf, no iat lower-bound, no req_id dedup. Comment at internal_principal.rs:75-76 asserts single-in-flight freshness that the code does not enforce.
findings.recommendation: Add a req_id replay guard (single-use per req_id within the TTL) and/or bind the principal to the specific request (method+path+body hash), reject iat too far in the future, and use `>=` against exp.

findings.title: Internal Rust error detail is leaked verbatim across the FFI boundary, including the attacker-supplied kid
findings.description: Every napi/pyo3 verify/decrypt error formats the internal Rust error enum directly into the message returned to the caller via format!("{:?}", e). This distinguishes BadSignature vs BadPayload vs Expired vs UnknownKid(kid) to the caller — including echoing the attacker-supplied kid back in the UnknownKid message. Surfacing whether a token failed on signature vs structure vs expiry is a classic oracle aiding forgery/enumeration, and echoing an attacker-controlled kid into a returned message (which frequently lands in logs) invites log injection/noise.
findings.evidence: napi src/lib.rs:108 `.map_err(|e| Error::from_reason(format!("verify failed: {:?}", e)))`; napi src/lib.rs:220 device decrypt `.map_err(|e| Error::from_reason(format!("{:?}", e)))` renders `UnknownKid("<attacker kid>")`; pyo3 src/lib.rs:124 `PyKeyError::new_err(format!("unknown kid: {}", kid))`.
findings.recommendation: Return a coarse, stable error code at the public FFI boundary (a single 'token invalid' for all signature/structure/expiry failures) while keeping the detailed enum for internal structured logging only. Never echo the attacker-supplied kid into a returned message.

findings.title: Telemetry scrub replacement sentinel diverges from the vector ("[Filtered]" vs "REDACTED") and is entirely untested
findings.description: telemetry-pii-scrub.json specifies the expected replacement as "REDACTED" (plexAuthToken -> "REDACTED", url ...t=REDACTED..., authorization -> "REDACTED", and a JWE_CIPHERTEXT case yielding "REDACTED."), but telemetry.rs emits "[Filtered]" for denied keys and "[Filtered:token]"/"[Filtered:bearer]"/"[Filtered:cookie]" for value-regex hits. Because vectors.rs never loads telemetry-pii-scrub.json, this divergence is completely UNTESTED. Either the vector is dead (authored against a different TS/Sentry-style impl) or there is a genuine cross-language scrub-output divergence that would silently break any consumer asserting on the redaction sentinel. Both are problems for a 'single source of truth' crate the contract relies on for MANDATORY Glitchtip PII safety (§15).
findings.evidence: telemetry.rs:87 `*v = Value::String("[Filtered]".to_string());`; telemetry.rs:115-123 replace_all with "[Filtered:token]"/"[Filtered:bearer]"/"[Filtered:cookie]". telemetry-pii-scrub.json `expected` blocks use "REDACTED" throughout. No load_vector("telemetry-pii-scrub.json") in tests/vectors.rs.
findings.recommendation: Reconcile the replacement sentinel to a single contract-fixed value across Rust/TS/Python, add a Rust loader for telemetry-pii-scrub.json, and assert exact scrubbed output so the constant cannot drift again.

findings.title: Telemetry token-redaction value regexes are narrow (eyJ prefix + only literal ?t=/&t= param)
findings.description: The PII value-regex for compact tokens requires the JWT-style `eyJ` prefix, and the stream-grant URL branch matches only the exact query param named `t` (`?t=`/`&t=`). A raw HMAC stream token captured mid-string without the `?t=` prefix, or a token under a differently-named param (`?token=`, `?grant=`, or path-embedded), is not redacted by the value regexes — only the key-match path (matches_pii_key) would catch it, and only when the secret sits under a denied key. Given that the contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15), the scrubber is the last line of defense and its freeform coverage is assumption-bound. (matches_pii_key itself is solid: substring, case-insensitive.)
findings.evidence: telemetry.rs:58-66 token_regex matches `(?:\?t=|&t=)[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.[A-Za-z0-9_-]+` (only param literally named t) OR `(?:eyJ[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\.){2,4}[A-Za-z0-9_-]+` (only eyJ-prefixed). matches_pii_key (telemetry.rs:107-110) lowercases the key and checks `contains` against PII_KEYS.
findings.recommendation: Broaden the URL token regex to any `[?&][a-z_]*t(oken)?=`/`grant=` param and to path-embedded grant tokens; add a generic two-segment HMAC-token pattern; add scrub vectors covering a bare stream token and a token under `?grant=` and assert redaction across all bindings.

findings.title: PyO3 binding omits stream_token and device_token surfaces yet its doc comment claims it mirrors the N-API binding
findings.description: The PyO3 module exports HKDF, parse_sub, PII scrub, and internal_principal (encrypt/decrypt/enforce). It does NOT export stream-token sign/verify or device-token encrypt/decrypt — those exist only in the N-API binding. The module doc comment claims the surface 'mirrors crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs so Hono + recommender call byte-identical code paths', which is false: the two binding surfaces are materially different. If the recommender ever needs to validate a stream or device token it must re-implement it in Python — precisely the cross-language drift this crate exists to prevent. This may be intentional scoping, but the comment overstates parity and the asymmetry is a latent trap.
findings.evidence: pyo3 src/lib.rs:9 comment 'Surface mirrors crates/emerald-contracts-napi/src/lib.rs so Hono + recommender call byte-identical code paths.' The #[pymodule] (pyo3 src/lib.rs:206-218) registers only hkdf_*, parse_sub, pii_scrub_*, internal_principal_* — no stream_token_* or device_token_*. The N-API binding exports stream_token_sign/verify/verify_dual_key and device_token_encrypt/decrypt.
findings.recommendation: Either add the missing surfaces to PyO3 or correct the doc comment to state the deliberate scope difference, and maintain a single table of which contract functions each binding must expose so drift is visible.

findings.title: Git merge-conflict markers were committed to stream_token.rs on the integration branch earlier in the audit (since resolved); no CI gate prevents recurrence
findings.description: At the start of this audit the committed HEAD (44ff715) of stream_token.rs contained raw unresolved git conflict markers inside enforce_time_window, which rustc rejects — making the canonical security crate and every downstream (Hono via N-API, recommender via PyO3, media-core, transcoder) fail to build. A concurrent session resolved it during the audit (HEAD advanced to 1e34db02; markers gone, crate builds RC=0, MAX_TTL_SECS=91d / BadTtl correctly defined and unit-tested). The residual finding is process, not current code: a security-foundation crate carried conflict markers on a shared integration branch, and there is no evidence of a CI gate that would have caught it. Reported MEDIUM because the current tree is clean, but the QA-gate gap remains real given multiple sessions commit concurrently.
findings.evidence: Early-session: `git grep -nE '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)'` matched stream_token.rs:176,177,194; `git show HEAD:...` (HEAD=44ff715) showed `<<<<<<< HEAD`/`=======`/`>>>>>>> c640b04 (fix(stream-token): scope BadTtl forged-window check ...)`; first `cargo build` failed `error: encountered diff marker --> stream_token.rs:176:1`. Later: HEAD=1e34db02, marker count 0 in worktree and HEAD blob, build RC=0.
findings.recommendation: Add a required pre-merge CI gate running `cargo build --workspace` AND `git grep -nE '^(<<<<<<<|=======|>>>>>>>)'` (fail on any match) so conflict markers can never reach a shared branch again.

findings.title: JWE protected-header parsing is non-strict: no deny_unknown_fields and no crit handling
findings.description: ProtectedHeaderOwned deserializes only alg/enc/kid and silently ignores any additional members, including a JOSE `crit` (critical) header. A token minted by a buggy/future producer carrying extra security-relevant headers would be silently accepted by older verifiers, defeating the purpose of `crit`. Exploitability is limited because the full encoded header is bound as AAD (an attacker cannot add parameters to an existing valid token without breaking the tag), but for a crate that aims to be the canonical multi-language verifier, header strictness should be explicit, not incidental.
findings.evidence: jwe.rs:36-41 ProtectedHeaderOwned { alg, enc, kid } with default serde (ignores unknown fields); jwe.rs:100 only checks `hdr.alg != "dir" || hdr.enc != "A256GCM"`. No #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] and no crit handling.
findings.recommendation: Add #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to the header struct (or explicitly reject any header containing crit/zip/epk) and add a vector with an extra header parameter asserting rejection across languages.
