findings.title: parse_sub runs regexes on unbounded FFI-supplied input with no length cap
findings.description: parse_sub is exposed across both N-API and PyO3 and receives caller/attacker-controlled strings. The regex crate is linear (no catastrophic backtracking), so ReDoS is not the concern, but there is no max-length guard before evaluation: a multi-megabyte input runs the linear regex over the whole string and heap-allocates copies (to_string) on every auth call. A security-boundary parser exposed to FFI should cap input length defensively.
findings.evidence: sub.rs:65 parse_sub immediately does `s.contains(':')` then `plex_re().is_match(s)` on the full untrusted `s` with no length cap; rest.to_string()/s.to_string() allocate the full input. PLEX_REGEX `^plex:(0|[1-9][0-9]*)$` scans an arbitrarily long digit run.
findings.recommendation: Add an early length cap (e.g. `if s.len() > 256 { return Err(SubError::InvalidFormat) }`) before regex evaluation, plus an oversized-input vector.

findings.title: encrypt()/sign() use .expect() panics reachable from the FFI boundary
findings.description: jwe::encrypt calls serde_json::to_vec(&header).expect(...) and cipher.encrypt(...).expect(...); device_token::encrypt and internal_principal::encrypt call serde_json::to_vec(claims).expect(...); stream_token::sign and compute_and_compare call Hmac::new_from_slice(...).expect(...). Most are genuinely unreachable, but all are reachable from #[napi]/#[pyfunction] exports. A panic in this context unwinds into the Node/Python runtime; napi-rs/pyo3 generally convert panics into thrown errors, but crypto paths reachable from foreign runtimes should return Result rather than panic.
findings.evidence: jwe.rs:53 .expect("static header serializes"); jwe.rs:69 .expect("aes-gcm encrypt with random nonce should not fail"); device_token.rs:64 .expect("DeviceClaims serializes"); stream_token.rs:147 and stream_token.rs:233 .expect("HMAC-SHA256 accepts any-length keys"). All reachable via the binding exports (e.g. device_token_encrypt -> device_token::encrypt -> jwe::encrypt).
findings.recommendation: Convert the encrypt/sign paths to return Result and map errors at the FFI layer; avoid .expect on any function reachable from FFI.

findings.title: device_token_decrypt rebuilds the kid->key HashMap on every call with no key-count cap
findings.description: device_token_decrypt accepts a Vec<KidKey> from JS and rebuilds a HashMap on every single token verification (allocation + hashing per call) instead of reusing a cached verifier, and there is no bound on the number of keys a caller may pass. Minor at homelab scale, but the stated target is App-Store scale with many concurrent streams; per-request HashMap reconstruction on the hot device-auth path is avoidable overhead.
findings.evidence: napi src/lib.rs:209-218 `let mut map: HashMap<String,[u8;32]> = HashMap::new(); for kk in keys { ... map.insert(kk.kid, arr); }` executed inside the per-call device_token_decrypt export.
findings.recommendation: Expose a verifier-handle object (build the key map once, reuse across verifies) or document that the JS side must memoize; bound the number of kids accepted.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: This crate is internal security infrastructure, not a user-facing media feature, so it has no direct Plex feature to match. Its relevance to the Plex-replacement goal is that it is the cross-language trust foundation a multi-client (tvOS/iOS/web) Plex-grade system needs: stream-grant tokens (equivalent to Plex's signed transcode/stream URLs), per-device tokens, internal service-to-service principals, and a sub-namespace that models identity across plex/local/apple (a deliberate superset of Plex, which only models Plex accounts). The cryptographic design choices (AES-256-GCM AEAD, kid rotation, constant-time MAC compares, canonical-byte HMAC, a 91-day stream-token cap) are appropriate and in places stronger than necessary. Gaps that matter for Plex-equivalent trust at App-Store scale: no token revocation/replay primitive in the crate (Plex supports per-device de-authorization; here device tokens live 180 days with only bulk kid-rotation); the new forged-token TTL cap is Rust-only, unmapped through the bindings, and unverified on the TypeScript playback path that actually gates content; and the byte-parity oracle never exercises rejection/tampering/expiry/kid-dispatch across languages. Close these before relying on this crate as the security spine of a public multi-device product.

dossiers.subsystem: Web SPA — Shell, Router, API Client & Auth
dossiers.purpose: The foundation layer of the React 19 + Vite SPA that is the project's real, primary client: app shell + provider tree (main.tsx -> App.tsx: StrictMode/QueryClientProvider/ConfirmProvider/AuthProvider with a lazy-loaded hash-routed tab shell), hash router (lib/router.ts), TanStack Query config (lib/queryClient.ts), the auth context implementing Plex PIN + Apple SIWA + WebAuthn passkey login/register over server-side cookie sessions (lib/auth.tsx), the fetch base + typed error envelope (lib/api/base.ts, errors.ts), eight per-domain API clients (iptv/media/sonarr/radarr/sab/tmdb/grabs/recommenderEvents), 27 data hooks bridging those into React Query, and two cosmetic helpers (navTransition, animatedFavicon).
dossiers.maturity_rationale: Shell, hash router, query config, and three real auth flows (Plex PIN polling, Apple SIWA, WebAuthn passkey via @simplewebauthn/browser) are coherently implemented against matching server routes, with consistent credentialed fetch, a typed ApiError envelope, namespaced query keys, visibility-gated polling, and unit tests on the pure logic — but it is NOT production-grade: zero React error boundaries, zero client error telemetry despite the mandatory-telemetry contract, no centralized mid-session 401 handling, no request timeouts on GETs, and GET clients throw opaque unnormalized errors on empty/non-JSON bodies.
what_works: Three real, end-to-end auth flows wired to matching server routes: Plex PIN popup with 5-min deadline + 1.5s polling (signIn -> /api/auth/plex/pin + /api/auth/plex/check), Apple SIWA (appleSignIn -> /api/auth/apple), and full WebAuthn passkey login AND registration via @simplewebauthn/browser (passkeyLogin/passkeyRegister -> /api/auth/passkey/{login,register}/{options,verify}). Verified mounts: app.ts:104 /api/auth, :111 /api/auth/passkey, :112 /api/me; GET /api/me hydrates the session.

Session model is a server-side HttpOnly cookie, not a JS-readable bearer: auth.tsx only mirrors identity for UI gating and uses credentials:'include' throughout. localStorage holds only non-secret UI prefs (viewAs role, nav-transition flag, AI toggle) and the per-user BYO Anthropic key — never the session credential.
applyUser() calls queryClient.clear() synchronously on every identity change, correctly preventing cross-user cache leakage on shared devices — a well-reasoned correctness fix.

Auth concurrency guards are careful: signInInFlightRef blocks double sign-in; popup/poll interval ownership is tracked per attempt (stopCurrentPoll, popupRef === popup checks); the poll distinguishes popup-closed, deadline-expired, 403-denied, 4xx, and authorized with specific user copy.

apiFetch error normalization (throwApiError) is strong: maps domain codes (insufficient_disk_space, default_quality_profile_missing, rootfolder_unreachable, 507 free_space_unknown, 401 session-expired) to specific human copy and preserves status/code/details on a typed ApiError; covered by errors.test.ts and per-client tests.

Polling hooks are visibility-aware where it matters: useDownloadQueue/useSonarrQueue/useRadarrQueue gate refetchInterval on useDocumentVisible() and set refetchIntervalInBackground:false; useIptvSessions also sets refetchIntervalInBackground:false.

Query keys are consistently namespaced/parameterized; dependent queries gate with enabled (seriesId != null, debounced.length >= 2, ids.length > 0); optimistic mutations (favorites, feedback) snapshot+rollback correctly and invalidate the right keys onSettled.

animatedFavicon is well-engineered: throttled to 14fps (not per-frame), pauses on document.hidden via visibilitychange, skips under navigator.webdriver to avoid crashing headless CI, falls back to the static SVG on WebGL init failure.
useUserApiKey hashes the BYO key (djb2) into a non-secret fingerprint for query keys, keeping plaintext out of cache keys/logs, and handles a one-time legacy-key migration.
usePlexLinks ships a pure, unit-tested resolver (resolvePlexLink) with a tmdb->tvdb->imdb->search fallback chain and degraded-mode behavior when the server map is unavailable.

CSRF posture is coherent: server applies requireSafeOrigin globally (app.ts:66) and the SPA's state-changing calls always send credentials with a matching Origin; suggestions GET opts into requireTrustedOrigin to protect its side effects.

findings.title: No React error boundary anywhere — any render throw or failed lazy chunk white-screens the entire SPA with no recovery
findings.description: The provider tree is StrictMode > QueryClientProvider > ConfirmProvider > App, and App.tsx adds AuthProvider + a lazy tab router; none is an error boundary. A repo-wide grep for ErrorBoundary|componentDidCatch|getDerivedStateFromError across src/ returns zero matches. Components consume API data via blind `as T` casts (every client's `res.json() as Promise<T>`), so any unexpected shape, any throw in a tab/detail/player, or a lazy-import chunk load failure (App.tsx lazy() has only a Suspense loading fallback, no error fallback) unmounts the whole React tree to a blank page with no reload affordance. For a Netflix-grade UI bound for Apple TV/iOS this is the worst UX failure mode.
findings.evidence: main.tsx renders <App/> with no boundary; App.tsx: `const TvTab = lazy(() => import('./components/tabs/TvTab')...)` wrapped only in `<Suspense fallback={<LoadingPulse>Loading</LoadingPulse>}>`; `grep -rn 'ErrorBoundary|componentDidCatch|getDerivedStateFromError' src/` => 0 results.
findings.recommendation: Add a top-level ErrorBoundary around <App/> (fallback with a reload action), plus a per-tab boundary inside Shell around <ActiveTab/> so one crashing tab doesn't kill the shell, and handle lazy-chunk load errors (stale deploy) with a reload prompt.

findings.title: Client has zero error/crash telemetry — violates the §15 mandatory-telemetry contract on the web tier
findings.description: The contract makes Glitchtip telemetry mandatory (§15); the server initializes @sentry/node (server/index.ts:36-46) and even exposes a DSN-distribution endpoint at /api/telemetry (app.ts:167). The web client wires NOTHING: no @sentry/react, no Sentry.init, no window.onerror, no window.onunhandledrejection. A repo-wide grep for Sentry/@sentry in src/ returns only an unrelated CSS comment. Combined with the absent error boundary, every client crash, failed lazy import, and unhandled rejection is invisible to operators — production failures on remote NAS/TV/phone clients are undiagnosable. recommenderEvents.postClickEvent even swallows its own errors with `.catch(() => {})`.
findings.evidence: `grep -rn 'Sentry|@sentry|onunhandledrejection|window.onerror' src/` => only MediaCard.css comment; server/index.ts:37 `Sentry.init({ dsn: env.EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, ... })` exists server-side only; api/recommenderEvents.ts:17 `}).catch(() => {})`.
findings.recommendation: Initialize the Glitchtip/Sentry browser SDK in main.tsx using the DSN the server already serves at /api/telemetry, feed the new ErrorBoundary into it, and add a window.onunhandledrejection capture. Required by the contract, not optional.

findings.title: No centralized 401/403 handler — an expired cookie session leaves a silently broken UI
findings.description: queryClient configures only staleTime/retry/refetchOnWindowFocus — no QueryCache or MutationCache onError. errors.ts maps 401 to 'Your session expired' copy but there is NO global interceptor (grep for isAuthError in src/ returns zero usages; it isn't even defined in this errors.ts but no equivalent global handler exists). The session cookie can expire mid-session; auth.tsx probes /api/me only ONCE on mount and never re-validates, so after expiry every query/mutation 401s independently, each component shows its own local error or nothing, and the user is never bounced to login or signed out. Result: a dead-looking dashboard requiring a manual reload.
findings.evidence: queryClient.ts has no cache-level callbacks; auth.tsx session probe is a single `useEffect(() => { fetch(apiUrl('/api/me'), ...) }, [applyUser])` with no refetch/re-validation; no data-query path calls signOut()/applyUser(null) on a 401.
findings.recommendation: Add a QueryCache/MutationCache onError to queryClient (or a check in apiFetch) that, on 401/unauthenticated, calls applyUser(null) so AuthGate re-renders login/walkthrough. Re-probe /api/me on window focus or a timer to detect expiry proactively.

findings.title: GET clients have no request timeout or AbortController — a hung request pins a query in 'pending' forever and never forwards React Query's signal
findings.description: Every client GET path calls fetch() with only { credentials:'include' } — no signal, no timeout. Only sonarr.ts/radarr.ts POSTs have a 60s AbortController; all GETs (iptv, media, sab, tmdb, grabs, suggestions, trending, recently-added, feedback, limits, plex-links) and the auth fetches have none. On the flaky NAS-over-Cloudflare-tunnel network this project explicitly fights (latest commit: 'tunnel-jitter rebuffering'), a stalled connection never rejects, so React Query stays 'pending' indefinitely — spinners that never resolve — and visibility-gated pollers can stack overlapping in-flight requests. React Query's queryFn AbortSignal is never forwarded, so cancelled queries keep their sockets open.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:18 `const res = await fetch(apiUrl(...), { credentials: 'include' })` — no signal; identical in media.ts:16, sab.ts:8, tmdb.ts:37, grabs.ts:35, useTrending.ts (inline), useRecentlyAdded.ts (inline), useLimits.ts:45; only sonarr.ts:15-24 / radarr.ts:15-24 POSTs add an AbortController.
findings.recommendation: Add a default AbortController timeout in a shared apiFetch and forward React Query's queryFn `signal` so reads fail fast (retry/error) instead of hanging, and so cancelled queries abort their network calls.

findings.title: GET clients do `res.json() as T` with no empty-body or parse-error guard — a 204/non-JSON 2xx throws an opaque, unnormalized error
findings.description: Every GET helper returns `res.json() as Promise<T>` gating only on !res.ok. A 200/204 with an empty or non-JSON body reaches res.json() and throws a raw SyntaxError that is NOT an ApiError and bypasses throwApiError's normalization, so hooks surface an opaque error (and with no error boundary, potentially a white screen if it reaches render). The iptv POST helper handles this correctly (`const text = await res.text(); return text ? JSON.parse(text) : undefined`) but the GET helpers across media/sab/sonarr/radarr/tmdb/grabs do not. There is also zero runtime schema validation (no zod) on any response, so a backend shape change surfaces as a render-time crash rather than a handled error.
findings.evidence: media.ts:20 `return res.json() as Promise<T>`; iptv.ts:22, sab.ts:10, sonarr.ts:11, radarr.ts:11, grabs.ts:37, tmdb.ts:44 same pattern; contrast iptv.ts:37-38 POST which DOES guard empty bodies.
findings.recommendation: Centralize reads through one apiFetch that tolerates empty/204 bodies and wraps JSON.parse failures as ApiError. For high-value responses (auth, library, suggestions) validate shape with zod so drift fails loudly and safely.

findings.title: useCast is a TMDB cast-list hook, not a Chromecast controller — casting to a TV does not exist in this layer
findings.description: Despite the name, useCast is a TanStack Query hook for TMDB cast/crew (useCast(args) -> fetchCast). The 'cast to TV' capability a Plex replacement needs is absent from the entire reviewed shell/hooks layer: no Cast Application Framework integration (no SDK load, no requestSession, no media load) and no AirPlay path for the Apple targets. For a 'full Plex replacement' bound for Apple TV/iOS, casting from the web client is a baseline expectation.
findings.evidence: useCast.ts body is entirely `export function useCast(args: Args) { ... fetchCast(...) }` returning CastMember[]; no window.cast / chrome.cast reference anywhere in reviewed files.
findings.recommendation: If casting is in scope, implement a real Cast/AirPlay hook separately; if not, ensure no UI presents a dead cast control. Rename useCast to useTmdbCast to remove the misleading name.

findings.title: Hash router has no guard layer — route protection is hand-rolled in the shell and duplicated
findings.description: lib/router.ts is a minimal hash store with no concept of guarded routes. Protection is hand-rolled in Shell: a useEffect that navigate('home')s for users/live/media when not permitted, PLUS a parallel `blocked` boolean and `effectiveRoute` fallback to avoid a flash. It works but is fragile — guard logic is duplicated (effect + blocked computation), every new gated route must update BOTH, and the gating contract lives in component code rather than the router. (Server-side authZ is correctly the source of truth via requireAdmin etc., so this is a maintainability risk, not an authZ bypass.)
findings.evidence: App.tsx:66-70 effect `if (route === 'users' && !isAdmin) navigate('home')...` duplicated by :71-74 `const blocked = (route === 'users' && !isAdmin) || ...` and :75 `const effectiveRoute = blocked ? 'home' : route`.
findings.recommendation: Centralize route metadata (requiredRole, requiredFlag) and derive both the redirect and the render fallback from one table so a new gated route can't be half-protected.

findings.title: Discover/Home rail hooks throw bare Errors that bypass the ApiError envelope
findings.description: useSuggested (via SuggestionsError), useTrending, useRecentlyAdded, useUserFeedback, and useLimits use inline fetch and throw plain Errors (or a custom SuggestionsError) instead of throwApiError, so the rich ApiError mapping (session-expired copy, code/details) is lost for these surfaces — the user gets a raw 'suggestions movie: 500'. With no error boundary and no cache onError, a persistent 500 on the Home rails renders whatever the consuming component does with isError. (Note: useSuggested DOES correctly disable retry on 4xx, which is good.)
findings.evidence: useSuggested.ts:131 `throw new SuggestionsError(r.status, body)`; useUserFeedback.ts:32 `throw new Error(\`feedback ${r.status}: ...\`)`; useTrending/useRecentlyAdded inline same pattern — unlike the iptv/media/sonarr clients which call throwApiError.
findings.recommendation: Route these inline fetches through the same apiFetch/throwApiError path the typed clients use so error copy and typing are consistent, and ensure each rail renders a retryable ErrorState.

findings.title: auth.test.ts covers only pure helpers + admin fetches — the security-critical React auth flows are untested
findings.description: The test file explicitly scopes itself to authModeFromUser, deniedMessage, and the admin invite/member fetches, noting 'The React context (signIn/appleSignIn) is exercised indirectly via the component tests.' The most security-sensitive logic — the Plex PIN poll lifecycle (deadline, popup-closed, 403-denied, interval ownership), the Apple SIWA POST branches, and the WebAuthn login/register ceremonies including @simplewebauthn/browser cancellation paths — has no direct unit coverage in this layer. These are the gate to the whole platform, so indirect-only coverage is thin.
findings.evidence: auth.test.ts:14-16 comment 'The React context (signIn/appleSignIn) is exercised indirectly via the component tests.'; the 8 it() blocks cover only helpers + admin API.
findings.recommendation: Add focused tests for signIn poll state transitions (fake timers + mocked popup), appleSignIn 403/401/ok branches, and passkeyLogin/passkeyRegister with a mocked @simplewebauthn/browser.

findings.title: navTransition home-reset effect suppresses exhaustive-deps and recreates its context value unmemoized
findings.description: The mount effect reads `route` to decide whether to clear the nav-transition-played gate but uses `[]` deps with an eslint-disable for react-hooks/exhaustive-deps. The intent (reset only on a real page load while on home) is documented, but suppressing the rule instead of capturing the mount-time route is fragile; additionally the provider's context value `{ transitionTo, navigate, replay }` is a fresh object every render, forcing all consumers to re-render on any provider state change. Minor, but indicative of lint being suppressed rather than satisfied.
findings.evidence: navTransition.tsx:79 `// eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` on a `}, [])` effect that reads `route`; context value object literal recreated each render at :128.
findings.recommendation: Read the route once at mount (ref or parseHash) instead of disabling the rule, and useMemo the context value.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Correction note: the web auth flows ARE real and wired — Plex PIN, Apple SIWA, and WebAuthn passkey all hit matching server routes (server/auth.ts exports `auth`+`me`, mounted at app.ts:104/112; passkey at :111), and GET /api/me hydrates the session. So sign-in works; an earlier draft that claimed otherwise was wrong. Versus Plex, the genuine gaps in THIS layer are: (1) Casting/AirPlay to a TV is absent — useCast here is a TMDB cast-list hook, not a Chromecast controller; a Plex replacement needs real cast support for the Apple TV/iOS targets. (2) Resilience is below Plex's web client: no error boundary and no client telemetry mean a single render error blanks the whole app and operators are blind, where Plex degrades per-panel. (3) No centralized session-expiry handling, so a lapsed session yields a dead UI rather than graceful re-auth. (4) Continue-watching/resume-progress is only present for IPTV (useIptvHistory + useReportPosition at a 5s cadence); there is no unified library playback-progress hook spanning media-core/Plex sources, which Plex treats as core. Architecturally the data layer (namespaced TanStack keys, cursor pagination for large IPTV catalogs, cookie sessions, per-user cache clearing, Plex deep-link resolver, BYO-key fingerprinting) is a sound and in places more ambitious base than Plex's; the parity blockers here are casting, resilience/observability, and unified resume state — on top of the roadmap-known fact that the native Apple clients this UI is meant to feed do not exist yet.

dossiers.purpose: Whole-repo operability audit (observability/Glitchtip wiring + PII scrubber, graceful shutdown + in-flight/stream/ffmpeg drain, config fail-fast, external-call timeouts/retries/backpressure, resource cleanup, health vs readiness probes, SQLite backup/restore, migration safety) of theemeraldexchange on branch m3-media-core.
dossiers.maturity_rationale: This is the corrected, final dossier; it supersedes BOTH my earlier StructuredOutput calls this session (the first wrongly claimed the environment was compromised — a misread of a malformed `base64` invocation; the second under-counted evidence). The repo is genuine (544 files, canary-confirmed) and I have now read 17 operability-critical files verbatim across all four runtimes (Node, Rust x2, Python). VERDICT: 'functional', not production-grade. The codebase shows real operational discipline in specific places — boot-time config fail-fast (env.ts) is excellent, EVERY external call I traced (Sonarr/Radarr/SAB/Plex/TMDB/recommender/media-core) routes through a single AbortController-timeout wrapper, the PII scrubber is thorough and wired into both Sentry hooks, per-session ffmpeg lifecycle has SIGTERM-then-SIGKILL escalation + idle sweeper + tmpdir cleanup, and destructive migrations are refused unless a recent backup exists. But the operability layer that matters at App-Store scale and under many concurrent streams has confirmed, code-level gaps: (1) Sentry is initialized but has NO Hono request/error handler, so handler exceptions never reach Glitchtip despite the mandatory-telemetry contract; (2) both Rust services (transcoder, media-core) handle ONLY ctrl_c/SIGINT and IGNORE the SIGTERM Docker sends, so every redeploy SIGKILLs in-flight transcodes; (3) the Node shutdown handler process.exit()s without server.close() or stream/ffmpeg drain, and competes with a SECOND signal handler in iptvScheduler; (4) NEITHER Rust service nor the Python recommender has ANY Sentry/Glitchtip wiring, so 3 of 4 runtimes emit zero crash telemetry; (5) /api/health is a static literal and compose has zero healthchecks; (6) backups are manual + migration-gated only — no scheduled/automated backup, and a project audit doc records the Python backup gate as silently weakened with 2 RED tests.
what_works: Config fail-fast (env.ts) is the standout strength. required()/positiveNumber()/positiveInt() throw at boot on missing/malformed values, explicitly because NaN would silently disable the disk-space safety gate (68-82). Prod gates fail CLOSED: ALLOWED_ORIGINS required or CSRF fails open (103-108); PLEX_SERVER_ID required unless ALLOW_UNSCOPED_PLEX_LOGIN=1 is explicit (121-131); SESSION_SECRET/RECOMMENDER_EVENT_SECRET enforce a 32-char minimum and reject placeholders like 'changeme' (159-213); EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN required in prod (267-274); assertSecretsDistinct runs in ALL envs (250-260).

Outbound-call timeout discipline is consistent and real. upstream.ts wraps every call in an AbortController (LAN 15s / WAN 10s), clears the timer in finally (60-62, 86-88), and maps AbortError/network errors to a synthesized 504 so a timeout never becomes an unhandled rejection (44-59). Confirmed used by the sidecar callers too: recommender.ts imports and calls fetchWithTimeout (15, 106) and media.ts routes the media-core proxy through it (media.ts 4, 70). Internal calls are NOT raw fetch.

The PII scrubber is thorough and correctly wired. telemetryPiiScrub.ts deep-walks the whole event without mutating input (151-176), redacts EEX keys + Sentry's 22-entry denylist via case-insensitive substring (43-86), strips stream-grant t=<token> and JWE ciphertext from strings (127-149), and rewrites Authorization/Cookie header values (178-211). Installed into BOTH beforeSend and beforeBreadcrumb (index.ts 41-42); the breadcrumb hook specifically closes the XHR-data.url token-leak class (243-274). setSentryUser() throws on persistent IDs to protect the App Store linked=No label (105-125).

Per-session ffmpeg lifecycle in iptvRemux.ts is genuinely well-built: SIGTERM then SIGKILL-after-5s escalation (84-101) with unref'd timers, an idle sweeper that reaps sessions after 30s no-heartbeat (104-112), tmpdir creation + best-effort recursive cleanup on stop/error/exit (51-57, 140-149), and stderr scrubbed for Xtream creds before logging (136-141). This is the most production-aware module reviewed.

ffmpeg is validated at boot (validateFfmpegOrExit) with a 5s ffprobe timeout, ENOENT/version handling across 4 real-world version-string families, and process exit on failure (ffmpeg.ts 106-171; index.ts 25-31).

SQLite opener sets sane pragmas — busy_timeout=5000, WAL, synchronous=NORMAL, foreign_keys=ON — and applies migrations transactionally with BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK per file plus a slow-migration warning (db.ts 18-22, 119-137). Migrations are idempotent (applied-version set) and tolerate three legacy migration-table shapes.

A backup safety gate DOES exist for the dangerous path: migrator.ts refuses any `-- DESTRUCTIVE` migration unless server_state.last_backup_at is within 10 minutes, with a clear operator message pointing at POST /api/admin/backup (migrator.ts 54-92, 283-285). This is a thoughtful guard most homelab projects lack.

The recommender's FastAPI lifespan is correct: migrate-on-start, an AsyncIOScheduler + retention sweeper task started on boot and cleanly cancelled/awaited on shutdown with suppress(CancelledError) (main.py 74-91); per-request SQLite connections opened/closed via a generator dependency to avoid cross-request cursor races (96-109); the retention sweeper wraps work in try/except with log.exception (65-71) rather than a bare except. Its /health actually queries the DB (titles/vectors/active model) — a real readiness signal (122-135).

A one-way crypto-debt deadline guard refuses boot on/after 2026-06-25 if the legacy SHA-256 path was not removed (index.ts 55-65). Network posture is sound: all long-lived compose services use restart: unless-stopped and bind 127.0.0.1 only; transcoder HLS scratch is a size-capped tmpfs that auto-clears (docker-compose 239-240). Internal-principal verification has staged off/log/enforce knobs with a documented rollout path (155-161, 199-202).

findings.title: Sentry/Glitchtip initialized but NOT wired to capture Hono request errors — handler exceptions silently dropped despite mandatory-telemetry contract (§15)
findings.description: Sentry.init() runs at boot but the ONLY integration is onUnhandledRejectionIntegration. Grep across all non-test server .ts confirms Sentry appears only in index.ts (init) and telemetryPiiScrub.ts (setUser wrapper) — zero captureException, onError, requestHandler, or setupHono anywhere. In @sentry/node v9 with Hono, init does not auto-instrument route handlers; an exception thrown inside any /api/* handler becomes Hono's default 500 and is NEVER sent to Glitchtip. The contract makes telemetry MANDATORY (§15.1) and env.ts hard-fails prod boot without a DSN — yet the primary error class (handler exceptions) is uncaptured. Declared-but-effectively-unwired observability for the most important signal.
findings.evidence: index.ts 43-45: `integrations: [ Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration({ mode: 'warn' }) ]` is the only integration. app.ts global middleware is only `app.use('*', logger())` (36) and `app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin)` (66); no app.onError exists. Grep for captureException/onError/requestHandler/setupHono across non-test server *.ts: zero matches.
findings.recommendation: Register `app.onError((err, c) => { Sentry.captureException(err); return c.json({ error: 'internal' }, 500) })` in app.ts (or the framework Sentry handler), add a test that throws in a route and asserts captureException fired, and flush Sentry on shutdown. Set tracesSampleRate if any latency data is wanted (currently none).

findings.title: Rust transcoder AND media-core handle only ctrl_c (SIGINT); both IGNORE the SIGTERM Docker sends — every redeploy SIGKILLs in-flight transcodes after the grace period
findings.description: Both services pass `with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal())` where shutdown_signal is `let _ = tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await`. tokio::signal::ctrl_c registers ONLY SIGINT. Docker/compose stop sends SIGTERM, which neither service handles — confirmed by grep: zero SignalKind/signal::unix/terminate matches in either crate. So in production the graceful path NEVER fires; the container runs until Docker's ~10s grace elapses, then SIGKILL hard-aborts every in-flight ffmpeg transcode mid-segment on every `docker compose up -d`. Identical defect in both Rust binaries.
findings.evidence: transcoder/src/main.rs 54 `.with_graceful_shutdown(shutdown_signal())`, 59-62 `async fn shutdown_signal() { let _ = tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await; ... }`. media-core/src/main.rs 54/59-62 identical. Grep `SignalKind|signal::unix|terminate` across crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src returned zero matches.
findings.recommendation: Use tokio::signal::unix to await BOTH SIGINT and SIGTERM via select! before invoking axum graceful shutdown; ensure spawned ffmpeg sessions get SIGTERM with a bounded grace then SIGKILL. Apply to both crates.

findings.title: Node graceful shutdown does not drain in-flight requests/streams or kill ffmpeg children, and a SECOND competing signal handler races it
findings.line: 72-102 (+ iptvScheduler.ts implicit cron, iptvRemux.ts sessions Map)
findings.description: The index.ts SIGTERM/SIGINT handler closes the two SQLite singletons then immediately process.exit()s. It never (a) stops the listener — serve()'s returned http.Server is DISCARDED at line 72 so there is no handle to .close(); (b) awaits in-flight requests; (c) drains the iptvRemux sessions Map (which holds live ffmpeg children); or (d) stops the node-cron tasks. iptvRemux DOES have per-session SIGTERM->SIGKILL teardown, but shutdown() never calls stopRemuxSession for the live sessions, so on deploy every live viewer is hard-killed and ffmpeg children are orphaned (the 5s SIGKILL timer is unref'd, so process.exit can fire before it runs). The cron tasks registered in iptvScheduler are never stopped, so a sync mid-write to iptv.db can race the closeIptvDb() call in shutdown.
findings.evidence: index.ts 72 `serve({ fetch: app.fetch, port: env.port }, ...)` return discarded; 93-99 `function shutdown(signal){ closeIptvDb(); closeServerDb(); process.exit(...) }`. iptvRemux.ts maintains `const sessions = new Map(...)` (45) with live `proc: ChildProcess` (27) never drained on shutdown. iptvScheduler.ts cron.schedule tasks (29, 34) are never cancelled.
findings.recommendation: Capture `const server = serve(...)`; in one centralized shutdown: stop accepting new streams, server.close() and await in-flight with a bounded timeout, stop cron tasks, iterate sessions and stopRemuxSession() each (awaiting child exit), THEN closeIptvDb()/closeServerDb()/exit. Replace the unref'd SIGKILL timer with an awaited bounded kill during drain.

findings.title: No crash telemetry in 3 of 4 runtimes — both Rust services and the Python recommender have ZERO Sentry/Glitchtip wiring despite §15 making it mandatory
findings.description: The contract makes Glitchtip telemetry MANDATORY (§15). Only the Node backend initializes Sentry. Grep found NO sentry/sentry-sdk references in crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src or their Cargo.toml, and NONE in recommender/app or pyproject. The Rust services use tracing_subscriber to stdout only; the Python service uses logging.basicConfig to stdout only. So a panic in media-core/transcoder or an unhandled 500 in the recommender produces zero events in Glitchtip — the operator is blind to crashes in the entire media-serving and recommendation path, which is exactly where production failures will concentrate (ffmpeg, scanning, vector queries).
findings.evidence: Grep `sentry|glitchtip` across crates/{media-core,transcoder}/src + Cargo.toml: only tracing_subscriber::fmt() (media-core/main.rs 10, transcoder/main.rs 7). Grep `sentry|sentry_sdk|glitchtip` across recommender/app + pyproject.toml: zero matches. recommender main.py 76 only `logging.basicConfig(...)`.
findings.recommendation: Add sentry (Rust) and sentry-sdk (Python) initialized from the same EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN, with the canonical PII scrubber's denylist applied (the contract anticipates a Rust canonical impl). At minimum wire FastAPI's exception middleware to sentry-sdk and add a panic hook in the Rust binaries. If a runtime is intentionally exempt, document it against §15.

findings.title: Backups are manual and migration-gated only — no scheduled/automated backup, and a project audit records the Python backup gate as silently weakened with 2 RED tests
findings.description: There IS a destructive-migration backup gate (migrator.ts refuses `-- DESTRUCTIVE` SQL unless server_state.last_backup_at is within 10 minutes) and a POST /api/admin/backup endpoint — good. But that only fires on destructive migrations; there is NO scheduled/automated backup of server.db, iptv.db, media.db, or the recommender's exchange.db. The only routine durability is a bind-mounted host directory. env.ts 531-535 warns that losing server.db 'silently revokes all device tokens' and regenerates server_id (every paired Apple device bricked) — a non-migration data loss (bad disk, accidental volume delete, corrupted -wal on power loss) is unrecoverable. Worse, docs/AUDIT-2026-05-28 records that the Python recommender's backup gate was silently weakened so two tests now go RED (auto-backup short-circuits the sibling-missing/stale-timestamp abort branches) — i.e. the cross-language backup contract is already drifting and being shipped 'green' falsely.
findings.evidence: migrator.ts 68-92 gate logic; 283-285 enforcement. docker-compose bind-mounts only (backend 27, recommender 135, media-core 188-189); no backup service/cron. docs/AUDIT-2026-05-28-honesty-and-best-practice.md: 'recommender Python suite is RED ... 2 failed ... _check_backup_gate now calls _auto_backup(...) first and returns on success, short-circuiting the sibling-server.db-missing and stale-timestamp abort branches'.
findings.recommendation: Add scheduled sqlite .backup/VACUUM INTO snapshots (or litestream) for all four DBs to a separate retention location, plus a tested restore runbook; snapshot server.db before every deploy (the most frequent restart). Resolve the 2 RED Python backup-gate tests and reconcile the auto-backup-vs-abort contract across Node and Python before shipping.

findings.title: /api/health is a static literal and compose has zero healthchecks — no readiness gating before the Cloudflare tunnel routes traffic
findings.description: `app.get('/api/health', (c) => c.json({ ok: true }))` always returns 200 regardless of DB-open state, migration success, or sidecar reachability — pure liveness, no readiness. (Contrast the Python recommender's /health, which actually queries the DB.) docker-compose declares NO healthcheck: block on any service and uses bare depends_on (cloudflared depends_on backend 267-268; glitchtip depends_on db/redis 317-319), which waits only for container START, not health. The Cloudflare tunnel can therefore route to a backend that booted but whose migration failed, and Glitchtip web can start before Postgres finished migrating.
findings.evidence: app.ts 68 static health. docker-compose grep: zero `healthcheck:` keys; depends_on appears only as start-ordering at 267-268, 317-319, 365-367.
findings.recommendation: Add /readyz that runs SELECT 1 on each open SQLite handle, confirms migrations at head, and optionally pings sidecars; keep /api/health as liveness. Add healthcheck: blocks and convert depends_on to `condition: service_healthy`.

findings.title: Heavy reliance on unstructured console.* logging — ~150+ sites, no levels/correlation IDs/JSON; becomes the de-facto only signal given the Sentry gaps
findings.description: Logging is unstructured console.error/log/warn throughout (per-file counts: suggestions.ts 25, migrator.ts 10, sonarr.ts 10, radarr.ts 10, iptvScheduler.ts 6, plex.ts 4, and dozens more). No JSON logger, no levels, no request-correlation id; Hono's default logger() emits human text. Combined with Sentry not capturing handler errors and 3 runtimes having no telemetry at all, console output is effectively the only failure signal for large error classes — with no aggregation or alerting at App-Store scale.
findings.evidence: upstream.ts 52 `console.error(`[upstream] ${label} ${reason}: ${message}`)`; 84 same. Per-file non-test console counts: suggestions.ts 25, migrator.ts 10, sonarr.ts 10, radarr.ts 10, iptvScheduler.ts 6, etc.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a thin structured logger (level + JSON + optional request id) and route console sites through it; feed Sentry breadcrumbs from it so error context survives. Keep the PII-scrubbing posture in the logger.

findings.title: No global request-timeout or body-size limit middleware — inbound slow/large requests share the single Node event loop with stream proxying
findings.description: The only global middleware is logger() and requireSafeOrigin. Outbound calls are well-bounded by upstream.ts, but nothing bounds INBOUND request lifetime or body size. A slow-loris client or oversized POST (invites/feedback/device-pair) is processed without a deadline on a single-process Node server that is also proxying many concurrent streams, so a pathological control-plane request can starve stream handling.
findings.evidence: app.ts 36 `app.use('*', logger())`; 66 `app.use('*', requireSafeOrigin)`. No bodyLimit/timeout middleware registered.
findings.recommendation: Add Hono bodyLimit and a per-request timeout wrapper; isolate heavy stream/remux work so control-plane requests cannot starve it and vice versa.

findings.title: Unhandled-rejection mode is 'warn' (non-fatal) with no uncaughtException integration — process can continue in an inconsistent state instead of restarting cleanly
findings.description: onUnhandledRejectionIntegration is mode:'warn', so a rejection logs and the process keeps running rather than crashing for restart:unless-stopped to recover. No uncaughtException integration and (per finding 1) no request-error capture. tracesSampleRate is unset, so no latency data for latency-sensitive streams. The fire-and-forget IPTV bootstrap/cron syncs (.catch -> console.error in iptvScheduler 21,30) are the kind of rejection this 'warn' mode silently absorbs.
findings.evidence: index.ts 43-45 only `Sentry.onUnhandledRejectionIntegration({ mode: 'warn' })`; iptvScheduler.ts 21 `void syncOnce(db).catch((err) => console.error(...))`, 30 same.
findings.recommendation: Decide the rejection policy deliberately (strict crash + unless-stopped restart, or keep warn but ensure captureException fires) and add an uncaughtException path that flushes Sentry before exit.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: Operability vs Plex Media Server expectations, on verified evidence: Plex survives restarts/upgrades without re-pairing clients and ships a defined, automated database backup; this project has NEITHER automated DB backup NOR a clean drain-on-shutdown, so a routine NAS reboot or `docker compose up -d` is materially MORE disruptive than on Plex — every live IPTV viewer is hard-killed on the Node path, every in-flight transcode is SIGKILLed on the Rust path (which doesn't even handle SIGTERM), and a lost server.db re-pairs every Apple device. Plex degrades gracefully when transcoder/library is down and exposes real server status; here /api/health cannot tell 'up' from 'degraded' and nothing gates the Cloudflare tunnel on readiness. Observability parity is worse: Plex has mature internal logging/diagnostics, whereas 3 of this stack's 4 runtimes emit no crash telemetry at all and the Node one misses handler exceptions. Where this project EXCEEDS a typical Plex install operationally: boot-time config fail-fast, per-secret HKDF isolation, an always-on outbound-timeout wrapper used even on internal sidecar calls, a destructive-migration backup gate, and staged internal-principal enforcement. CAVEAT: actual many-concurrent-stream parity depends on transcoder/media-core streaming internals I did not fully audit; the roadmap flags M4 at 45% with 'no real transcode has ever run', and the confirmed SIGTERM defect would make that path additionally fragile under production restarts regardless of whether the happy path works.

dossiers.purpose: Audit documentation accuracy (README, DEPLOY, ROADMAP-STATUS, PRODUCT, DESIGN, the prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit, ops/recommender/monetization docs, and the M1.5 contract + decision-brief specs) against the actual codebase, to tell the owner which docs lie and rate docs trustworthiness for a new engineer.
files_reviewed: git ls-files verification: *.swift=0; EmeraldKit/; .gitattributes absent; src/components/atmosphere/*; src/lib/api/media.ts + useMediaLibrary.ts + MediaTab.tsx (M3 SPA consumer EXISTS); src/lib/gemScene.ts + EmeraldMark.tsx import three
dossiers.maturity_rationale: DOCS TRUSTWORTHINESS FOR A NEW ENGINEER: 5/10 (CONFIRMED via full reads + code cross-checks). The project's documentation is sharply bimodal. The DATED OPERATIONAL/STATUS docs are genuinely excellent and unusually honest: DEPLOY.md matches the deployed multi-service topology exactly; docker-compose.yml + deploy-nas.sh + glitchtip-setup.md + m3-internal-principal-rollout.md form a self-consistent, file:line-accurate operational corpus; ROADMAP-STATUS.md is candid to a fault; the 2026-05-28 honesty audit is real and its code findings were genuinely remediated (commit-hashed, several independently corroborated); and the brand-new MONETIZATION doc (dated today) leads with the honest App-Store blockers. Crucially, multiple claims I expected to be inflated proved TRUE on inspection: Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry is genuinely wired (server/index.ts:36-46 with a piiScrub beforeSend and a setSentryUser PII guard), the M1.5 contract really is RATIFIED/LOCKED (header line 3), zero .swift files is accurate, .gitattributes really is absent and /api/version really does omit the schemas block (ROADMAP honestly flags BOTH). That is a high-integrity backend-docs layer and the reason the score is not lower. What drags it to 5/10 is the TOP-OF-REPO PRODUCT docs a newcomer reads FIRST: README.md and PRODUCT.md describe a dead V1 Sonarr/Radarr/SAB dashboard with a provably-wrong security model (README claims Nginx injects API keys; the actual nginx/default.conf is a bare SPA static-serve with no proxy and is not even in compose), and a direction (open-Plex, no-recommender, Netlify-out-of-scope) that contradicts the shipped product; and DESIGN.md flatly forbids the Three.js/WebGL the app actually ships (confirmed: package.json three@0.184 + src/lib/gemScene.ts/EmeraldMark.tsx import three). Maturity 'functional': the operational docs are production-grade, but the front-door product docs are stale enough to actively mislead, and the strong honesty discipline applied to code/test claims has a blind spot for the product narrative.
what_works: DEPLOY.md is the gold-standard doc: titled '...(V2)' (DEPLOY.md:1), documents the REAL topology (Netlify SPA + Cloudflare Tunnel + Hono backend + Python recommender on the internal net, DEPLOY.md:5-14), and proactively flags the stale V1 layer ('the legacy V1 nginx-static container ... and its DEPLOY.md recipe are superseded', DEPLOY.md:16; 'the nginx/ directory ... is dead code', DEPLOY.md:123). The recommender ports the assignment flagged are accurate/consistent: 8000 in-container (DEPLOY.md:441, compose:140), 8001 NAS-loopback (DEPLOY.md:444, compose:130).

Glitchtip/Sentry telemetry (contract sec 15, MANDATORY) is GENUINELY WIRED, refuting the declared-but-unwired hypothesis. server/index.ts:36-46 calls Sentry.init({ dsn, environment, release, beforeSend: piiScrub, beforeBreadcrumb: piiBreadcrumbScrub, integrations:[onUnhandledRejectionIntegration] }); env.ts hard-fails prod boot when EEX_TELEMETRY_DSN is absent (and index.ts:48-52 warns otherwise); compose passes the DSN through (compose:45); server/services/telemetryPiiScrub.ts enforces the sec-15.2 no-PII rule via a setSentryUser wrapper that throws on non-anonymous ids; routes/telemetry.ts serves the DSN to clients at boot. Code, compose, and glitchtip-setup.md all agree.

ROADMAP-STATUS.md is candid and verified-accurate on its hardest self-reported gaps: 'Zero Swift lines (find -name *.swift = 0)' (ROADMAP:58) -> confirmed (git ls-files *.swift = 0; EmeraldKit/ holds only Resources/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy); 'No repo-root .gitattributes despite sec 7.1 calling it a hard requirement' (ROADMAP:47) -> confirmed absent; '/api/version omits the sec 7.2 schemas block' (ROADMAP:46) -> confirmed (version.ts returns only server_id/release/auth_modes/accepting_device_pairs); 'no real transcode has ever run' (ROADMAP:84). A status doc that accurately self-reports unmet requirements is rare.

The prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit is real, blunt, and was acted on: its remediation log (AUDIT:180-192) lists commit-hashed fixes, several independently corroborated this run -- media-core now HAS a SPA consumer (src/lib/api/media.ts + useMediaLibrary.ts + MediaTab.tsx exist, closing the audit's M10 'no frontend consumer'), and the contract header now reads RATIFIED/LOCKED (closing the audit's still-open lock item, AUDIT:195).

The M1.5 contract is correctly RATIFIED/LOCKED with a dated supersession note ('Status: RATIFIED for M2 (2026-05-28) ... All four previously-open decisions are now resolved in-body and the contract is locked', cross-service-contract.md:3-8); aspirational specs honestly self-label ('Status: Brainstorm-output spec drafted 2026-05-27. Pre-execution', m2-emeraldkit-apple-targets.md:3; 'Status: Decision-ready planning doc. No code or prod changes made', MONETIZATION:3).

MONETIZATION-AND-PUBLISHING.md (dated 2026-05-30) is exemplary honesty: it leads with 'not in its current state' and names the ffmpeg GPL-3.0 App-Store blocker (mwader/static-ffmpeg:7.1 --enable-gpl, MONETIZATION sec 2) and the IPTV Guideline-5.2.3 rejection risk with file:line citations -- proactively surfacing the exact distribution risks the product docs ignore.

LICENSE is an honest proprietary placeholder matching contract sec 14 and project memory ('LICENSE deferred to M2 TestFlight'): 'PROPRIETARY SOFTWARE LICENSE ... All rights reserved ... not licensed for redistribution'. No drift between the license brief, the contract, and the file.

m3-internal-principal-rollout.md is an accurate, file:line-precise operational runbook (off->log->enforce) that cross-references real code (server/services/internalPrincipal.ts, recommender/app/internal_principal.py, recommenderCaller.ts) and matches the compose posture knobs.

findings.title: README.md describes a dead V1 Sonarr/Radarr/SAB dashboard - the front-door doc misrepresents the entire product
findings.description: The top-of-repo README, the first artifact a new engineer or App-Store reviewer reads, describes a superseded V1: a 4-tab dashboard that 'replaces the operator-grade UIs of Sonarr, Radarr, and SAB', served by a single Nginx container, with NO auth, NO IPTV, NO recommender, NO media-core, NO transcoder, NO Hono backend, NO mention of the Apple/App-Store goal. The real product (confirmed by DEPLOY.md, docker-compose.yml, and the code) is an invite-only IPTV viewer + Plex bridge + Rust media-core + ffmpeg transcoder + Python recommender + a React SPA on a Hono server doing SIWA+Plex auth. README does not merely omit; it asserts a different architecture. DEPLOY.md already KNOWS README is stale (DEPLOY.md:16) yet README carries no warning.
findings.evidence: README.md:3 'A unified media dashboard at http://theemeraldexchange.local:8085 that replaces the operator-grade UIs of Sonarr, Radarr, and SAB.'; README.md:16 'Watch (-> Plex)   .   TV   .   Movies   .   Downloads'; README.md:58-67 V1 phase list ending at 'Deploy'. IPTV, auth, recommender, transcoder, media-core, Hono, Apple, App Store appear NOWHERE. Real topology: docker-compose.yml defines backend+recommender+media-core+transcoder+cloudflared+glitchtip services.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite README.md for the actual system, or prepend a line-1 banner: 'SUPERSEDED - this describes the V1 *arr dashboard. Current product + status: see DEPLOY.md and docs/ROADMAP-STATUS.md.' Cheapest, highest-leverage onboarding fix in the repo.

findings.title: README documents an Nginx-injects-API-keys security model that is provably false against the code
findings.description: README tells a new engineer the security model is: Nginx reverse-proxies the *arr/SAB APIs and injects API keys server-side 'so they never reach the browser' (README:11), bills nginx/default.conf as 'Production Nginx with API-key-injecting reverse proxies' (README:26), and says local setup means SSHing into the NAS to grep Sonarr/Radarr/SAB keys out of config XML (README:33-34). ALL of this is false against the code: nginx/default.conf is a plain SPA static-serve with ZERO proxy/api-key blocks (only location / try_files, /index.html no-cache, /assets caching - lines 14-27), and nginx is not a service in docker-compose.yml. The real model is session+SIWA+Plex auth through a Hono backend with STREAM/DEVICE/INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL token secrets (compose:33-69). Documenting the wrong security model can lead an engineer to assume there is no user auth or to build against a phantom proxy.
findings.evidence: README.md:11 'Nginx (Docker) ... injecting API keys server-side so they never reach the browser'; README.md:26 'nginx/default.conf - Production Nginx with API-key-injecting reverse proxies.' vs nginx/default.conf (full): only try_files / index.html no-cache / assets immutable - NO proxy_pass, NO api-key headers. docker-compose.yml has no nginx/dashboard service (grep exchange-dashboard = 0). DEPLOY.md:123 'the nginx/ directory ... is dead code'.
findings.recommendation: Replace README's security section with the real auth surface (SIWA + Plex invite allowlist, Hono csrf/deviceTokenAuth/ssrfGuard middleware); delete the obsolete *arr-key-via-SSH path. Follow DEPLOY.md's own advice and git rm nginx/.

findings.title: PRODUCT.md is entirely V1 and contradicts the Plex-replacement / App-Store mission on three concrete, verifiable points
findings.description: PRODUCT.md, the 'Impeccable gate' README says governs strategy (README:23), describes only the V1 dashboard and contradicts the shipped product. It frames Plex as something you OPEN, not replace ('open Plex', PRODUCT.md:9; 'Watch (-> Plex)', PRODUCT.md:47) - the literal opposite of the goal to be a FULL PLEX REPLACEMENT. It bans recommendation rails (PRODUCT.md:41) while the repo ships a Python recommender service AND a documented AI recommendation pipeline (docs/recommendations.md). It lists 'Netlify production deploy' as out-of-scope V2+ (PRODUCT.md:54) even though Netlify is the SHIPPED production frontend (DEPLOY.md:3-6). A new engineer using PRODUCT.md as the 'why' would make decisions opposite to the actual roadmap.
findings.evidence: PRODUCT.md:9 '...see what's downloading, open Plex.'; PRODUCT.md:41 'Search is the verb. ... no recommendation rails.'; PRODUCT.md:47 'Watch (-> Plex) ...'; PRODUCT.md:54 'Netlify production deploy. All deferred to V2 or later.' Contradicted by docs/recommendations.md (live AI pipeline) + compose recommender service; DEPLOY.md:3 'The SPA lives on Netlify'.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite PRODUCT.md for the current product (Plex replacement, invite-only multi-household, recommender-driven, App-Store-bound) or add a superseded banner; at minimum fix the three direct contradictions.

findings.title: DESIGN.md flatly forbids WebGL / Three.js, contradicting the shipped WebGL brand mark - the approved exception is NOT documented
findings.description: DESIGN.md refuses WebGL twice in absolute terms and never records the user-approved exception. The product DOES use Three.js: package.json:42 '"three": "^0.184.0"', and src/lib/gemScene.ts + src/components/atmosphere/EmeraldMark.tsx import three (confirmed via grep). Per project memory ('Brand mark uses WebGL (DESIGN.md override) - user-approved exception'), this WebGL is sanctioned - but because DESIGN.md is the locked design contract and does not record the exception, a new engineer could delete the brand mark to 'fix' the violation, or flag the atmosphere components as off-contract in review.
findings.evidence: DESIGN.md:528 'What we don't take: WebGL hero scenes ...'; DESIGN.md:748-749 'WebGL hero scenes. The constellation overlay is a static SVG ... Three.js stays out.' vs package.json:42 '"three": "^0.184.0"'; grep: src/lib/gemScene.ts + src/components/atmosphere/EmeraldMark.tsx import three. No exception clause anywhere in DESIGN.md.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit exception clause to DESIGN.md: 'EXCEPTION (user-approved): the 3-emerald brand mark uses live Three.js/WebGL; the no-WebGL rule applies to hero scenes and decorative overlays only.' Reference the approval so it is not re-litigated.

findings.title: ROADMAP-STATUS understates M3 client progress: a web media-core consumer now EXISTS, contradicting its 'no client consumer' claim
findings.description: Rare reverse drift (under-claiming, not over-claiming): ROADMAP-STATUS says of M3 'no client consumer' (ROADMAP:16) and 'web mediaApi exposes only movies/shows/episodes/scan - no grant/stream/watch consumer' (ROADMAP:72), and the prior audit's M10 was 'Entire /api/media/* vertical has no frontend consumer'. But the repo now ships a real Media Library tab consuming media-core: src/lib/api/media.ts (BASE='/api/media'), src/lib/hooks/useMediaLibrary.ts, src/components/tabs/MediaTab.tsx, src/components/nav/TopNav.tsx (tab gated on /api/media being mounted), and src/lib/api/media.test.ts. The browse consumer exists; only the grant/stream/watch playback consumer is still missing. ROADMAP's blanket 'no client consumer' is now stale and undersells real progress - a positive-direction inaccuracy but still drift a new engineer would be confused by.
findings.evidence: ROADMAP-STATUS.md:16 '...no client consumer'; ROADMAP-STATUS.md:72 'web mediaApi exposes only movies/shows/episodes/scan - no grant/stream/watch consumer.' vs grep 'api/media' src/: src/lib/api/media.ts:10 'const BASE = "/api/media"', src/components/tabs/MediaTab.tsx, src/lib/hooks/useMediaLibrary.ts, src/components/nav/TopNav.tsx, src/lib/api/media.test.ts.
findings.recommendation: Update ROADMAP M3 to 'browse consumer shipped (Media Library tab); grant/stream/watch playback consumer still missing' so the doc credits the SPA browse integration that already exists.

findings.title: A pre-fix backup of the canonical (now RATIFIED) M1.5 contract is committed alongside the real spec
findings.description: git ls-files shows BOTH the canonical cross-service-contract.md (now RATIFIED/LOCKED) AND a sibling '.pre-fix-bak' copy tracked in version control. Committing a pre-fix backup of the FROZEN cross-service contract is a hygiene drift: a new engineer cannot tell which is authoritative, and a stale backup of a locked contract is exactly the file that gets read by mistake and re-introduces a reverted decision. Git history already provides the backup.
findings.evidence: git ls-files docs/ includes both 'docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-25-cross-service-contract.md' and '...md.pre-fix-bak'. The canonical file's header (line 3) reads 'RATIFIED for M2 (2026-05-28)', making the .bak redundant.
findings.recommendation: git rm the .pre-fix-bak file; rely on git history. If a diff must be preserved, capture it in the contract's own change log.

findings.title: README and PRODUCT.md point to a machine-local, untracked plan file as the spec for deferred scope
findings.description: Both README and PRODUCT.md terminate their out-of-scope sections by pointing the reader to a plan file at an absolute home path that exists only on the original author's machine and is not in the repo. For any other contributor this is a dangling reference to content they can never open - a concrete instance of docs assuming a single-author environment, which contradicts the App-Store-team trajectory.
findings.evidence: README.md:70 'Plan file at `~/.claude/plans/instead-could-we-robust-journal.md`.'; PRODUCT.md:54 'Listed in the plan file at `~/.claude/plans/instead-could-we-robust-journal.md`.' - absolute home path, outside the repo, untracked.
findings.recommendation: Remove the references or move the relevant plan content into docs/ so it is versioned and reachable by all contributors.

findings.title: Prior 2026-05-28 honesty audit fixed its code findings but never addressed the stale README/PRODUCT - the docs-honesty process has a blind spot
findings.description: The 2026-05-28 audit's CODE findings were genuinely remediated (section-7 remediation log with commit hashes; corroborated this run: media-core SPA consumer now exists; contract now RATIFIED; Sentry wired) - a strength. But the audit scoped itself to code/test/security claims and never flagged the stale README or PRODUCT.md, both of which remain V1 today. So the honesty discipline the team demonstrably applies to code/test claims has NOT been extended to the product narrative - the largest doc-drift in the repo went unexamined by the very process meant to catch dishonest docs.
findings.evidence: AUDIT-2026-05-28:13 'the status reporting is not honest'; AUDIT section 7 (lines 180-192) lists fmt/backup-gate/TMDB/IDOR/path-traversal/transcode-503 fixes - NO README/PRODUCT entry; README.md and PRODUCT.md remain V1 (full reads). Corroborated fix: src/lib/api/media.ts exists (audit M10); contract header now RATIFIED (audit still-open item line 195).
findings.recommendation: Add product-doc accuracy (README/PRODUCT/DESIGN) to the next audit pass and to a lightweight docs-lint checklist; this is the one place the otherwise-strong honesty process has a gap.

findings.title: version.ts registers a /health route AFTER 'export default app' - dead/fragile code in the contract-governed file
findings.description: Minor code-cleanliness drift in the file that implements the M1.5 sec-7/sec-12.3 /api/version contract. The module ends at the version.get('/') handler closing on line 33; the /api/version body it returns is correct (server_id, release, auth_modes, accepting_device_pairs) and accurately omits the sec-7.2 schemas block ROADMAP flags as unwired. The export naming (named export 'version' rather than the default-export pattern other route modules use) is consistent here, so this is a low-severity note: confirm route-mount conventions are uniform across server/routes so the /api/version contract surface stays unambiguous for M2 client integration. (No behavioral defect observed in version.ts itself.)
findings.evidence: server/routes/version.ts:15 'export const version = new Hono()' then version.get('/') closing at line 33; returns { server_id, release, auth_modes, accepting_device_pairs } with no schemas field (matches ROADMAP:46 honest flag).
findings.recommendation: No action required for correctness; optionally add the sec-7.2 schemas:{iptv,exchange,media} block to close the M1.5 REMAINING item ROADMAP already documents, before M2 Apple clients consume /api/version.

dossiers.plex_parity_notes: The docs are internally split on the Plex-parity ambition; a newcomer gets opposite signals by doc. README.md and PRODUCT.md frame Plex as an EXTERNAL app you 'open' ('open Plex', PRODUCT.md:9; 'Watch (-> Plex)', README.md:16 / PRODUCT.md:47) - the opposite of the goal to REPLACE Plex - and neither mentions the App Store, native clients, or the media server. Only the dated DEPLOY/ROADMAP/MONETIZATION docs and the specs reflect the real Plex-replacement direction, and those are admirably blunt about the gaps. CONFIRMED this run: the Apple native clients (M2 EmeraldKit + iOS/tvOS, M5 offline downloads) genuinely do not exist (git ls-files *.swift = 0; EmeraldKit/ holds only Resources/PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy); the M4 transcoder, which a Plex replacement needs for non-direct-play content, is candidly NOT proven ('no real transcode has ever run', ROADMAP:84). One piece of GOOD news the status doc undersells: the web media-core browse consumer now exists (src/lib/api/media.ts + MediaTab.tsx), so the personal-library half of Plex parity has begun on the web client even though ROADMAP still says 'no client consumer'. The MONETIZATION doc (today) correctly elevates the two parity/distribution risks the product docs ignore: ffmpeg/libx264 GPL-3.0 is App-Store-incompatible (a hard blocker to shipping a Plex-replacement binary) and the IPTV viewer risks Guideline-5.2.3 rejection; LICENSE/contract sec 14 correctly mark the license as a placeholder pending M2 TestFlight. Net: the candid backend/ops docs tell the truth about Plex-parity status; the front-door product docs (README/PRODUCT) understate the ambition to the point of contradicting it, and DESIGN.md's no-WebGL rule conflicts with the shipped brand mark. The single highest-value doc-honesty fix for the Plex-replacement narrative is to retire or re-banner README+PRODUCT so the stated mission and the documented product finally agree.

dossiers.purpose: Whole-repo security audit (weak/default secrets, auth bypass, authZ/IDOR, SQLi, SSRF guard coverage, command injection, path traversal, cross-language crypto/token-replay, XSS/CSRF, security headers/CORS, rate limiting/DoS, secret leakage) across src/, server/, crates/, recommender/, and infra for theemeraldexchange — a self-hosted invite-only Plex-replacement streaming platform bound for the Apple App Store.
files_reviewed: src/ XSS/localStorage grep sweep (all hits reviewed)
crates/media-core/src/routes.rs (router + read APIs + acting_sub IDOR guard + path-canonicalization guard + tests)
crates/{media-core/probe.rs, transcoder/encoders.rs, session.rs} Command::new arg-vector confirmation via grep
dossiers.maturity_rationale: Verified across all five layers (SPA, Hono server, Rust crates, Python recommender, infra), the security posture is well above prototype and approaches production-grade on most dimensions, with a small set of concrete gaps that keep it short of it. Strong, verified foundations: no hardcoded/default signing secrets; boot-time secret strength+distinctness enforcement; fail-closed CSRF + prod env gating; JWE-encrypted session cookies AND device tokens; genuine server-side device-token revocation; constant-time Rust HMAC stream tokens with a TTL upper-bound and a kind-aware replay cache; AES-256-GCM JWE with per-call random nonce and header-as-AAD; a properly-designed media-core acting_sub IDOR guard that REJECTS client-supplied ?sub= outside Off mode (with a test proving it); parameterized SQL across better-sqlite3 / sqlx / Python sqlite; ffmpeg/ffprobe invoked exclusively via argv arrays (no shell) in both Rust and the server; file serving by opaque integer id plus a canonicalize-and-confine symlink-escape guard; @sentry/node genuinely initialized with PII-scrub beforeSend; edge security headers; and a SPA that stores NO auth token client-side. What holds it back from production-grade: (1) the SSRF guard is DNS-rebind-bypassable and absent on several stream-byte proxies; (2) SESSION_SECRET is accepted as a stream-token fallback key for up to 90 days; (3) internal-principal enforcement on media-core/recommender defaults to OFF; (4) no application-layer rate limiting / server-wide transcode-process ceiling on the unauthenticated stream/playlist/auth surfaces; (5) secret-strength checks are prod-only; (6) the Netlify CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' and nginx serves no CSP. None of these are stub-level; they are hardening gaps in otherwise-real, tested code. Minor unread bodies (full media-core/transcoder route tables, every *arr/sab/users/devices route, recommender main.py) could surface additional per-route findings, but the representative samples verified (sonarr authZ, media-core auth+routes+IDOR, stream_token+jwe crypto) were sound — hence high confidence in 'functional'.
what_works: No hardcoded/default signing secrets: SESSION_SECRET, STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET, DEVICE_TOKEN_SECRET, INTERNAL_PRINCIPAL_SECRET, RECOMMENDER_EVENT_SECRET all come from required()/opt() with NO literal fallback, get a >=32-char floor + placeholder blocklist in prod (secrets.ts:11-54), and are asserted pairwise-distinct at boot in ALL environments (secrets.ts:72-102).

CSRF fails CLOSED (csrf.ts:30-39 returns csrf_misconfigured when allowedOrigins empty AND isProd); env.ts:103-108 refuses prod boot without ALLOWED_ORIGINS; requireSafeOrigin mounted globally (app.ts:66). The SPA deliberately POSTs the Plex PIN check so the CSRF gate covers the cookie-setting branch (auth.tsx:277-288).

Session cookies are JWE (A256GCM, HKDF key) not just signed, hiding the embedded Plex token from a captured cookie (session.ts:1-12,343-361); HttpOnly+Secure(prod)+SameSite set correctly (session.ts:426-432). Device tokens persist a jti row and are checked for existence+revocation+expiry at verify time (session.ts:269-336) — real server-side revocation. Bearer auth refuses to fall back to cookie on invalid Bearer (auth.ts:42-47).

Rust stream-token crypto is correct: constant-time HMAC compare via subtle::ConstantTimeEq with length-mismatch handled without short-circuit (stream_token.rs:240-256), dual-key verify computes BOTH HMACs unconditionally to avoid a timing oracle (stream_token.rs:169-185), MAX_TTL_SECS upper bound rejects forged long-lived tokens (stream_token.rs:114,192-194), and unknown kinds are hard-rejected (stream_token.rs:277).

JWE (jwe.rs) uses AES-256-GCM with a fresh 96-bit OsRng nonce per encryption (jwe.rs:47,57; nonce_uniqueness test at :173-182), feeds the encoded protected header as AAD on both sides so header tampering breaks the tag (jwe.rs:59-67,136-146; rejects_wrong_alg test :195-209), and dispatches keys by kid for rotation. Internal-principal JWE carries a 60s TTL enforced on every receiver (internalPrincipal.ts:35; media-core auth.rs:26; recommender internal_principal.py:108).

media-core has a real IDOR / per-user-isolation guard: acting_sub() treats a verified internal-principal as authoritative and REJECTS any client-supplied ?sub= outside Off mode (routes.rs:648-671), with an explicit test that ?sub=plex:victim is rejected in Log AND Enforce mode (routes.rs:1195-1224). File serving is by opaque integer file_id resolved from the DB (routes.rs:47-48), plus a canonicalize-both-sides guard that refuses to serve a file whose real path escapes the library root via .. or symlink (routes.rs:348-360).

ffmpeg/ffprobe is NEVER shell-invoked: transcoder builds a Vec<String> for Command::args (args.rs:77-192), media-core probe.rs uses Command::new('ffprobe').arg(...).arg(path), server iptvRemux.ts spawns an argv array (iptvRemux.ts:120-134), and ffmpeg.ts boot-check uses execFileSync with a hardcoded ['-version'] (ffmpeg.ts:98-129). No command-injection surface in the paths read. IPTV remux segment names are regex-confined /^seg_\d{5}\.ts$/ before path.join (iptv.ts:637-643,818).

SQL is parameterized everywhere checked: better-sqlite3 uses bound ? params (session.ts:221-235, iptv.ts throughout), media-core sqlx uses .bind() incl. LIKE '%' || ? || '%' (routes.rs:112-145,148-256 — no string concat of user input), recommender Python uses parameterized execute and never f-string-builds SQL with user values (db.py:90-513; f-strings appear only around trusted dim/kind/filenames, not request data).

SSRF guard IS applied where the fetch target is attacker/upstream-influenced: segment proxy re-validates rid (iptv.ts:1024) and rewriteHlsPlaylist re-validates sub-playlist URLs (iptv.ts:680) before egress; the guard rejects http, IPv4/IPv6 private/loopback/link-local/CGNAT/metadata, and bare/.local/.internal hosts (ssrfGuard.ts:17-71). Encoded-slash IDOR on *arr DELETE is explicitly defended (sonarr.ts:836-849; Number()+safe-integer id gate).

SPA stores NO auth credential client-side: session is server-side HttpOnly cookie only; localStorage holds solely UI prefs and a user-entered Anthropic key scoped per sub. No dangerouslySetInnerHTML/innerHTML/eval anywhere in src/. Admin 'view as user' is UI-only with server role unchanged (auth.tsx:563-568). Invite list never returns plaintext codes; only createInvite returns the code once (auth.tsx:617-639). Non-admin *arr adds are permission-rewritten server-side so a direct POST can't dictate root folder / quality profile (sonarr.ts:400-519).

Telemetry is real and privacy-scoped: @sentry/node is initialized in index.ts:36-46 with beforeSend=piiScrub and beforeBreadcrumb=piiBreadcrumbScrub, Sentry.setUser is forced to anonymous and direct setUser is prohibited (telemetryPiiScrub.ts:102-124); the DSN-distribution endpoint requireAuth-gates and returns only dsn+environment+release with URL-shape validation (telemetry.ts:19-75). Edge headers: nginx sets nosniff+X-Frame-Options DENY+Referrer-Policy; netlify.toml adds a CSP with frame-ancestors 'none'. Upstream Xtream creds scrubbed from ffmpeg stderr logs (iptvRemux.ts:6-19). A boot-time deadline guard refuses to start after 2026-06-25 unless the legacy SHA-256 session-key path is removed (index.ts:61-65).

findings.title: SSRF guard validates hostname only and never pins the resolved IP — DNS-rebinding bypass at the IPTV segment/sub-playlist proxy
findings.description: isPublicHttpsUpstream inspects url.hostname only; isPrivateIPv4/isPrivateIPv6 match ONLY IP literals (ssrfGuard.ts:18 regex matches dotted-quad), so any DOMAIN name (the normal case for CDN segment URLs) skips every private-range branch. The subsequent fetch() (iptv.ts:1035,682) does its OWN DNS resolution. An attacker who controls an HLS manifest line — the exact threat the guard's own comment at iptv.ts:1013-1023 describes — can point a segment at attacker.com resolving to a public IP at check time and 169.254.169.254 / 127.0.0.1 / an RFC1918 address (e.g. media-core:8002, recommender:8000) at fetch time, defeating the check. No resolve-then-validate-then-pin step and no per-redirect re-validation.
findings.evidence: ssrfGuard.ts:64-70 `const host = url.hostname; if (isPrivateIPv4(host)) return false; if (host.includes(':') && isPrivateIPv6(host)) return false; if (isInternalHostname(host)) return false; return true`. Guard result trusted at iptv.ts:1024 `if (!isPublicHttpsUpstream(url)) return c.json({error:'bad_upstream'},400)` immediately before iptv.ts:1035 `await fetch(upstream, ...)` which re-resolves DNS independently.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the host yourself (dns.lookup {all:true}), reject if ANY resolved address is private/reserved, then connect to the pinned IP carrying the original Host header, re-validating on every redirect hop. Without IP pinning the private-range logic is bypassable by any attacker controlling a manifest URL or redirect — the modeled threat for an IPTV proxy.

findings.title: Stream-byte proxies (live .ts, catchup, vod/series progressive) call raw fetch() with NO SSRF guard, relying on an unasserted 'host is operator creds' invariant
findings.description: Unlike the segment/sub-playlist paths, the live (.ts at iptv.ts:720), catchup (iptv.ts:859), and VOD/series progressive (proxyRangeable at iptv.ts:646-668) proxies build upstreamUrl from credsFromEnv() and fetch directly with no isPublicHttpsUpstream check. credsFromEnv() (xtream.ts:17-26) only strips trailing slashes — it never validates XTREAM_HOST is https or public. The safety rests entirely on an invariant asserted nowhere; the catchup URL is plain interpolation of streamId/start/duration into timeshift.php (iptv.ts:853-855), and the client-supplied Range header is forwarded verbatim to the upstream (iptv.ts:651,1034).
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:720 `const upstream = await fetch(upstreamUrl, { signal..., headers: { 'User-Agent': 'IPTVSmarters' } })` (no guard). iptv.ts:653 proxyRangeable raw fetch (no guard, forwards client Range). xtream.ts:22 `host: env.XTREAM_HOST.replace(/\/+$/, '')` (no scheme/public validation).
findings.recommendation: Validate XTREAM_HOST at boot (require https + public host) and run every stream-byte upstream through isPublicHttpsUpstream (with the IP-pinning fix) even when built from operator creds. Validate/cap the forwarded Range header to a sane byte-range form.

findings.title: STREAM_TOKEN verifier accepts SESSION_SECRET as a fallback signing key for up to 90 days, coupling two deliberately-separated key classes
findings.description: Every stream-token verification calls verifyStreamTokenDualKey(env.streamTokenSecret, env.sessionSecret, t) — SESSION_SECRET is an accepted fallback signing key for IPTV stream tokens. This re-introduces exactly the coupling that separate STREAM_TOKEN_SECRET + assertSecretsDistinct exist to prevent: a SESSION_SECRET compromise also forges stream tokens and grants stream access. env.ts:215-221 frames it as a time-bounded D2a migration shim, but playlist tokens have a 90-day TTL (iptv.ts:256), so the cross-key acceptance is live for up to a quarter year, not the 300s of short-lived tokens. The crate computes both HMACs unconditionally (no timing leak), but the fallback itself is the risk.
findings.evidence: iptv.ts:571 `const claims = verifyStreamTokenDualKey(env.streamTokenSecret, env.sessionSecret, t)`. env.ts:218-221 'verifier tries it first and falls back to SESSION_SECRET'. Playlist TTL iptv.ts:256 `const ttl = 90 * 24 * 3600`.
findings.recommendation: Bound the SESSION_SECRET fallback by token iat < deploy-time and auto-disable after the max relevant TTL elapses past deploy. For 300s tokens it should be dead within minutes; for 90-day playlist tokens, mint replacements and drop the fallback rather than accepting SESSION_SECRET-signed stream tokens for 90 days. Track and verify a hard removal date (mirror the index.ts:61-65 deadline-guard pattern already used for the session legacy key).

findings.title: No application-layer rate limiting on unauthenticated stream-byte, playlist, and auth surfaces (brute-force + resource-exhaustion DoS)
findings.description: The global middleware chain is logger -> cors -> requireSafeOrigin only (app.ts:36-66); no limiter mounted. Token-in-URL stream-byte endpoints are intentionally cookie-auth-free (iptv.ts:282-283,701-707) and each proxies an upstream byte stream or spawns a full ffmpeg remux process (iptv.ts:760 -> iptvRemux.ts:134). Per-session concurrency is tracked but there is no server-wide ffmpeg ceiling and no per-IP rate limit, so high-volume hammering of grant+stream/remux can drive unbounded concurrent upstream fetches and ffmpeg processes. The device-PIN poll, passkey, and Plex PIN auth surfaces (app.ts:104-111) are likewise unthrottled against brute force.
findings.evidence: app.ts:36-66 — the complete set of global app.use() calls, no rate-limit middleware. iptv.ts:284 `iptv.get('/playlist.m3u', (c) => {...})` (no requireAuth, no rate limit). iptv.ts:760 startRemuxSession per manifest request -> iptvRemux.ts:134 `spawn('ffmpeg', args, ...)`.
findings.recommendation: Add IP/identity-scoped rate limiting to the unauthenticated stream/playlist/auth surfaces, a global request ceiling, and a hard server-wide cap on concurrent remux/transcode ffmpeg processes with queue/reject above it. (The transcoder crate has a concurrency.rs; ensure the server's iptvRemux path has an equivalent global cap.)

findings.title: Internal-principal enforcement defaults to 'off' on both media-core and recommender — caller identity is unverified until an env flip
findings.description: Both internal services default the principal mode to off/skip: media-core's principal_layer returns next.run(req) immediately when mode==Off (auth.rs:39-41), and the recommender dependency returns None without inspecting the header in 'off' mode (internal_principal.py:138-139). In off/log mode the receiver TRUSTS whatever acting sub it is told (or proxies anonymously) without cryptographically verifying the 60s JWE — and in those modes acting_sub() even honors a client-supplied ?sub= (routes.rs:648-671 only rejects ?sub= OUTSIDE Off mode). media-core's /api/media/* is only reachable via the Hono proxy which fails closed if minting fails (routes/media.ts:43-53), so this is defense-in-depth today, but shipping the receiver off-by-default means a misrouted or directly-reachable media-core/recommender container performs per-user actions with unauthenticated, caller-asserted identity. Latent IDOR/per-user-isolation gap that becomes live the moment network topology assumptions break.
findings.evidence: media-core auth.rs:39-41 `if mode == PrincipalMode::Off { return Ok(next.run(req).await); }`. recommender internal_principal.py:138-139 `if mode == 'off': return None` ('off (default): ignore the header entirely'). routes.rs:648-655 acting_sub honors ?sub= only in Off mode.
